args: A 32-year-old Vietnamese-American flight attendant booked a dawn appointment in the airport-adjacent internal medicine clinic after the airline’s annual wellness checkpoint issued an automated notice stating “fasting sugar slightly above goal—suggest follow-up with PCP.” She purposely delayed any dietary or exercise changes until after today’s venipuncture so that baseline conditions would be documented without confounding. Her work schedule consists of east-bound red-eyes that invert circadian eating patterns; she often relies on complimentary cola and packaged biscotti to stay alert during beverage service, then naps in crew quarters and awakens with a hollow, light-headed sensation that resolves once she consumes a carbohydrate-dense snack from the galley cart. Since graduating from flight school six years ago her weight has drifted from 53 kg to 60 kg at an unaltered height of 163 cm, yielding a body-mass index of approximately 22.6 kg/m²—enough to notice that the uniform skirt now fits snugly but not enough to require a larger size. She logs approximately 8,000 steps on layover days spent sightseeing, yet on consecutive flight legs she may sit belted for hours and skips formal exercise because hotel gyms often close before crews arrive. She denies waking to void more than once nightly, has not observed polydipsia beyond typical cabin dehydration, and has experienced no unexplained weight loss, blurry vision, furunculosis, or distal paresthesias. Episodes of post-prandial fatigue occur if the in-flight meal service is delayed, but they resolve quickly after a sweetened latte. Her medical history is limited to mild intermittent asthma treated with an albuterol inhaler once or twice per month during aircraft disinsection procedures; she has never received systemic corticosteroids, antiretroviral medication, atypical antipsychotics, or hormonal contraception. Surgical history is negative. She takes 1,000 IU of vitamin D3 daily, occasional melatonin on trans-Pacific routes, and no herbal or prescription agents aimed at glycemic control. Menstrual cycles are regular without hirsutism or acne. A maternal aunt was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes at 57 and now uses basal insulin; her parents are living without metabolic disease. She has never used tobacco, limits alcohol to a single glass of sauvignon blanc at layover dinners, and denies recreational drug use. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pressure, exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, mood disturbance, heat or cold intolerance, or changes in shoe size. Upon arrival at 06:30 she appeared well hydrated, in uniform, and eager to finish before a scheduled Mumbai departure. After eight minutes of quiet sitting, vital signs were blood pressure 114/74 mm Hg in the right brachial artery, heart rate 68 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14/min, oral temperature 36.5 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Neck circumference measured 33 cm without thyromegaly or acanthosis; skin tags were absent. Cardiovascular examination revealed a brisk carotid upstroke and a crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; peripheral pulses were 2+ symmetrically. Lungs were clear to auscultation with prolonged expiratory phase absent. Abdomen was flat, soft, and nontender; liver and spleen were not palpable. No pedal edema was noted. Neurologic screen demonstrated intact cranial nerves, symmetric 2+ reflexes, preserved 10-g monofilament sensation on plantar surfaces, and normal vibration perception at the great toes. Funduscopic inspection revealed sharp disc margins with no microaneurysms. She confirmed ingesting only water after 21:00 the previous evening, having declined crew-lounge coffee. Venipuncture occurred at 06:44; samples were transported on wet ice and reached the core laboratory within 15 minutes. The chemistry analyzer employing hexokinase methodology returned a fasting plasma glucose of 90 mg/dL, and an electrochemiluminescent immunoassay on the same specimen showed a fasting serum insulin concentration of 5 µIU/mL. Additional studies included hemoglobin A1c 5.1 %, serum creatinine 0.72 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate >90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 104 mEq/L, bicarbonate 26 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 11 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 22 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 24 U/L, total cholesterol 184 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein 112 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein 55 mg/dL, and triglycerides 129 mg/dL. Thyroid-stimulating hormone measured 1.7 µIU/mL, morning cortisol 13 µg/dL, and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 1.9 mg/L. Urinalysis demonstrated specific gravity 1.011, negative protein, negative glucose, and absence of ketones; urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio was 7 mg/g. A resting 12-lead electrocardiogram documented normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats/min with no conduction delay or ischemic change. She reiterated that she has not begun calorie restriction, intermittent fasting beyond crew schedules, high-intensity interval training, or pharmacologic agents since the wellness notice, and she seeks evidence-based counsel on maintaining glycemic stability during irregular trans-meridian duty cycles, airport-friendly meal planning, and whether further testing such as an oral glucose tolerance study is warranted before her next licensing physical.

fasting_glucose: Fasting glucose in mg/dL (e.g., 100)
fasting_insulin: Fasting insulin in uU/mL (e.g., 10)

args:
A 74-year-old, avid female gardener and active smoker with a past medical history notable for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and hypertension presented to the hospital with one week of progressively worsening New York Heart Association (NYHA) class III dyspnea and fatigue. She endorsed associated cough productive of yellowish mucoid sputum. She denied chest pain/discomfort, palpitations, pre-syncope, syncope, orthopnea, or paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND). There was no history of fever, arthralgia, myalgia, or rashes.
In the emergency department, the patient was afebrile, blood pressure was 85/49 mm Hg, heart rate was 150 per minute, and respiratory rate was 22 per minute, with oxygen saturation of 94% on six liters of oxygen via a nasal cannula. The patient appeared lethargic and was using accessory muscles for respiration. General examination showed pallor. The oral mucosa was dry, with a thickly coated tongue. The neck veins were flat. Heart examination revealed a fast, irregular heart rate, variable first heart sound, and normal second heart sound without any murmurs or gallops. Lung examination revealed bilateral mid to late inspiratory crackles. The abdomen was soft, distended, non-tender, with normal bowel sounds. Extremities were noted to be cold, with 1+ pitting edema and normal capillary refill time.
Routine laboratory investigations revealed a white blood cell (WBC) count of 7.5 (4.0-10.5 k/uL), hemoglobin of 9.9 (12.5-16 g/dL), and hematocrit of 32.3 (37-47%). Her baseline hemoglobin concentration was around 15 g/dL. Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) was 101.9 (78-100 fL) with elevated mean cell hemoglobin (MCH) of 34 (25-33 pg) and normal mean cell hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) of 33.4 (32-36 g/dL). Platelet count was 34 (150-450 K/uL), with elevated mean platelet volume (MPV) of 12.4 (7.4-11.4 fL). Peripheral blood smear showed intracytoplasmic parasites suspicious for Babesia along with reduced platelets (Figure ). The parasitic level was found to be at 9.04%. Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) was 1544 U/L (125-220 U/L), haptoglobin was <6 mg/dL (27-139 mg/dL), total bilirubin was 5.4 mg/dL (0.3-1.0 mg/dL), with a direct fraction of 3.5 mg/dL (0.0-0.2 mg/dL). Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) was 202 U/L (5-40 U/L), alanine aminotransferase (ALT) was 90 U/L (7-52 U/L), with albumin of 2.3 g/dL (3.5-5.0 g/dL). Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) was 51 mg/dL (7-17 mg/dL) with a normal creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL, sodium 129 (135-145 mmol/L), potassium 4 (3.5-5.1 mmol/L), chloride 103 (98-107 mmol/L), serum bicarbonate 19 (24-32 mmol/L), and calcium 7 (8.4-102 mg/dL).
The chest X-ray was remarkable for cardiomegaly with a small right pleural effusion and small airspace opacity within the right lower lobe concerning for consolidation or segmental atelectasis. Given her presentation, she was started on intravenous fluids along with empiric antibiotic coverage for community-acquired pneumonia with ceftriaxone and azithromycin. As the peripheral smear was remarkable for Babesia, she was started on atovaquone and doxycycline for a possible co-infection with Lyme. Blood titers for Anaplasma and Lyme were sent. Positive pressure ventilation was started to help with respiratory distress. She received intravenous fluids; however, she continued to remain hypotensive and required norepinephrine for hemodynamic support. Given the severity of her disease and parasitic load, a decision was made to do erythrocytapheresis.
Following erythrocytapheresis, the parasitic load decreased to 5.54%. The patient remained in shock and clindamycin was added to the regimen. Her renal function worsened, which was believed to be secondary to pigment-induced nephropathy from hemolysis. Due to the inadequate response and development of acute kidney injury, repeat erythrocytapheresis was performed, resulting in a reduction in parasite load to 1.75%. At this point, Lyme immunoglobulin M (IgM) immunoblot was found to be positive, with negative immunoglobulin G (IgG). Anaplasma titers also showed recent/current infection, with IgG >1:1024 (reference <1:64) and IgM 1:80 (reference <1:20). Atovaquone and azithromycin were discontinued and quinine was added. Her hemodynamic status improved and she was titrated off of vasopressor support along with an improvement in respiratory status. Her complete blood count and renal and hepatic function normalized after 14 days of antimicrobial therapy.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 24)

args: A 58-year-old woman with a fifteen-year history of chronic hepatitis B infection complicated by compensated cirrhosis was referred to the liver clinic for her semi-annual surveillance visit after recent primary care laboratory work disclosed a slight rise in her coagulation profile. She denied abdominal bloating, ankle swelling, early satiety, breathlessness when lying flat, gastrointestinal bleeding, changes in bowel habits, confusion, daytime sleepiness, tremor, or difficulty performing her job as an accountant. Her past medical history included well-controlled hypertension managed with amlodipine 5 mg daily, class II obesity treated through a structured nutrition program, and osteopenia for which she took weekly alendronate; she had never consumed alcohol, had no history of recreational drug use, and completed a course of tenofovir five years earlier that rendered her viral load undetectable, with maintenance entecavir 0.5 mg daily ever since. Family history was significant for a mother with breast cancer and a father with type 2 diabetes; no relatives were known to have liver disease. Review of systems was otherwise unremarkable. Immunizations were current, including hepatitis A vaccination, and she recently underwent screening colonoscopy that was normal. Vital signs at presentation revealed temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 128/68 mm Hg, heart rate 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 13 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99 % on ambient air. She appeared well nourished, alert, and conversant, volunteered a detailed medication list without hesitation, and easily performed serial sevens and recited the current news headlines, suggesting intact higher cortical function. Conjunctivae were clear without scleral yellowing, parotid glands were normal in size, and the oral cavity was moist with no fetor. There were a few spider angiomas over the upper sternum, but no palmar erythema, digital clubbing, or muscle wasting. Cardiopulmonary examination demonstrated a regular rhythm without murmurs and clear breath sounds bilaterally. Her abdomen was flat and soft with a well-healed Pfannenstiel scar from a remote hysterectomy; percussion produced resonant tones across the flanks with no shift when she rolled laterally, and an elicited fluid wave was absent even when counterpressure was applied. The liver edge was palpable 1 cm below the right costal margin, firm yet smooth, non-tender, and the spleen was not felt. There were no abdominal wall collaterals, no umbilical protrusion, and no pedal edema. Neurologic examination showed symmetric strength, intact sensation, rapid alternating movements without hesitation, and a steady narrow-based gait. Laboratory studies drawn that morning prior to any therapy demonstrated total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, serum albumin 4.5 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 44 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 48 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 151 U/L, sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, creatinine 0.78 mg/dL, glucose 112 mg/dL, hemoglobin 13.3 g/dL, white blood cell count 5.9 ×10⁹/L, platelet count 142 ×10⁹/L, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.45. Alfa-fetoprotein measured concurrently was 5 ng/mL. Hepatitis B surface antigen remained positive whereas e-antigen was negative, and viral DNA by PCR was below the lower limit of quantification. A focused point-of-care ultrasound obtained during the visit displayed a nodular hepatic contour with coarse echotexture, patent portal and hepatic veins with hepatopetal flow, no intrahepatic mass, spleen length of 13 cm, and no detectable free intraperitoneal fluid. Electrocardiogram recorded normal sinus rhythm at 76 beats/min without QT prolongation, and a screening chest radiograph revealed clear lung fields. Having reviewed the data, the hepatology team emphasized continued antiviral adherence, avoidance of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, maintenance of dietary sodium restriction to less than 2 g daily, and routine imaging every six months for hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance.
CRITICAL HINT: For Child-Pugh, ascites_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Slight/Mild, 3=Moderate/Severe. encephalopathy_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Grade 1-2, 3=Grade 3-4.

output.reasoning: The patient is a 58-year-old woman. Child-Pugh Score criteria include Bilirubin, Albumin, INR, Ascites, and Encephalopathy. 1. Bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (<2.0 = 1 pt). 2. Albumin: 4.5 g/dL (>3.5 = 1 pt). 3. INR: 1.45 (1.7-2.3 = 2 pts). 4. Ascites: Patient denied abdominal bloating; examination showed abdomen flat and soft, percussion resonant, no fluid wave, ultrasound showed no detectable free intraperitoneal fluid. Therefore, Ascites is Absent (Grade 1 = 1 pt). 5. Encephalopathy: Patient denied confusion/daytime sleepiness; exam alert/conversant, performed serial sevens, recited news, steady gait. Therefore, Encephalopathy is Absent (Grade 1 = 1 pt). Total score components mapped.

args: A 97-year-old man who has lived independently in a one-level bungalow and still conducts his own grocery shopping was found early this morning by his visiting niece sitting at the kitchen table complaining of a “heavy chest” after a night of intermittent cough productive of thin, yellow-tinged sputum; she reported that he manages chronic osteoarthritis with over-the-counter acetaminophen only, has never been prescribed corticosteroids, biologic agents, or chemotherapy, and has no history of organ transplantation, cirrhosis, advanced pulmonary disease, or dialysis-requiring kidney injury. Emergency medical technicians arrived within twelve minutes, noted the patient to be upright, conversant, and oriented, with fingertip saturation readings fluctuating between 86 % and 88 % on ambient air; they applied a Venturi collar delivering precisely 30 % inspired oxygen verified by an inline analyzer and placed a single 20-gauge peripheral intravenous catheter that remained saline-locked. During the ten-minute transport he received no intravenous fluids, bronchodilators, antibiotics, vasopressors, diuretics, analgesics, or antipyretics. On wheel-in to the medical intensive-care receiving bay—and before any hospital interventions or change in oxygen delivery—first set of vital signs captured on two independent monitors showed an oral temperature of 98.8 °F, a regular pulse of 86 beats per minute, a respiratory rate counted over a full minute at 17 breaths per minute without accessory muscle recruitment, and a non-invasive blood pressure reproducibly cycling at 135⁄76 mm Hg on both arms. The patient opened his eyes spontaneously, answered every orientation question correctly, recalled breakfast contents, and followed complex three-step commands, establishing a Glasgow Coma Score of 15 in the absence of sedatives, alcohol, or head trauma. Skin examination revealed warm, pink extremities with capillary refill under two seconds, no peripheral edema, and no rash or petechiae. Neck veins were flat at 30°, carotid upstrokes were brisk, and radial plus dorsalis pedis pulses were 2+ bilaterally. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a crisp S1, physiologic splitting of S2, and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Breath sounds were diffusely diminished at the right base with scattered late-inspiratory crackles; percussion there was mildly dull and tactile fremitus was increased, while the left lung field was clear. The abdomen was flat, moved with respiration, and was nontender with active bowel sounds; there was no costovertebral angle tenderness. Extremities exhibited full range of motion with symmetric strength, and a brief cerebellar screen was unremarkable. Seven minutes after bay placement—while the same 30 % Venturi source remained untouched—a right radial arterial specimen was analyzed, revealing a pH of 7.40, PaCO₂ 38 mm Hg, and a PaO₂ of 58 mm Hg, confirming moderate hypoxemia despite supplemental oxygen; end-tidal capnography displayed 37 mm Hg, corroborating the arterial CO₂. Simultaneous point-of-care metabolic panel reported sodium 141 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, glucose 112 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.3 mg/dL—values comparable to outpatient labs drawn at his annual wellness visit three months prior, indicating preserved renal function without acute insult. A complete blood count returned a white-cell concentration of 11 300 cells/mm³ with 78 % segmented neutrophils, hematocrit 39 %, hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, and platelets 224 000/µL. Venous lactate measured 1.6 mmol/L. Portable anteroposterior chest radiography performed before any antimicrobial, steroid, or diuretic therapy depicted a right lower-lobe infiltrate with subtle air bronchograms, clear costophrenic angles, and a normal cardiac silhouette. Point-of-care lung ultrasound corroborated focal consolidation in the posterior right base with dynamic air bronchograms and preserved lung sliding elsewhere. Focused transthoracic echocardiography revealed a small, vigorously contracting left ventricle with no regional wall-motion abnormalities and an inferior vena cava diameter of 1.5 cm that collapsed more than 50 % with inspiration, suggesting adequate preload and no overt right-heart strain. Twelve-lead electrocardiography printed during the arterial draw showed normal sinus rhythm at 88 beats per minute, PR interval 160 ms, QRS 90 ms, QTc 420 ms, and no ischemic ST-T changes. A bladder scan registered 120 mL, and the patient reported voiding normally overnight. Importantly, from the moment the niece dialed emergency services through handoff to the intensive-care fellow—a span of roughly thirty minutes—he had not received crystalloid boluses, inhaled bronchodilators, systemic antibiotics, corticosteroids, vasopressors, narcotic analgesics, or antipyretics; the sole intervention remained the Venturi collar delivering 30 % oxygen. Consequently, the contemporaneous measurements—temperature 98.8 °F, systolic pressure 135 mm Hg, diastolic pressure 76 mm Hg, heart rate 86 beats per minute, respiratory rate 17 per minute, arterial pH 7.40, serum sodium 141 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, hematocrit 39 %, white blood cell count 11 300 /mm³, fraction of inspired oxygen 30 %, and arterial oxygen tension 58 mm Hg—represent an untreated physiologic snapshot at presentation for suspected community-acquired pneumonia complicated by early hypoxemic respiratory compromise in a very elderly yet cognitively intact and immunocompetent individual with preserved baseline organ function.

CRITICAL HINT: For APACHE II oxygenation, use PaO2 to score if FiO2 < 0.50. Only use A-a gradient if FiO2 >= 0.50. Do not hallucinate A-a gradient if PaCO2 is missing. GCS score points = 15 minus actual GCS.

output.reasoning: The patient is 97 years old (Age > 65: +6 points in APACHE II). Vital signs recorded at presentation: Temperature 98.8 F (37.1 C; 0 pts), MAP calculated as [(2*76)+135]/3 = 95.6 mmHg (0 pts), Heart Rate 86 bpm (0 pts), Respiratory Rate 17/min (0 pts). Oxygenation: Patient on FiO2 0.30, PaO2 is 58 mmHg (PaO2 < 60 mmHg: +4 points). Arterial pH is 7.40 (0 pts). Serum Sodium 141 mmol/L (0 pts), Potassium 4.1 mmol/L (0 pts), Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL (0 pts), Hematocrit 39 % (0 pts), WBC count 11,300/mm3 (0 pts). GCS is 15 (15-15 = 0 pts). No evidence of severe organ insufficiency or immunocompromise stated.

args:
A 34-year-old Hispanic female was admitted with the chief complaint of a painful nonhealing abdominal wound for 2 weeks duration. The patient initially developed few scabs in the infra umbilical area which led to frequent itching and irritation. She used plain cotton gauze with vaseline ointment to treat them by herself. The scabs later turned into ulcers. Her past medical history was significant for ESRD, and she has been on continuous ambulatory peritoneal dialysis (CAPD) for 5 years, gastroesophageal reflux disease, obesity, anemia of chronic kidney disease (CKD), secondary hyperparathyroidism of renal origin, and coccidioidomycosis infection. Her chronic renal disease was from amphotericin B drug toxicity secondary to previous disseminated coccidioidomycosis treatment. The patient had a history of uncontrolled hyperparathyroidism with an intact parathyroid hormone level as high as 2400 picograms/milliliter (pg/mL) and an elevated phosphorus level of 10 mg/dL as an outpatient. These were values 6 months before the current hospitalization. This was difficult to control mainly because of financial constraints in getting the medications and nonadherence to prescribed phosphate binders. Home medications include sevelamer carbonate 3200 milligrams (mg) 3 times a day with meals. The patient lived at her parent’s home and has stopped working because of multiple medical issues. The patient denied any history of smoking, alcohol, or illicit drug use. Her peritoneal dialysis prescription was 4 daytime exchanges of 2-L peritoneal dialysate fluid, each bag composed of 2.5% dextrose and each exchange for 4 hours daily. Her weekly Kt/V was 2.1, with no residual kidney function.
Vital signs on presentation were, temperature of 98.6 Fahrenheit (F), pulse rate of 84 beats per minute (bpm), respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute, blood pressure 117/59 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Physical examination showed a morbidly obese female in mild distress due to abdominal pain. Abdominal examination revealed multiple tender indurated ulcers with surrounding erythema and black eschar in the lower abdomen section ( and ) along with skin ulceration with exposed subcutaneous tissue and granulation in lateral walls ( and ). The rest of the physical examination was non-significant.
Laboratory data revealed elevated white blood count (WBC) 21,000/mm3, hemoglobin 8.5 g/dL, platelets 526,000/mm3, sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 3.4 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 59 mg/dL, creatinine 13.26 mg/dL, albumin 3.3 g/dL, calcium 8.6 mg/dL, phosphorus 8.3 mg/dL, parathyroid hormone 712 pg./mL, C reactive protein 129.8 mg/L, CAPD fluid was not positive for an infection, and blood cultures were negative for any infection. Computerized tomography (CT) abdomen and pelvis with and without contrast revealed anterior abdominal wall subcutaneous calcification. Parathyroid imaging by 29.2 mCi Technetium 99m Sestamibi scan revealed no abnormal radiotracer accumulation to suggest parathyroid adenoma.
To the patient was initially treated with empiric antibiotics until the infection was ruled out and was also started on sodium thiosulphate 25 g in sterile water intravenous infusion over 30 minutes, 3 times a week for 3 months. Due to unavailability of dermatologist and skilled surgeons, skin biopsy was not possible. The peritoneal dialysis regimen was intensified with the patient switching to continuous cycler peritoneal dialysis and doing 6 exchanges throughout the night with 2.5-L peritoneal dialysate fluid, each bag composed of 2.5 % dextrose and each exchange for 2.5 hours. The patient underwent 4-gland parathyroidectomy with implantation of half of the right superior parathyroid gland on the right forearm. The patient also received aggressive wound care daily. The calciphylaxis lesions healed completely in 3 months with intense peritoneal dialysis, sodium thiosulfate infusion for 3 months, and parathyroidectomy. The dialysis modality was never changed to hemodialysis during the management of calciphylaxis lesions.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

args:
A 52-year-old male patient was admitted in emergency in our clinic for acute abdomen and signs of peritonitis: diffuse abdominal pain and abdominal rigidity, ileus, nausea and chills. Anamnesis revealed no significant personal or family medical history, but the patient described a decreased appetite and weight loss of approximately 5 kg in the last 4 weeks.
Paraclinical exams supported the diagnoses of peritonitis, with leukocytosis (18000/ mmc), increased CRP and ESR (20mm/ h). The lab test also showed severe anemia (Hb 7 mg/ dl). As simple abdominal X-ray revealed pneumoperitoneum, an emergency surgery was mandatory.
After midline laparotomy, purulent generalized peritonitis, peritoneal carcinomatosis, ascitis and a giant malignant gastric perforation of 3,5-4 cm diameter were encountered. The surrounding gastric walls were rigid, infiltrated by the neoplastic process, with signs of gastric linitis plastica. The intent to mobilize the stomach was difficult and risky, so the classical techniques for repair could not be applied.
Due to the lack of the available gastric material, an atypical solution of gastric packing with greater omentum by means of transgastric traction had to be used. The intra-surgery images are relevant regarding this aspect.
The surgical technique had to be adapted to the local conditions. It includes highlighting the 3 distinct anatomical areas, individualized and personalized as operatory steps. The greater omentum became the efficient mobile part, which could be adapted for the creation of the contention effect. The gastric part is part of the technical criteria and the imposition carrying transduction becomes omentoplasty. The anatomic oro-gastric path represents the third distinct component reserved for supporting and handling the entire device and tissue-to-tissue fixation, whose biosynthesis is effective in time.
Materials, tools, and devices are specific and are part of the arsenal for transgastric omentoplasty kit.
A fragment of 4/3/1 cm of epiploic tissue was collected and sent to the histopathological examination for confirmation. The macroscopic examination showed whitish areas suggestive of mucinous carcinoma metastasis. The microscopic examination of hematoxylin and eosin-stained tissue sections from the formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded surgical specimens revealed invasive mucinous carcinoma epiploic metastasis.
The postoperative follow up includes both drainage of subhepatic lodge, individualized in this case with two drain tubes (Pezzer and silicone) and a nasogastric probe. Loco-regional specific imagistics is imposed by the initiation of oral food intake.
The Barium radiograph 19 days post-surgery revealed: normal esophagus, increased fornix-diaphragm distance, giant malignant niche, with normal gastro-intestinal transit. Thoracic X-ray showed pleural liquid in both pulmonary bases.
The discharge diagnosis was perforated mucinous gastric carcinoma, gastric linitis, peritoneal carcinomatosis, neoplastic ascitis, generalized purulent peritonitis, gastric packing with epiploic material, severe secondary anemia, bilateral pleurisy.
The surgical solution proved successful for the immediate development of gastric perforation. Due to the extensive lesions and general risk factors, the two-stage surgery was chosen for the presented case. The underlying condition was not focused on in emergency, the patient being directed to the Oncology Department.

The Barium radiograph 19 days post-surgery revealed: normal esophagus, increased fornix-diaphragm distance, giant malignant niche, with normal gastro-intestinal transit. Thoracic X-ray showed pleural liquid in both pulmonary bases.
The discharge diagnosis was perforated mucinous gastric carcinoma, gastric linitis, peritoneal carcinomatosis, neoplastic ascitis, generalized purulent peritonitis, gastric packing with epiploic material, severe secondary anemia, bilateral pleurisy.

args:
A 66-year-old African American male with a past medical history of hypertension, alcohol use, and asthma presented with facial swelling. He was discharged two months prior for a similar event related to lisinopril. On initial presentation, the patient's vitals were significant for heart rate of 104 beats per minute, respiratory rate of 30 breaths per minute, blood pressure of 190/100 mmHg, and oxygen saturation of 99% with a non-rebreather mask at 15 liters. Physical exam was significant for moderate to severe respiratory distress, inspiratory stridor, and subcostal and intercostal retractions. Lungs were clear to auscultation. The patient was otherwise alert and oriented. Arterial blood gas showed pH 7.447, pCO2 39, pO2 197, and HCO3 27 on 60% FiO2. His initial metabolic panel was significant for sodium 104 mmol/L, potassium 2.6 mmol/L, chloride 66 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) 5 mg/dL (baseline 20 mg/dL), and creatinine 0.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.0 mg/dL). Glucose was 165 mg/dL. Magnesium and phosphorus were 1.1 and 2.1 mg/dL, respectively. Serum uric acid was low at 2.7 mg/dL. Complete blood count showed no leukocytosis or anemia. Urinalysis had a specific gravity of 1.010, moderate blood, and 15-29 red blood cells. Urine electrolytes were notable for a sodium of 48 mmol/L and osmolality of 217 mOsm/kg. Serum osmolality was 215 mOsm/kg. Urine and serum toxicology were negative for any substances. Alcohol level was negative.
Due to concern for airway protection, the patient was intubated and admitted to the intensive care unit. Initial chest x-ray held concern for infectious process, but antibiotics were stopped after infectious workup was negative. The patient was given two units of fresh-frozen plasma, famotidine, and Benadryl, and started on methylprednisolone 60 mg every six hours for angioedema. Lisinopril was held at this time. For his hyponatremia, the patient's labs were suspicious for syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone (SIADH) and a one-liter fluid restriction was initiated with nephrology consult. Thyroid and adrenal workup to rule out other causes of SIADH was unremarkable. A CT chest scan was done to evaluate for malignancy as a cause of SIADH, but did not demonstrate overt suspicious lung pathology (Figure ). The patient's hyponatremia appropriately corrected over the course of one week and was 130 mmol/L upon discharge. Lisinopril was discontinued and marked as an allergy for the patient. On follow-up approximately nine months later, the patient's sodium had corrected and remained within normal limits.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 18)

args: A 49-year-old man with class I obesity, well-controlled asthma, and no prior thrombo-embolic episodes presented to the acute evaluation area this morning complaining that his right lower leg had become “tight and sore” over the past 18 hours. He explained that five days earlier he developed a febrile influenza-like illness that confined him to bed except for brief trips to the bathroom; according to the activity tracker on his smartwatch his daily step count fell below 250 for four consecutive days, whereas his baseline averages more than 6500. Fevers resolved two days ago, but yesterday evening, while rising from the couch after a prolonged streaming-video session, he felt a pulling discomfort deep in the upper portion of the calf. Overnight the pain intensified when he bore weight, and by dawn he noticed his right trouser leg fit tighter mid-calf, prompting today’s visit. He denies recent flights, car trips longer than an hour, trauma, falls, vigorous exercise, insect bites, dog scratches, erythema, skin breaks, groin masses, hematuria, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, syncope, fevers since the viral episode, or unintentional weight loss. Medications include budesonide-formoterol inhaler twice daily and as-needed albuterol; he has never taken glucocorticoids systemically, hormones, erythropoiesis-stimulating agents, or anticoagulants. Family history is negative for venous or pulmonary emboli, and he does not smoke; he has no personal history of malignancy and is not undergoing evaluation or treatment for cancer; alcohol is limited to two beers on Saturday, and he denies recreational drugs. On arrival—prior to analgesics, fluids, or antithrombotic therapy—he sat upright with the knee flexed to ease discomfort. Triage vitals were temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 126/78 mm Hg, heart rate 88 beats min⁻¹ regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths min⁻¹, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Jugular venous pulsation was not elevated. Cardiac examination revealed a crisp S₁ and S₂ without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lungs were clear to auscultation. The abdomen was soft and nontender, with no hepatosplenomegaly or ascites. Inspection of the lower extremities showed normal coloration, absence of streaking, hyperpigmentation, vesicles, varicosities, or newly surfaced subcutaneous channels. The right calf contour appeared fuller posteriorly, whereas the right thigh, knee, ankle, and foot matched the contralateral side. Using a cloth metric tape placed exactly 10 cm distal to the tibial tuberosity, nursing staff documented a circumference of 41.0 cm on the symptomatic side versus 36.8 cm on the left, a 4.2-cm discrepancy; 15 cm above the superior patellar pole both thighs measured 56.3 cm, and at the malleolar level girths were 27.1 cm right and 26.9 cm left. Thumb pressure over the anterior tibial crest rebounded immediately without leaving a depression. Palpation tracing the anticipated path of the popliteal vein through the gastrocnemius bellies elicited a sharply localized tenderness just medial to the soleus origin; identical pressure over adjacent muscle groups produced only dull, diffuse soreness. No palpable cord, fluctuance, crepitus, cystic mass, or warmth beyond a mild temperature increase over the tender track was appreciated. Passive ankle dorsiflexion with the knee extended reproduced the deep aching at the same spot but did not generate cramp elsewhere. Hip, knee, and ankle ranges of motion were otherwise full and painless; distal pulses (dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial) were 2 + bilaterally, capillary refill to all toes was under two seconds, light-touch sensation was intact throughout, and motor strength in ankle plantar flexion and dorsiflexion graded 5/5. There was no popliteal or inguinal lymphadenopathy. A pre-phlebotomy 12-lead ECG showed normal sinus rhythm at 86 beats min⁻¹ without ST-T changes. Point-of-care laboratory testing obtained before any medication revealed hemoglobin 14.1 g dL⁻¹, leukocytes 7.2 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, platelet count 229 × 10⁹ L⁻¹, serum sodium 138 mmol L⁻¹, potassium 4.1 mmol L⁻¹, bicarbonate 24 mmol L⁻¹, blood urea nitrogen 15 mg dL⁻¹, creatinine 0.95 mg dL⁻¹, international normalized ratio 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 27 s. A portable anteroposterior chest radiograph showed clear lung fields and a normal cardiac silhouette. No signs of cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, muscle tear, peripheral arterial disease, systemic fluid overload, or musculoskeletal injury were identified that would more plausibly explain the acute unilateral calf enlargement combined with pinpoint tenderness along the deep venous trajectory in the context of recent prolonged bed rest following an influenza illness; therefore a comprehensive duplex sonographic examination of the right ilio-femoral-popliteal and calf venous system was ordered while the patient remained hemodynamically stable, untreated, and awaiting imaging.

output.reasoning: The patient is a 49-year-old male. Active cancer is denied. Paralysis, paresis, or recent plaster immobilization of the lower extremities is not mentioned. Bedridden recently for 3 days or more, or major surgery within 12 weeks: The patient had a febrile illness confining him to bed for 4 days with step count <250/day. Localized tenderness along the distribution of the deep venous system: Present (tenderness medial to soleus origin). Entire leg swollen: Not mentioned (only calf discrepancy). Calf swelling > 3 cm compared to the asymptomatic side: Present (4.2 cm discrepancy). Pitting edema confined to the symptomatic leg: Not mentioned (the note explicitly says 'rebounded immediately without leaving a depression'). Collateral superficial veins: Not mentioned (explicitly stated 'absence of... newly surfaced subcutaneous channels'). Previously documented DVT: Patient denies prior thrombo-embolic episodes. Alternative diagnosis at least as likely as DVT: The note explicitly states 'No signs of ... ruptured Baker cyst, muscle tear... were identified that would more plausibly explain the acute unilateral calf enlargement'.

active_cancer: Active cancer (treatment within 6 months) (+1)

args: A 58-year-old woman with an eight-year history of biopsy-proven nonalcoholic steatohepatitis that progressed to compensated cirrhosis despite sustained weight-loss efforts was brought by ambulance to the emergency department after her adult son found her at home around noon lying on the living-room carpet, staring blankly at the ceiling, mumbling incomprehensibly, and unable to recognize him. According to the son, she had seemed “a bit slow” for two days, had skipped breakfast that morning, and had been awake until 3 a.m. watching television but could not be roused for her usual morning insulin dose. No seizure-like activity, head trauma, or witnessed ingestion of alcohol, sedatives, or illicit substances was reported. Her medical background included long-standing type 2 diabetes on insulin glargine 22 units nightly and mealtime insulin aspart, stage 3 chronic kidney disease with a baseline creatinine of 1.3 mg/dL, hypertension treated with amlodipine 10 mg daily, remote gastric sleeve surgery, and obstructive sleep apnea for which she intermittently used continuous positive airway pressure. She had never consumed more than one glass of wine monthly, denied tobacco or recreational drug use, and had no known drug allergies. Surveillance endoscopy six months earlier showed small varices that did not warrant banding, and she had missed her most recent hepatology appointment because of transportation problems. Family history was notable for a mother who died of complications of viral hepatitis at 62 and a father with coronary artery disease; no siblings had liver disease. She lived alone, worked part-time as a bank receptionist, and followed a predominantly plant-based diet but admitted occasional fast-food meals. Review of systems obtained from her son revealed intermittent nausea, darker urine over the past week, yellowing of her eyes noticed two days earlier, and generalized fatigue; there had been no coughing, chest pain, palpitations, fevers, chills, vomiting of blood, black stools, or weight change. Upon arrival—before any fluids, medications, or blood draws—triage vital signs were temperature 37.1 °C, blood pressure 98/52 mm Hg, heart rate 112 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 95 % on ambient air. She appeared deeply jaundiced, diaphoretic, and unresponsive to verbal commands; pupils were equal and reactive, corneal reflexes were intact, she withdrew her arms sluggishly to a trapezius pinch, and when her arms were briefly extended she exhibited no flapping tremor but rather drifted and dropped them back onto the stretcher. Gag and cough reflexes were present yet weak, and her speech consisted of low-pitched moans without discernible words. Skin examination revealed multiple spider angiomata across the upper chest and faint palmar erythema; mucous membranes were tacky. The neck was supple with flat jugular veins. Cardiac auscultation disclosed a tachycardic but regular rhythm without murmurs or extra sounds, and lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender with normal bowel sounds; the liver edge was difficult to appreciate beneath the right costal margin, and no splenic tip was palpated. No pedal edema or peripheral bruising was noted. Capillary glucose measured 74 mg/dL. Point-of-care abdominal ultrasonography performed at the bedside before venipuncture demonstrated a shrunken, coarsely echogenic liver with nodular surface and patent hepatopetal portal flow at 11 cm/s; the gallbladder contained no stones, common bile duct diameter was 5 mm, and the spleen measured 18 cm in length. Portable chest radiograph showed clear lung fields and elevated diaphragms without effusion, and a 12-lead electrocardiogram recorded sinus tachycardia at 110 beats/min with a QTc of 448 ms but no ischemic changes. Initial laboratory studies drawn immediately thereafter—prior to any vitamin K, albumin infusion, antibiotics, or osmotic cathartics—revealed total bilirubin 7.9 mg/dL, serum albumin 2.35 g/dL, alanine aminotransferase 72 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 98 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L, gamma-glutamyl transferase 284 U/L, sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 96 mmol/L, bicarbonate 19 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 42 mg/dL, creatinine 1.7 mg/dL, white blood cell count 9.8 × 10⁹/L, hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL, platelet count 74 × 10⁹/L, and a prothrombin time corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 2.88. Serum ammonia sent concurrently returned markedly elevated at 156 µmol/L. Hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibody remained negative, ferritin measured 240 ng/mL, and alpha-fetoprotein was 9 ng/mL. Urinalysis demonstrated dark amber urine with moderate bilirubin and 1 + protein but no leukocyte esterase or nitrites. Blood and urine cultures were obtained, and broad-spectrum antibiotics were ordered but not yet administered at the moment these findings were documented.
CRITICAL HINT: For Child-Pugh, ascites_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Slight/Mild, 3=Moderate/Severe. encephalopathy_grade is 1=Absent, 2=Grade 1-2, 3=Grade 3-4.

output.reasoning: The patient is a 58-year-old woman with known cirrhosis. Child-Pugh scoring requires assessing five parameters: Total Bilirubin, Serum Albumin, INR, Ascites, and Encephalopathy. 1) Total Bilirubin: 7.9 mg/dL (>3 mg/dL = 3 points). 2) Serum Albumin: 2.35 g/dL (<2.8 g/dL = 3 points). 3) INR: 2.88 (>2.3 = 3 points). 4) Ascites: Exam noted "The abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender" and "no pedal edema". Ascites is assessed as absent (1 point). 5) Encephalopathy: The patient is unresponsive to verbal commands, mumbling, and "staring blankly", which fits criteria for Grade 3-4 (3 points).

args: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman arrived to the outpatient urgent evaluation suite late this morning because her right calf had become progressively tight and sore over the preceding thirty-six hours. She had been recovering well from a minimally invasive total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy performed five weeks ago for symptomatic leiomyomata; the procedure lasted three hours under general anesthesia, involved placement of four laparoscopic ports, and she was discharged on postoperative day two after ambulating independently on the ward. At follow-up with gynecology two weeks ago the incisions were healing cleanly, and she had resumed light household tasks, including walking her dog around the block twice a day. Yesterday before breakfast she sensed a dull pressure deep in the posterior aspect of the right calf while descending a flight of stairs; by evening she noticed that her pant leg was noticeably tighter on that side and that the discomfort intensified when she extended the knee or stood still for several minutes. This morning her husband wrapped a tailor’s tape around both legs at a point ten centimeters distal to the tibial tuberosity and obtained 42.5 cm on the symptomatic side compared with 37.8 cm on the left, a discrepancy of 4.7 cm that prompted the present visit. She denies swelling of the thigh, knee, ankle, or foot, and reports no change in skin coloration, rash, or warmth elsewhere on the limb. Her past history is notable for hypertension controlled with amlodipine 5 mg daily and a remote episode of left-sided deep venous thrombosis diagnosed eight years ago after a thirteen-hour trans-Pacific flight; that event was treated with six months of warfarin and there have been no recurrences since. She has never had malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, nephrotic syndrome, or systemic rheumatologic illness, and no first-degree relative has experienced venous thromboembolism before age fifty. She quit smoking two decades ago, drinks wine on holidays only, and uses no estrogen replacement, glucocorticoids, or over-the-counter supplements. Review of systems is negative for fever, chills, night sweats, weight loss, chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, palpitations, dyspnea, cough, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, or recent infectious symptoms. She has not sustained trauma to the limb, undergone intramuscular injections, or received new vaccinations, and she denies varicose vein stripping, sclerotherapy, or prior vascular procedures on the extremities. On arrival she was seated comfortably but favored the right leg when rising. Vital signs before any medication administration were temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 132/74 mm Hg, heart rate 82 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 14 per minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed a normal S1 and S2 with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and clear breath sounds bilaterally without wheeze or crackle. Abdomen was soft with well-healed port sites, no tenderness, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated visible fullness confined to the middle third of the right calf; the skin was the same color as the contralateral side, with a subtle glossy sheen but no erythema, bruising, ulceration, or tortuous superficial channels. There was no edema of the dorsum of the foot or dependent ankle. Palpation beginning at the groin and continuing through the femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, and posterior tibial groove elicited discrete tenderness when the examiner pressed just medial to the gastrocnemius belly along the expected trajectory of the deep venous bundle; pressing elsewhere on muscle bulk produced only generalized soreness. No palpable subcutaneous cord, nodularity, or fluctuance was detected, and the compartments were soft without tenseness. Measurement by clinic staff confirmed the patient’s home findings: circumference 42.5 cm on the right versus 37.8 cm on the left at the standardized landmark. Thigh girth fifteen centimeters proximal to the superior patellar border was symmetric at 49 cm bilaterally, and ankle circumference was likewise equal. Both dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ and symmetric, capillary refill in all toes was under two seconds, skin temperature assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand felt marginally warmer over the affected calf, and sensation to light touch and pinprick remained intact. Active and passive range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle was full, although full knee extension accentuated the posterior calf ache. There was no appreciable knee effusion, meniscal click, or ligamentous laxity, and straight-leg raise testing did not reproduce radicular pain. Examination of the lumbar spine was unremarkable, making neurogenic claudication unlikely. Basic laboratory studies drawn prior to any anticoagulant therapy showed hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelet count 256 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 6.9 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.92 mg/dL, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, aspartate aminotransferase 22 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 24 U/L, prothrombin time 12.0 seconds with an international normalized ratio of 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 30 seconds. Urinalysis was unremarkable and a resting electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm without acute ischemic changes. No signs pointed toward cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, Achilles tendinopathy, compartment syndrome, or musculoskeletal tear that might plausibly rival a thrombotic process, and the absence of visible superficial collateral veins or whole-limb edema further narrowed the differential. Given the recent intra-abdominal surgery under general anesthesia inside the last twelve weeks, the objectively documented calf circumference difference exceeding three centimeters, focal tenderness traced precisely along the deep venous tract, her prior history of venous clotting, and the paucity of another equally compelling explanation, an occlusion of the deep venous system in the right calf remains the leading working diagnosis pending duplex ultrasonography, which has been requested on an expedited basis while the patient is monitored in the unit.

Localized tenderness along the distribution of the deep venous system: Yes (elicited discrete tenderness along the expected trajectory of the deep venous bundle). Entire leg swollen: No (swelling confined to right calf). Calf swelling >3 cm compared to asymptomatic side: Yes (4.7 cm difference). Previously documented DVT: Yes (remote episode 8 years ago). Alternative diagnosis at least as likely as DVT: No (note explicitly states 'paucity of another equally compelling explanation').

args: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman arrived to the outpatient urgent evaluation suite late this morning because her right calf had become progressively tight and sore over the preceding thirty-six hours. She had been recovering well from a minimally invasive total abdominal hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy performed five weeks ago for symptomatic leiomyomata; the procedure lasted three hours under general anesthesia, involved placement of four laparoscopic ports, and she was discharged on postoperative day two after ambulating independently on the ward. At follow-up with gynecology two weeks ago the incisions were healing cleanly, and she had resumed light household tasks, including walking her dog around the block twice a day. Yesterday before breakfast she sensed a dull pressure deep in the posterior aspect of the right calf while descending a flight of stairs; by evening she noticed that her pant leg was noticeably tighter on that side and that the discomfort intensified when she extended the knee or stood still for several minutes. This morning her husband wrapped a tailor’s tape around both legs at a point ten centimeters distal to the tibial tuberosity and obtained 42.5 cm on the symptomatic side compared with 37.8 cm on the left, a discrepancy of 4.7 cm that prompted the present visit. She denies swelling of the thigh, knee, ankle, or foot, and reports no change in skin coloration, rash, or warmth elsewhere on the limb. Her past history is notable for hypertension controlled with amlodipine 5 mg daily and a remote episode of left-sided deep venous thrombosis diagnosed eight years ago after a thirteen-hour trans-Pacific flight; that event was treated with six months of warfarin and there have been no recurrences since. She has never had malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, nephrotic syndrome, or systemic rheumatologic illness, and no first-degree relative has experienced venous thromboembolism before age fifty. She quit smoking two decades ago, drinks wine on holidays only, and uses no estrogen replacement, glucocorticoids, or over-the-counter supplements. Review of systems is negative for fever, chills, night sweats, weight loss, chest pain, pleuritic discomfort, palpitations, dyspnea, cough, hemoptysis, abdominal pain, or recent infectious symptoms. She has not sustained trauma to the limb, undergone intramuscular injections, or received new vaccinations, and she denies varicose vein stripping, sclerotherapy, or prior vascular procedures on the extremities. On arrival she was seated comfortably but favored the right leg when rising. Vital signs before any medication administration were temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 132/74 mm Hg, heart rate 82 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 14 per minute, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Cardiopulmonary examination revealed a normal S1 and S2 with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, and clear breath sounds bilaterally without wheeze or crackle. Abdomen was soft with well-healed port sites, no tenderness, and no hepatosplenomegaly. Inspection of the lower extremities demonstrated visible fullness confined to the middle third of the right calf; the skin was the same color as the contralateral side, with a subtle glossy sheen but no erythema, bruising, ulceration, or tortuous superficial channels. There was no edema of the dorsum of the foot or dependent ankle. Palpation beginning at the groin and continuing through the femoral triangle, adductor canal, popliteal fossa, and posterior tibial groove elicited discrete tenderness when the examiner pressed just medial to the gastrocnemius belly along the expected trajectory of the deep venous bundle; pressing elsewhere on muscle bulk produced only generalized soreness. No palpable subcutaneous cord, nodularity, or fluctuance was detected, and the compartments were soft without tenseness. Measurement by clinic staff confirmed the patient’s home findings: circumference 42.5 cm on the right versus 37.8 cm on the left at the standardized landmark. Thigh girth fifteen centimeters proximal to the superior patellar border was symmetric at 49 cm bilaterally, and ankle circumference was likewise equal. Both dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses were 2+ and symmetric, capillary refill in all toes was under two seconds, skin temperature assessed with the dorsum of the examiner’s hand felt marginally warmer over the affected calf, and sensation to light touch and pinprick remained intact. Active and passive range of motion at the hip, knee, and ankle was full, although full knee extension accentuated the posterior calf ache. There was no appreciable knee effusion, meniscal click, or ligamentous laxity, and straight-leg raise testing did not reproduce radicular pain. Examination of the lumbar spine was unremarkable, making neurogenic claudication unlikely. Basic laboratory studies drawn prior to any anticoagulant therapy showed hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelet count 256 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 6.9 × 10⁹/L, serum creatinine 0.92 mg/dL, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, aspartate aminotransferase 22 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 24 U/L, prothrombin time 12.0 seconds with an international normalized ratio of 1.0, and activated partial thromboplastin time 30 seconds. Urinalysis was unremarkable and a resting electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm without acute ischemic changes. No signs pointed toward cellulitis, ruptured Baker cyst, Achilles tendinopathy, compartment syndrome, or musculoskeletal tear that might plausibly rival a thrombotic process, and the absence of visible superficial collateral veins or whole-limb edema further narrowed the differential. Given the recent intra-abdominal surgery under general anesthesia inside the last twelve weeks, the objectively documented calf circumference difference exceeding three centimeters, focal tenderness traced precisely along the deep venous tract, her prior history of venous clotting, and the paucity of another equally compelling explanation, an occlusion of the deep venous system in the right calf remains the leading working diagnosis pending duplex ultrasonography, which has been requested on an expedited basis while the patient is monitored in the unit.

args: A 75-year-old woman was transferred to the pre-operative holding area this morning for a planned right knee arthroscopy to address persistent mechanical symptoms and degenerative meniscal tearing that has failed conservative management. She has spent the past week on the orthopedic ward after slipping in her kitchen and twisting the same knee; although no fracture was identified, the resulting pain has rendered her essentially chair-to-bedbound, and over the last four days nursing notes document that she has only ambulated a few steps to the bathroom with a walker. During this period of relative immobility, she has noticed progressive pitting edema extending to the mid-calf on both sides, worse in the evening, accompanied by a sense of heaviness but no erythema or warmth. Of note, twelve years ago she developed a thrombus in the left popliteal vein following a prolonged trans-Atlantic flight, treated then with a three-month course of warfarin and serial duplex surveillance; she has had no lung clots and has not required chronic anticoagulation since. Her cardiovascular history is significant for longstanding hypertension and an exacerbation of left-sided systolic heart failure three weeks ago triggered by dietary indiscretion and medication non-adherence, for which she was hospitalized, diuresed aggressively, and discharged on a higher dose of loop diuretics along with strict salt restrictions; since that discharge she reports orthopnea limited to one pillow and no further paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, though she still fatigues easily. She has not undergone any other surgical procedures in the past 30 days. She denies fevers, chills, productive cough, urinary complaints, abdominal pain, or recent infections, and she has not required intravenous lines beyond routine peripheral access. There have been no episodes of sepsis or pneumonia, and she has not needed an immobilizing plaster cast or brace. Imaging confirmed no fractures of the hip, pelvis, or leg, and she has had no recent strokes, multisystem trauma, spinal cord injuries, or myocardial infarctions. She does not have clinically apparent varicose veins, has never had malignancy, inflammatory bowel disease, or chronic lung disease. She quit smoking thirty years ago and drinks wine socially. Home medications include lisinopril, furosemide, carvedilol, low-dose aspirin, and calcium-vitamin D; she is not on hormone therapy or oral contraceptives and has been menopausal since age 50. Family history is notable only for a brother with coronary artery disease; there is no known familial clotting disorder. On arrival her blood pressure is 138/74 mmHg, heart rate 86 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 18 per minute, oxygen saturation 97 % on room air, and temperature 36.8 °C. She appears comfortable at rest but needs assistance to swing her legs onto the stretcher. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated, and heart sounds reveal a soft S1, normal S2, and an S3 gallop consistent with her ventricular dysfunction; no murmurs are appreciated. Chest is clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdominal exam is benign with no hepatosplenomegaly. Both lower extremities show 2+ pitting edema to mid-calf with intact distal pulses, no skin discoloration, and fullness of the right knee joint without overlying warmth; passive range of motion elicits pain along the medial joint line. Neurologic exam is non-focal, and she moves all extremities against gravity. Laboratory results drawn yesterday show hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, platelet count 256 × 10^3 /µL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, normal electrolytes, and BNP mildly elevated at 220 pg/mL. Baseline ECG demonstrates sinus rhythm with left bundle branch block unchanged from prior. A recent transthoracic echocardiogram showed an ejection fraction of 40 % with mild mitral regurgitation and no intracardiac thrombus. Duplex ultrasound of the legs performed on admission revealed chronic post-thrombotic changes in the left popliteal segment without acute thrombus. She has no indwelling catheters or ports. Given her age, history of venous clotting, chronic heart pump dysfunction with a recent flare, current bilateral leg swelling, and the fact that she has spent most of the past week largely confined to bed awaiting this minimally invasive orthopedic procedure, she is considered to be at heightened risk for peri-operative thromboembolic complications. The surgical, anesthesia, and medical teams are coordinating to optimize her fluid balance, consider prophylactic strategies, and minimize delays so that she can regain mobility promptly after the scope-guided intervention.

CRITICAL HINT: For Caprini, Age 41-60=1 pt, 61-74=2 pts, >=75=3 pts. Elective lower extremity arthroplasty = 5 pts. Hip, pelvis or leg fracture = 5 pts. Stroke/paralysis = 5 pts. Multiple trauma = 5 pts. Acute spinal cord injury = 5 pts. Major surgery (>45 min) = 2 pts. Central venous access = 2 pts. Bed rest > 72 hours = 2 pts. Minor surgery = 1 pt. History of major surgery (<1 month) = 1 pt. COPD = 1 pt. BMI > 25 = 1 pt.

output.reasoning: Age: 75 (≥75=3 pts). Gender: Female (0 pts). Elective lower extremity arthroplasty (knee): planned (5 pts). History of major surgery (<1 month): Denied (0 pts). History of major surgery (>45 min): Denied recent (0 pts). Hip, pelvis or leg fracture: Denied (0 pts). Stroke/paralysis: Denied (0 pts). Multiple trauma: Denied (0 pts). Acute spinal cord injury: Denied (0 pts). Central venous access: Denied (0 pts). Bed rest > 72 hours: Present (spent past week mostly in bed, 2 pts). Minor surgery: Denied (0 pts). COPD: Denied (0 pts). BMI > 25: Not explicitly stated, though implies adult weight, BMI calculation not possible without height/weight (0 pts). Other risk factors like VTE history and heart failure are generally not points in standard Caprini scoring as listed in the hints, but will be noted as present/absent per instructions.

args:
A 17-year-old South Asian man was referred to our department for recurrent episodes of generalized muscle weakness, fatigue and cramps from the age of two years. Hypokalemia was initially detected at the first episode and he was treated for periodic hypokalemic paralysis. Before the age of 12 years, these episodes were mild, readily improved with ringer lactate and potassium chloride by his treating physician and occurred only twice a year. Beyond the age of 12 years, these episodes gradually worsened in frequency and intensity to the point that they were refractory to previously administered therapies and sometimes required hospitalization.
His symptoms were aggravated by physical activity and hot weather, and were associated with polyuria, polydipsia, nocturia, paresthesias and occasional painless non-bloody diarrhea. He denied the use of any medication, including laxatives and diuretics. He also had a history of short stature and poor weight gain. His birth history was unremarkable but developmental landmarks were delayed. There was no family history of renal salt-wasting syndromes or delayed puberty. However, his parents were first cousins.
An examination revealed a thin and lean, but well-oriented adolescent with no acute distress. His height and weight were below the third percentile (weight 30kg; height 147cm). His blood pressure was 95/60mmHg with orthostatic changes. His sexual development was at Tanner Stage II.
Investigations at the admitting hospital revealed a normal leukocyte count, platelet count, hemoglobin level and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. His urea level was 34mg/dL, creatinine 0.9mg/dL and random blood glucose 105mg/dL. His estimated glomerular filtration rate based on the Modification of Diet in Renal Disease formula was 97.9ml/min per 1.73m2. His high blood urea nitrogen level was attributed to mild dehydration due to the hot weather. He was found to have metabolic alkalosis: pH 7.58, HCO3- 33.5, base excess +7.6. His serum electrolytes were as follows: sodium 124mmol/L, potassium 2.4mmol/L, calcium 6.5mmol/L and phosphate 3.4mg/dL. His serum magnesium level was not checked at that time. Serum albumin, creatine phosphokinase, free thyroxin, thyroid-stimulating hormone and parathyroid hormone levels were normal.
He was provisionally diagnosed with periodic hypokalemic paralysis and hypocalcemia. Treatment was started with potassium and calcium supplements. After one month, he showed little improvement and he was referred to our department for further evaluation.
Further laboratory tests revealed a low magnesium level (1.2mg/dl) and decreased 24-hour urinary excretion of calcium (25.9mg/24 h). Luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone levels were also normal. His level of testosterone was low (0.85ng/mL, normal for his age 2.67 to 10.12ng/mL). Electrocardiography showed a normal sinus rhythm and prolonged QT interval. No abnormality was found on an abdominal X-ray, chest X-ray or brain magnetic resonance imaging. A renal ultrasound revealed a prominent pelvicalyceal system. An insulin tolerance test showed normal cortisol and growth hormone response.
Based on the findings of hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis without hypertension, severe hypomagnesemia and hypocalciuria, a diagnosis of GS was made. No organic cause of hypogonadism was established and the sex hormone findings were attributed to delayed puberty. Treatment was started with oral supplementation of potassium, magnesium and calcium along with spironolactone and liberal salt intake. Our patient and his parents were also counseled about delayed puberty.
A limitation to this case report was that the diagnosis could not be ascertained with sequencing of the implicated gene due to unavailability of genetic sequencing at the presenting hospital (Jinnah Hospital Lahore, Pakistan).

weight_kg: Patient weight in kg (e.g., 70)
sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 150)
age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")

args: A 55-year-old Samoan warehouse supervisor arrived to the endocrinology walk-in evaluation unit at 06:45 after his employer’s annual “dawn draw” health fair flagged a “high fasting sugar—see a doctor promptly” next to his badge number; he purposefully scheduled today’s visit before attempting any diet or medication changes so that the results would represent an uncompromised baseline. For the past decade his duties have shifted from operating a pallet jack to coordinating inbound shipments from a seated workstation overlooking the loading docks, and in that interval he has accumulated roughly 18 kg—the needle on a recent home scale hovered around 122 kg at a height of 178 cm, yielding a body-mass index of 38.5 kg/m². He notes that over the last two years his belt has required two additional notches, and a favorite rugby jersey feels tight across the abdomen. His typical weekday breakfast is skipped in favor of a sugary canned energy drink during the 40-minute commute; lunch is often a plate from the rotating food-truck court (fried noodles or teriyaki chicken with white rice), and late-night dinners consist of reheated leftovers after his children’s basketball practice. He reports a persistent dry mouth by sunrise, refills a one-liter insulated tumbler three to four times during each shift, and usually wakes once nightly to void—changes he had attributed to the ambient heat on the warehouse floor. He denies unintended weight loss, incessant hunger, blurred vision, slow-healing skin wounds, recurrent yeast infections, or distal paresthesias, though he admits irritability and hand shakiness if lunch is delayed past 14:00, symptoms relieved within minutes of a sweet snack from the vending machine. Past medical history includes borderline hypertension measured on pharmacy kiosks but never treated, mild gastroesophageal reflux disease controlled with over-the-counter ranitidine, and a remote left Achilles tendon repair after a recreational rugby injury at age 39. He has never required systemic glucocorticoids, antiretroviral therapy, second-generation antipsychotics, anabolic steroids, or testosterone supplementation. No prior formal evaluation of fasting glucose or hemoglobin A1c has been completed, and he has not used metformin, weight-loss injections, herbal bitter melon tablets, or ketogenic diets. His mother was diagnosed with adult-onset diabetes at 62 now managed by oral medication; a younger sister began oral antihyperglycemics after gestational glucose issues; and his father suffered a fatal anterior myocardial infarction at 58. He smoked cigarettes socially in his twenties (estimated 3-pack-years) but quit when his first child was born and denies e-cigarette, alcohol, or recreational drug use. Physical activity is limited to supervising forklift routes and weekend spectator duties at his children’s games; structured exercise is absent because of chronic ankle stiffness. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pressure, exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, snoring with apneas, mood depression, heat intolerance, or changes in hat size. At presentation he appeared well, conversant, and in no distress. Following nine minutes of seated rest vital signs read blood pressure 138/84 mm Hg in the right arm, pulse 76 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14/min, oral temperature 36.7 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % on room air. Neck circumference measured 45 cm without thyromegaly; a velvety hyperpigmented plaque with several pedunculated tags was observed along the posterior cervical fold and in both axillae. Cardiac examination disclosed a regular rhythm with crisp S1 and S2 and no murmurs, rubs, or gallops; radial and dorsalis pedis pulses were 2+ bilaterally. Pulmonary auscultation was clear. The abdomen was protuberant yet soft, non-tender, and without palpable liver edge or spleen; waist circumference at the midpoint between the lowest rib and iliac crest measured 118 cm. No peripheral edema was evident; Semmes-Weinstein monofilament testing demonstrated intact light-touch sensation on plantar surfaces, and vibration perception with a 128-Hz tuning fork was slightly diminished at both great toes relative to the medial malleoli. Deep tendon reflexes were symmetric. Funduscopic inspection revealed sharp disc margins and no hemorrhages. The patient confirmed consuming only water after 8 p.m. the previous evening; venipuncture occurred at 07:02, and specimens reached the laboratory within 20 minutes in a chilled carrier. Fasting plasma glucose returned at 142 mg/dL by hexokinase assay, and a simultaneous fasting serum insulin concentration measured 34 µIU/mL using a chemiluminescent immunoassay platform. From the same sample, hemoglobin A1c was 6.3 %, serum creatinine 0.96 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate >90 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.3 mEq/L, chloride 103 mEq/L, bicarbonate 25 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 16 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 41 U/L, and aspartate aminotransferase 36 U/L. Fasting lipid profile revealed total cholesterol 226 mg/dL, low-density lipoprotein 148 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein 38 mg/dL, and triglycerides 211 mg/dL. Thyroid-stimulating hormone was 2.0 µIU/mL, morning cortisol 14 µg/dL, and high-sensitivity C-reactive protein 3.8 mg/L. Urinalysis showed specific gravity 1.012, trace protein, negative ketones, and no glucosuria; spot urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio measured 17 mg/g. A resting 12-lead electrocardiogram displayed normal sinus rhythm at 74 beats/min with no ischemic changes or conduction delay. The patient emphasized that he has not yet altered dietary patterns, enrolled in a gym, initiated carbohydrate counting, or taken any prescription or non-prescription glycemic agents since the warehouse screening; he expresses interest in culturally relevant meal planning that accommodates communal family dinners, as well as feasible low-impact activity to fit between late shifts and weekend obligations.

fasting_glucose: Fasting glucose in mg/dL (e.g., 100)
fasting_insulin: Fasting insulin in uU/mL (e.g., 10)

args: A 38-year-old woman who works as a package-delivery driver, with a past medical history notable only for episodic migraine treated intermittently with over-the-counter naproxen and topiramate 50 mg nightly, was rushed to the emergency department by co-workers after they found her slumped in the back of her truck complaining that she had not voided a “full cup” of urine since the previous afternoon despite gulping energy drinks to stay awake during a heat-intensive holiday shift. She reported that three days earlier she initiated a stringent carbohydrate-restricted diet in preparation for a fitness competition, replacing most meals with salted jerky, protein bars, and caffeinated beverages; over the ensuing 48 hours she developed throbbing retro-orbital pain, photophobia, and repeated bouts of non-bloody, non-bilious emesis that rendered her unable to tolerate solid food or plain water. She steadfastly continued her topiramate but denied use of diuretics, antihypertensives, herbal weight-loss supplements, or recent iodinated contrast exposure. Baseline laboratories drawn at a primary-care visit four months earlier documented a serum creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL and normal electrolytes. On arrival—prior to any intravenous access, antiemetics, analgesics, or crystalloid—the patient appeared fatigued, spoke in whispered monosyllables, and was oriented only to person and place. Vital signs recorded at triage showed an oral temperature of 37.3 °C, blood pressure 96/62 mm Hg (self-reported baseline mid-110s systolic), heart rate 112 beats/min with a regular rhythm, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. She was 165 cm tall and weighed 63 kg, down approximately 2 kg from her last clinic visit. Mucous membranes were parched, the tongue fissured, and skin over the sternum tented slowly when pinched; jugular venous pulsations were not visible with the head of bed at 45°. Cardiac examination revealed a rapid but crisp S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; lungs were clear to auscultation. The abdomen was flat, mildly tender in the epigastrium without rebound or guarding, and bowel sounds were hypoactive. There was no flank or costovertebral angle tenderness, no palpable bladder distention, and a bedside bladder scan estimated only 22 mL of retained urine. Extremities were warm with a capillary refill time of four seconds, lacked peripheral edema or petechiae, and exhibited mild diffuse myalgia on palpation of the calves. Neurologic screen demonstrated sluggish pupillary reaction but no focal deficits. Within ten minutes of arrival a peripheral venipuncture sample and a freshly catheterized urine specimen were obtained. Core laboratory chemistries returned: serum sodium 157 mEq/L, potassium 4.9 mEq/L, chloride 114 mEq/L, bicarbonate 18 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 48 mg/dL, creatinine 2.55 mg/dL, glucose 109 mg/dL, calcium 8.7 mg/dL, phosphorus 4.1 mg/dL, and lactate 2.0 mmol/L. Venous blood gas analysis demonstrated pH 7.31 with PaCO₂ 35 mm Hg and base deficit −6 mEq/L. Complete blood count revealed hemoglobin 15.6 g/dL, hematocrit 47 %, white blood cell count 12.7 × 10³/µL with 83 % neutrophils, and platelets 245 × 10³/µL. High-sensitivity troponin was undetectable. A coagulation profile showed prothrombin time 13.0 s and international normalized ratio 1.1. The catheter tubing filled with dark amber urine that had a sharp ammoniacal odor; dipstick testing noted specific gravity greater than 1.030, trace protein, ketones 2+, no nitrites or leukocyte esterase, and small heme. Microscopy revealed fewer than three red blood cells and fewer than two white blood cells per high-power field with occasional fine hyaline casts but no granular or pigmented casts. Spot urine chemistries measured sodium 6 mEq/L, potassium 26 mEq/L, and creatinine 98 mg/dL; urine osmolality later returned markedly above concomitant serum osmolality. Point-of-care ultrasound showed kidneys of normal size and echotexture without hydronephrosis, and the inferior vena cava collapsed to less than one centimeter during quiet inspiration, suggestive of intravascular volume depletion. A focused transthoracic echocardiogram revealed a small, hyperdynamic left ventricle without pericardial effusion. Portable chest radiograph displayed clear lung fields and a normal cardiac silhouette, while a 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 110 beats/min with a QTc of 454 ms and no ST-segment deviations or peaked T waves. During the initial 45-minute observation period in the resuscitation bay—still before any fluid boluses, diuretic challenge, vasopressors, or nephrotoxic antibiotics—the Foley catheter drained only 9 mL of additional highly concentrated urine, corroborating persistent oliguria in the setting of marked hypernatremia and an abrupt rise in serum creatinine from a well-documented baseline, leaving the precise mechanism of her acute kidney dysfunction uncertain and prompting emergent nephrology consultation while plans were made for cautious isotonic fluid resuscitation, antiemetic control, serial metabolic panels, and strict urine output monitoring.

urine_sodium: Urine sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 20)
serum_sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
urine_creatinine: Urine creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 100)
serum_creatinine: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.5)
