args:
A 58-year-old male presented to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of fever, worsening perineal erythema, and pain. His medical history revealed only mild seasonal allergies; his functional status was very good. His surgical history was significant for an inguinal herniorrhaphy three years prior to his current presentation. His medications consisted of fexofenadine and pseudoephedrine for seasonal allergies. He was not in acute distress. On physical examination, he was noted to have significant perineal cellulitis and pain. His preoperative chest X-ray revealed no infiltrates, pulmonary edema or cardiomegaly. Computed tomography (CT) demonstrated extensive inflammation of the perineum and scrotum as well as subcutaneous air, consistent with the diagnosis of Fournier's gangrene. Preoperatively the patient was normotensive, but tachycardic (heart rate 110 – 120 beats / minute). No cardiac murmurs were identified. His electrocardiogram on admission to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) did not show any ST, T-wave changes. He did not demonstrate a third heart sound, peripheral edema, or jugular venous distention. His lungs were clear on auscultation bilaterally. His baseline SpO2 was 98% on room air. His hemoglobin and hematocrit were 14.9 g / dL and 44%, white blood cell count was 12.7 × 103 / mL, and platelet count was 146,000 / mL. His electrolyte panel featured sodium of 135 mmol / L, potassium 4.3 mmol / L, chloride 99 mmol / L, HCO3 26 mmol / L, blood urea nitrogen 16 mg / dL, and creatinine 1.14 mg / dL. He was without nourishment for > 8 hours preoperatively.
The patient was taken to the Operating Room for emergency surgical debridement of the Fournier's gangrene. He was premedicated with 2 milligrams (mg) of midazolam intravenously (IV) and anesthesia was induced with IV propofol (200 mg) and fentanyl (100 micrograms [mcg]). There was no central venous pressure monitor placed. A laryngeal mask airway (LMA, size number 4) was placed without difficulty. Anesthesia was maintained with sevoflurane 2 – 3%, with 50% oxygen and air. The patient received fentanyl 500 mcg and 1 mg of dilaudid for intraoperative analgesia. He remained hemodynamically stable throughout, but was tachypneic, with a respiratory rate of 35 breaths / minute. Surgical blood loss was ~200 mL and the procedure was otherwise uneventful. At the conclusion of surgery sevoflurane was discontinued and the patient was able to follow commands. However, before LMA removal the patient forcefully bit down on the LMA. Immediately after LMA removal, a laryngospasm was noted, with concurrent tachycardia and hypertension. Application of positive pressure via face mask (FiO2 100%) was unsuccessful in providing effective ventilation. Intravenous propofol and succinylcholine were administered to facilitate orotracheal intubation with a 7.5 endotracheal tube (ETT). Auscultation revealed bilateral rales, and pink frothy secretions were suctioned from the ETT. He did develop a brief episode of tachycardia (120 – 130 beats per minute) and hypertension (170 – 180 / 90 – 100 mmHg) on extubation. His SpO2 remained at 85 – 88% despite an FiO2 of 100%. His arterial blood gas (ABG) in the operating room showed pH 7.27, PaCO2 59 mmHg, PaO2 46 mmHg, HCO3 of 16 mmol / L, base excess of –1.9, and oxygen saturation of 75%, with a lactate of 1.27 mmol / L. He was on synchronized intermittent mechanical ventilation (SIMV) at 12 cycles / minute, tidal volume 750 mL, pressure support 10 cm H2O, with positive end-expiratory pressure (PEEP) of 10 cm H2O. After PEEP was increased to 12 cm H2O, his SpO2 recovered to > 90%. Of note, his peak and plateau pressures were only minimally elevated and his ability to generate a negative pressure was adequate (> –25 cm H2O). He was transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU) where he was continued on SIMV, with a tidal volume of 580 mL, FiO2 100%, pressure support 15 cm H2O, and PEEP of 12 cm H2O. His initial ABG in the ICU showed pH 7.6, PaCO2 25 mmHg, PaO2 165 mmHg, HCO3 27 mmol / L, and SpO2 99% (FiO2 of 80%). Appropriate ventilatorory adjustments were made. An ICU admission chest radiograph demonstrated bilateral patchy infiltrates, no pneumothoraces or effusions, and a normal heart size; these changes were central and peripheral and not in the dependent areas of the lung []. Over the subsequent 12 hours his FiO2 was weaned to 40%, and his ABG showed pH 7.45, PCO2 41 mmHg, PaO2 78 mmHg, HCO3 28 mmol / L, base excess 3.8, and oxygen saturation 96%. Postoperatively, during the first few days he was in positive fluid balance, however, he was in a negative fluid balance thereafter. His arterial line did demonstrate pulse wave variability.
The patient was taken for a planned re-debridement on postoperative day #2 and was not extubated until postoperative day #3. Shortly after extubation, he complained of acute shortness of breath, with an ABG showing pH 7.49, PCO2 39 mmHg, PaO2 62 mmHg, HCO3 29 mmol / L, and SpO2 93% (FiO2 of 80%). He was placed on intermittent continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP, 10 cm H2O) via full face mask. Although the chest radiogram showed an improvement from the index postoperative film, the patchy infiltrates persisted []. After ensuring adequate volume status, furosemide (40 mg intravenous) was administered, with resultant improvement in oxygenation, shortness of breath, and subsequent roentgenographic examination. The patient received two additional doses of furosemide (20 mg intravenous) over the next 24 hours, as determined on the basis of clinical re-evaluation. He was weaned off oxygen on postoperative day six as his chest radiogram showed near complete resolution of the pulmonary edema and his oxygenation was appropriate []. He was discharged from the hospital on postoperative day seven. The patient was doing well on follow-up at three months, and underwent uneventful skin grafting of the residual perineal wound without any general anesthesia-related complications.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 18)

args: A 68-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department after abruptly vomiting two cups of dark brown, coffee-ground material at home earlier in the afternoon and again in the triage bay shortly after arrival; she had not noticed any change in the color of her bowel movements over the previous week, and her daughter who assists with toileting corroborated that the stool in the commode this morning appeared its usual medium brown without an unusual odor. The patient described a dull, burning epigastric discomfort that had waxed and waned for three days and was transiently relieved by small sips of milk but recurred after she took her scheduled morning dose of low-dose aspirin for coronary protection. She denied lightheadedness when standing, blurred vision, or any episode in which she blacked out, though she acknowledged feeling somewhat more fatigued than usual while folding laundry yesterday. Past medical history is notable for ischemic cardiomyopathy with a last documented left ventricular ejection fraction of 30 % four months earlier, chronic New York Heart Association class II symptoms controlled with carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, and furosemide 20 mg every morning, as well as longstanding hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and osteoarthritis of the knees for which she intermittently takes over-the-counter ibuprofen when the weather turns cold. She has never undergone an upper endoscopy and has no known history of peptic ulcer disease or variceal bleeding. Surgical history includes cholecystectomy and cataract extraction. She quit smoking three decades ago and drinks a glass of wine only on holidays; she denies recreational drug use. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, ankle swelling greater than baseline, fever, night sweats, or recent weight loss. On examination she was alert, oriented, and conversant but appeared pale around the lips. Initial vitals in triage showed a blood pressure of 115 / 68 mm Hg measured manually with the patient seated, a regular pulse of 112 beats per minute, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, temperature 37.1 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. Jugular venous pulsation was not distended at 30 degrees, yet the carotid upstroke was slightly diminished. Cardiac auscultation revealed a normal S1 and S2 with a soft holosystolic murmur at the apex but no gallop; peripheral pulses were thready but symmetric without appreciable delay. Lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdominal examination demonstrated mild epigastric tenderness without rebound or guarding, normoactive bowel sounds, and no palpable masses or organomegaly; rectal examination produced scant brown stool that tested positive for occult blood on bedside guaiac. The legs showed trace pitting edema over the shins stable to the patient’s baseline according to her daughter. Point-of-care laboratory results drawn immediately after IV access revealed a hemoglobin concentration of 10.6 g/dL (baseline six months ago 12.8 g/dL), hematocrit 32 %, mean corpuscular volume 91 fL, white blood cell count 7.2 × 10³/µL, platelets 265 × 10³/µL, blood urea nitrogen 30 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.0 mg/dL; electrolytes were sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, and chloride 103 mEq/L. A bedside electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 110–115 beats per minute with nonspecific ST-T wave changes unchanged from a tracing performed in clinic last month. Upright chest radiograph showed a mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette consistent with her known ventricular dilation but clear lung fields. Prior to initiation of any resuscitative therapy she was placed on cardiac monitor, kept nil per os, and a type and screen was sent while arrangements for gastroenterology consultation were initiated to evaluate the likely source of this acute upper gastrointestinal hemorrhage.

CRITICAL HINT: For GBS, use BUN in mg/dL: 18.2-22.3=2 pts, 22.4-27.9=3 pts, 28-69.9=4 pts, >=70=6 pts. Hemoglobin in g/dL: Men 12.0-12.9=1 pt, 10.0-11.9=3 pts, <10.0=6 pts. Women 10.0-11.9=1 pt, <10.0=6 pts. Systolic BP: 100-109=1 pt, 90-99=2 pts, <90=3 pts. Heart rate >=100=1 pt. Melena=1 pt, Syncope=2 pts, Hepatic disease=2 pts, Cardiac failure=2 pts.

output.reasoning: Demographics: 68-year-old woman. BUN: 30 mg/dL (corresponds to 4 points based on hint 28-69.9). Hemoglobin: 10.6 g/dL (Women 10.0-11.9 = 1 point). Systolic BP: 115 mm Hg (does not meet criteria for >=1 pt). Heart rate: 112 bpm (>=100 = 1 point). Melena: Denied/absent ('medium brown' stool, 'scant brown stool' with positive guaiac does not equal melena). Syncope: Denied ('denied ... any episode in which she blacked out'). Hepatic disease: None mentioned. Cardiac failure: Present ('ischemic cardiomyopathy').

args: A 40-year-old woman with no prior episodes of decompensated liver disease but a long history of heavy ethanol consumption—described by family as two bottles of fortified wine daily with recent three-day binge on distilled liquor—was emergently transferred to our intensive care unit for escalating jaundice, progressive confusion, and oliguric renal failure that had required initiation of continuous veno-venous hemodialysis at the referring community hospital thirty-six hours earlier. According to the outside records, she had already undergone two separate sessions of intermittent hemodialysis earlier in the week when creatinine began to climb despite aggressive volume resuscitation, and the nephrology team had transitioned her to the continuous modality after blood pressure became labile. No vasopressors were being used at the time of transfer, but she remained connected to the CRRT circuit during transport through a right internal jugular double-lumen catheter. She had not been exposed to aminoglycosides, NSAIDs, or iodinated contrast, and there was no known history of chronic kidney disease prior to this admission. Seven days before arrival she had presented to the same outside facility with malaise, low-grade fever, abdominal discomfort, and new-onset scleral icterus. Work-up then revealed markedly elevated transaminases in the hundreds, an aspartate aminotransferase to alanine aminotransferase ratio greater than two, and a Maddrey discriminant function well above 32, leading the hepatology consultants to diagnose severe alcoholic hepatitis; corticosteroids were withheld because of concern for gastrointestinal bleeding risk given a markedly prolonged prothrombin time. Over the ensuing seventy-two hours she developed worsening ascites, low urine output, and grade II encephalopathy, prompting placement of a nasogastric tube that returned scant coffee-ground fluid but no brisk hemorrhage. She had never undergone transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt placement, abdominal surgery, or liver transplantation evaluation. Past medical history was notable only for hypertension treated sporadically with lisinopril and an episode of pancreatitis two years earlier. She lived alone, worked as a bartender, had no children, smoked half a pack of cigarettes daily, and denied illicit drug use. There was no family history of hereditary liver disorders. On arrival to our unit—just before the dialysis machine was momentarily paused for laboratory draws—vital signs were blood pressure 98/62 mm Hg, heart rate 104 beats/min, respiratory rate 18/min, temperature 37.1 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % on 2 L nasal cannula. She appeared deeply jaundiced with bright yellow conjunctivae, had stigmata of chronic alcohol use including parotid enlargement and palmar erythema, and was intermittently disoriented to date but followed simple commands. Asterixis was present. The abdomen was distended, tense, but non-tender with a prominent fluid wave; there were no surgical scars. Caput medusae were absent, though multiple spider telangiectasias peppered the upper chest. Cardiac examination revealed a regular tachycardia without murmurs, and chest auscultation was clear bilaterally. Extremities were cool with 1+ pedal edema. Rectal examination yielded brown heme-negative stool. Point-of-care ultrasound identified a nodular liver, moderate ascites, and kidneys of normal size and echogenicity. Prior to any additional therapy at our institution, the initial laboratory panel showed total bilirubin 10.2 mg/dL, direct fraction 7.4 mg/dL, albumin 2.3 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 158 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 72 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 196 U/L, venous ammonia 94 µmol/L, serum creatinine 2.3 mg/dL (baseline 0.8 mg/dL documented six months earlier), blood urea nitrogen 48 mg/dL, and an international normalized ratio of 3.3. Electrolytes at that same draw revealed sodium 129 mEq/L, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 20 mEq/L, and glucose 112 mg/dL. Hematologic indices included hemoglobin 8.9 g/dL, hematocrit 27 %, leukocytes 15.2 × 10³/µL with neutrophil predominance, and platelets 64 × 10³/µL. Arterial blood gas on supplemental oxygen demonstrated pH 7.31, pCO₂ 34 mm Hg, pO₂ 92 mm Hg, and serum lactate 4.0 mmol/L. Chest radiograph obtained in the resuscitation bay was clear, and electrocardiography showed sinus tachycardia without ischemia. The patient had not yet been given antibiotics, steroids, albumin, vasoactive medications, or additional blood products at the time these values were recorded. Gastroenterology and hepatology were immediately consulted to reassess candidacy for adjunctive therapies in severe alcoholic hepatitis and to discuss listing for transplantation in light of progressive hepatic synthetic failure, rising bilirubin, significant coagulopathy, worsening renal dysfunction necessitating continuous renal replacement therapy, and moderate hyponatremia.

output.reasoning: The patient is a 40-year-old woman. MELD-Na requires: Total Bilirubin (10.2 mg/dL), INR (3.3), Creatinine (2.3 mg/dL), Sodium (129 mEq/L). All are explicitly provided in the note. Dialysis status is confirmed as the patient is currently on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT).

args: A 72-year-old man presented to the pre-operative medicine service this morning for assessment before next month’s scheduled open repair of a 5.9-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm that will be approached through a transperitoneal midline incision with anticipated cross-clamp time of about an hour and an expected intensive care unit stay post-operatively. He arrived walking slowly with a rolling walker, stating that over the last year he can manage two flights of stairs if he pauses midway, but when he tries to mow half his lawn without stopping he develops mild ankle puffiness and needs to catch his breath; he sleeps on two pillows and gained three pounds of water weight during a recent weekend trip but responded promptly to an extra dose of his loop diuretic. The cardiology record documents a hospitalization ten months ago for acute decompensation marked by orthopnea and bibasilar crackles, during which a transthoracic echocardiogram showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30 % with diffuse hypokinesis. He has been stable since discharge on carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, sacubitril-valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, and furosemide 40 mg each morning; his last clinic note four weeks earlier described no paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea and only trace pedal edema. Coronary disease was first identified eight years ago after an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction treated with two drug-eluting stents to the right coronary artery; he completed cardiac rehabilitation, remains on dual antiplatelet therapy with low-dose aspirin and clopidogrel, and denies exertional chest pressure, jaw discomfort, presyncope, or palpitations since. A nuclear stress test performed six months ago to guide aneurysm surgery planning demonstrated a fixed inferolateral defect consistent with scar and no inducible ischemia at 7 metabolic equivalents; baseline ECG today again shows Q waves in leads III and aVF without acute ST-T changes. His medical history also includes long-standing hypertension, stage 4 chronic kidney disease attributed to ischemic nephropathy with a baseline serum creatinine of 2.0 mg/dL drawn this morning, dyslipidemia managed with high-intensity rosuvastatin, and diet-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus for which he takes metformin 500 mg twice daily but has never used injectable glucose-lowering agents. He has no personal history of stroke, transient focal neurologic events, or carotid intervention, and a carotid duplex obtained last year was unremarkable. Surgical history is limited to an uncomplicated laparoscopic appendectomy in his twenties. He quit smoking fifteen years ago following a 40-pack-year exposure, drinks a glass of bourbon on weekends, and does not use illicit substances. Current medications, in addition to those mentioned, include spironolactone 25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, lisinopril 10 mg each morning, omeprazole 20 mg for reflux, and cholecalciferol 1,000 IU daily; no drug allergies are reported. Family history is notable for a father who died of myocardial infarction at 60 and a sister with congestive heart failure, but no known aneurysms. Review of systems is negative for fever, weight loss, abdominal pain, claudication, syncope, or visual disturbances. On examination he appears euvolemic and in no distress. Vital signs seated: blood pressure 118/66 mm Hg in the right arm, heart rate 72 beats per minute and regular, respiratory rate 16 per minute, temperature 36.6 °C, and oxygen saturation 96 % breathing ambient air; body mass index is 27 kg/m². The jugular venous pulsation is measured at 7 cm above the sternal angle, carotid upstrokes are brisk without bruit, the point of maximal impulse is displaced laterally, and auscultation reveals a soft S3 with a grade 1/6 apical holosystolic murmur but no pericardial rub. Lungs are clear except for faint late inspiratory crackles at both bases. Abdomen is protuberant yet soft, revealing a pulsatile midline mass without tenderness; femoral and distal pulses are symmetrical. There is 1+ pitting edema at the ankles. Neurologic screening shows alert mentation, intact cranial nerves, 5/5 strength, normal sensation, and steady gait with the walker. Pre-operative laboratory results obtained an hour before the visit reveal sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.3 mmol/L, chloride 100 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 38 mg/dL, creatinine 2.0 mg/dL, hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, platelet count 210 × 10³/µL, and fasting glucose 108 mg/dL; liver function tests are within reference ranges and international normalized ratio is 1.1. Chest radiograph demonstrates mild cardiomegaly and clear lung fields. Airway evaluation notes a Mallampati class III oropharynx with good cervical extension and dentition intact. The vascular surgery team plans to continue the beta-blocker through the morning of surgery, hold the angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor on the day of operation to mitigate intra-operative hypotension, administer half the usual diuretic dose the evening before, and resume clopidogrel after epidural catheter removal on postoperative day three. He has been counseled extensively on smoking abstinence, incentive spirometry, renal protective strategies including pre-operative hydration, and the risks of cardiac decompensation, renal failure possibly necessitating dialysis, bleeding, graft infection, and lower-extremity ischemia. The patient voices understanding of the peri-operative plan and affirms his wish to proceed with definitive aneurysm repair.

output.reasoning: The Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) has six predictors. 1) High-risk surgery (e.g., vascular surgery including aortic surgery): Present (abdominal aortic aneurysm repair). 2) History of ischemic heart disease: Present (history of ST-elevation MI, stents). 3) History of congestive heart failure: Present (hospitalization for decompensated heart failure with LVEF 30%). 4) History of cerebrovascular disease (stroke or TIA): Absent (explicitly denies). 5) Preoperative insulin treatment for diabetes: Absent (diet-controlled, takes metformin). 6) Preoperative serum creatinine > 2.0 mg/dL: Absent (Creatinine is 2.0 mg/dL, which is not strictly greater than 2.0).

high_risk_surgery: Intraperitoneal, intrathoracic, or suprainguinal vascular surgery
ischemic_heart_disease: History of MI, positive stress test, angina, nitrate use, Q waves

args: A 66-year-old woman was seen this morning in the ambulatory anesthesia preparation suite five days before a scheduled laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy intended to aid weight reduction after years of diet- and exercise-resistant class III obesity; the bariatric team anticipates four trocar sites, an operative time of approximately ninety minutes, blood loss under 150 mL, and overnight observation on the general surgical ward with early ambulation and clear-liquid advancement. She rode the shuttle from the parking lot and walked the length of the lobby without stopping, though she acknowledged needing to slow her pace on inclined corridors because of mild breathlessness and occasional mid-foot swelling by evening. At home she cooks, gardens in raised planters for 20-minute stretches, and can climb a single flight of household stairs while holding the railing, pausing briefly on the landing to catch her breath but without chest tightness, palpitations, dizzy spells, or jaw discomfort. Ten months ago she was admitted for progressive ankle edema and nocturnal coughing; echocardiography at that time revealed a dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 38 percent and moderate functional mitral regurgitation. Since discharge she has taken carvedilol 12.5 mg twice daily, sacubitril-valsartan 49/51 mg each morning, and torsemide 10 mg most days; she weighs herself every sunrise, adheres to a 2-gram sodium regimen arranged by heart-failure nursing, sleeps on two pillows to avoid coughing fits, and has not required intravenous diuretics, emergency care, or oxygen therapy in the interim. She denies orthopnea when propped, paroxysmal nocturnal breathlessness over the last three months, or recent weight gain beyond the slow upward trend related to long-standing obesity. Type 2 diabetes mellitus was diagnosed eighteen years earlier; progressive fasting hyperglycemia prompted initiation of insulin four years ago, and she now injects insulin glargine 36 units at bedtime and uses insulin lispro before meals following a 1:10 carbohydrate ratio with a correction factor of one unit for every 30 mg/dL above 150. Capillary glucose logs reviewed today show pre-breakfast values typically 110–140 mg/dL and no readings below 70 within the past month; her most recent hemoglobin A1c, obtained by endocrinology five weeks ago, was 7.6 percent. Hypertension is managed with the sacubitril-valsartan noted above, and hyperlipidemia is treated with rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly; asthma, chronic lung disease, prior myocardial infarction, angina, revascularization, transient ischemic attack, stroke, carotid surgery, peripheral arterial claudication, kidney stones, or chronic liver disease are not part of her record. Surgical history includes laparoscopic cholecystectomy at age 42 without complications and two normal vaginal deliveries in her twenties. She quit cigarette smoking twenty years ago after a 15-pack-year exposure, consumes a glass of wine at holiday gatherings but otherwise abstains, and denies recreational drug use. Her father died of complications from congestive pump failure at 79, and her mother underwent a hip fracture repair at 84; no first-degree relatives experienced premature coronary or cerebrovascular events. Review of systems today is negative for fever, chills, night sweats, abdominal pain beyond occasional reflux, gastrointestinal bleeding, urinary complaints, or unexplained weight loss. After five minutes seated, vital signs in the right arm recorded blood pressure 118/72 mm Hg, regular radial pulse 64 beats per minute, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 97 percent breathing room air, oral temperature 36.8 °C, height 158 cm, weight 114 kg, and body mass index 45.7 kg/m². She appeared comfortable, conversant, and euvolemic. Jugular venous pulsation was estimated at 6 cm above the sternal angle with the head of bed at 45°, carotid upstrokes were brisk without bruit, and the apical impulse was felt in the sixth interspace just lateral to the mid-clavicular line but was not sustained. Cardiac auscultation revealed a soft third heart sound and a grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur best at the apex that diminished with handgrip, consistent with the known valve insufficiency; no pericardial rub or loud pulmonary component was appreciated. Breath sounds were vesicular throughout with faint bibasilar crackles that cleared after deep inspiration. The abdomen was round, soft, non-tender, and without hepato-splenomegaly or palpable masses; an old infra-umbilical scar from her cholecystectomy port sites was well healed. Extremities were warm with 2+ dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses and trace pitting to the mid-shins that did not rebound after two seconds. Neurologic screening revealed full orientation, clear speech, intact cranial nerves, 5/5 strength throughout, normal sensation, and a stable tandem gait. Laboratory tests drawn one hour before the visit showed hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 39 percent, platelet count 242 ×10⁹/L, white blood cells 6.8 ×10⁹/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 24 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.59 mg/dL, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 49 mL/min/1.73 m²; simultaneous fasting plasma glucose measured 132 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 26 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 23 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L, and international normalized ratio 1.0. A 12-lead electrocardiogram performed in clinic displayed sinus rhythm at 62 beats per minute, left-axis deviation, QRS duration 104 ms, and nonspecific ST-T wave flattening inferiorly, unchanged from a tracing six months earlier. Upright postero-anterior chest radiography showed mild cardiomegaly, prominent pulmonary vasculature, and clear lung fields without effusion. Airway assessment documented a Mallampati class III view, inter-incisor gap 3.8 cm, thyromental distance 6 cm, and full cervical extension. The peri-operative plan is to continue beta-blocker, sacubitril-valsartan, loop diuretic, and statin on the morning of surgery, reduce the basal insulin dose to 75 percent the night before, withhold short-acting insulin while fasting until point-of-care glucose measurements dictate, apply sequential compression devices in the operating suite, employ lung-protective ventilation, and guide crystalloids by stroke-volume variation to avoid volume overload. After an extensive discussion covering bleeding, pulmonary embolism, arrhythmia, renal injury, wound leak, and importance of postoperative incentive spirometry, the patient articulated her medication timing, demonstrated understanding of strict clear-liquid and carbohydrate-free intake after midnight, and expressed readiness to proceed with metabolic surgery.

output.reasoning: 1. High-risk surgery: The patient is undergoing laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy (intraperitoneal surgery). This counts as high-risk surgery (1 point). 2. History of ischemic heart disease: Patient has no history of MI, angina, or revascularization. 3. History of heart failure: Patient had a hospitalization 10 months ago for ankle edema/coughing, with echocardiogram showing dilated LV and EF 38% (HFrEF). This counts as a history of congestive heart failure (1 point). 4. History of cerebrovascular disease: No history of stroke or TIA. 5. Preoperative insulin treatment: Patient takes insulin glargine and insulin lispro (1 point). 6. Preoperative serum creatinine > 2.0 mg/dL: Patient creatinine is 1.59 mg/dL. This is not > 2.0 mg/dL. Scoring: High-risk surgery (1) + CHF (1) + Insulin (1) = 3 points.

congestive_heart_failure: History of CHF, pulmonary edema, PND, bilateral rales, S3, CXR with pulmonary edema
cerebrovascular_disease: History of stroke or TIA
insulin_diabetes: Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus
creatinine_elevated: Preoperative creatinine > 2.0 mg/dL

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: high_risk_surgery, history_congestive_heart_failure, insulin_dependent_diabetes
- Conditions ABSENT: history_ischemic_heart_disease, history_cerebrovascular_disease, preoperative_creatinine_greater_than_2_mg_dL
- Demographics: Age=66, Sex=female

args: A 67-year-old male with a background of insulin-treated type 2 diabetes mellitus, stage B chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and remote alcohol use disorder was brought by emergency medical services after his spouse found him progressively somnolent and dyspneic over the preceding 24 hours. According to prehospital personnel, he was already tachypneic with audible expiratory wheeze, required bag-mask assistance for oxygen saturations in the mid-80s, and became increasingly combative during transport, prompting rapid sequence intubation in the field. On arrival to the resuscitation bay he remained endotracheally intubated and was receiving volume-controlled ventilation with 60% inspired oxygen; an arterial blood gas obtained immediately after transfer confirmed a partial pressure of oxygen of 60 mm Hg, PCO2 of 52 mm Hg, pH 7.26, and bicarbonate 22 mmol/L. Mechanical settings had not yet been adjusted, and no non-invasive interface had been trialed prior to intubation. Vital signs prior to any pharmacologic support showed temperature 38.3 °C, heart rate 118 beats/min, respiratory rate set at 16 on the ventilator, and blood pressure 85/48 mm Hg with a mean of 60 mm Hg; his skin was cool and mottled, and there was no documentation of pressor infusion at that moment. Peripheral pulses were thready, capillary refill was delayed at four seconds, and jugular venous pressure was not elevated. Neurological examination demonstrated that he opened his eyes only to voice, uttered confused phrases without appropriate words once the endotracheal tube was briefly occluded for testing, and obeyed simple commands such as squeezing the examiner’s hand, yielding a Glasgow Coma Score of 10 prior to sedative administration in the department. Pupils were equal and reactive, deep tendon reflexes were symmetrical, and there were no lateralizing motor deficits. Bedside pulmonary auscultation detected diminished breath sounds bilaterally with coarse crackles in the dependent lung fields. Cardiac auscultation revealed a regular tachycardia without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The abdomen was mildly distended but soft, and there was tenderness in the right upper quadrant without rebound or guarding. Initial complete blood count demonstrated a platelet concentration of 160,000 per microliter, hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, and white blood cell count 14.9 K/µL with 12% bands. Comprehensive metabolic panel showed total bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL, direct fraction 1.8 mg/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 88 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 76 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 162 U/L, sodium 134 mEq/L, potassium 5.2 mEq/L, chloride 102 mEq/L, bicarbonate 21 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 46 mg/dL, and a creatinine of 2.8 mg/dL, which is markedly higher than his known baseline of 1.2 mg/dL from three months ago. Point-of-care lactate was 5.6 mmol/L. Bedside ultrasound of the inferior vena cava revealed a collapsible vessel suggestive of relative hypovolemia, and lung ultrasound corroborated bilateral B-lines consistent with interstitial syndrome. Foley catheterization performed on arrival yielded only 20 mL of concentrated amber urine; review of the nursing flow sheet indicated a total output of 350 mL over the past 24 hours according to records provided by the transferring paramedics, who had weighed his diapers during transport from a rural facility. Chest radiograph demonstrated diffuse patchy opacities predominantly in the lower lobes. Abdominal ultrasound later in the workup displayed a mildly enlarged, echogenic liver without biliary ductal dilation and normal splenic size. Blood cultures, sputum Gram stain, and legionella urine antigen were sent but pending at the time of this note. No vasopressors, antibiotics, or intravenous fluids had yet been initiated while laboratory studies were being verified, and the patient remained under synchronized mandatory ventilation through the endotracheal tube. Family reported no prior episodes of decompensated liver disease, and the last known drink was over a year ago. Medications at home included metformin, long-acting insulin glargine, tiotropium inhaler, and low-dose aspirin. There was no known allergy history. Social history was notable for a 45-pack-year smoking exposure, discontinued three years earlier. He had been independent in activities of daily living and lived in a single-story home without occupational exposures. The working differential at presentation encompasses severe community-acquired pneumonia leading to acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, septic shock, acute kidney injury likely multifactorial from sepsis and hypoperfusion, and possible cholestatic hepatitis from sepsis or drug toxicity. Early consultation with critical care, nephrology, and infectious diseases was requested while awaiting initial cultures and imaging to further direct management.

output.reasoning: Respiration: PaO2 60 mmHg, FiO2 60% (0.6). PaO2/FiO2 = 60/0.6 = 100. (SOFA pts: 3). Coagulation: Platelets 160,000/uL. (SOFA pts: 0). Liver: Bilirubin 3.2 mg/dL. (SOFA pts: 2). Cardiovascular: MAP 60 mmHg. No vasopressors. (SOFA pts: 1). CNS: GCS 10 (intubated, unable to assess eye/verbal correctly in standard fashion, but note states GCS 10). (SOFA pts: 2). Renal: Creatinine 2.8 mg/dL. Urine output 20 mL over arrival duration (documented 350 mL total over 24h). Based on creatinine > 2.0 (SOFA pts: 3).

args:
A 53-year-old Caucasian man was admitted to the emergency department (ED) with complaints of diarrhea of 3 days' duration, general weakness leading to multiple falls, abdominal pain with lack of appetite, dry cough, and dyspnea on exertion since the last 6 months. His medical past history included chronic alcoholism with 28 units of alcohol per week, active tobacco smoking (22,5 pack-years), sleep apnea syndrome treated with nocturnal continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) ventilation, high arterial blood pressure controlled by a daily dose of perindopril 5mg and bisoprolol 10 mg, and a morbid obesity with a body mass index (BMI) of 47,3 kg/m2. There was no history of abdominal surgery. Vital signs in the ED were arterial blood pressure 106/64 mmHg, sinus tachycardia with heart rate ranging from 104 to 144 bpm, respiratory rate 37/min, temperature 36.2°C, and oxygen peripheral saturation (SpO2) 92% at room air. Initial examination revealed crackles at the basis of the right lung and abdominal distension with decreased bowel sounds. There were no clinical signs of muscular injury.
The relevant laboratory investigations were the following: CRP 192 mg/L (< 5), platelet count 115.000 /μL, serum creatinine 4.48 mg/dL, BUN 94 mg/dL, sodium 126 mmol/L, and potassium 3mmol/L. A major rhabdomyolysis was early noted: CK 96,012 IU/L (< 397), troponin-I 103.3 ng/L (< 34.2), AST 818 IU/L (< 37), and LDH 2,960 IU/L (< 214). The CK level further increased to 120,059 IU/L 4 hours after hospital admission. Arterial blood gas analysis was consistent with a compensated metabolic acidosis: pH 7.44, pCO2 21.8 mmHg, pO2 79.7 mmHg, bicarbonate 18.6 mmol/L, base excess -7.2 mmol/L, and lactate level 2 mmol/L (< 1.6). Urine analysis revealed moderate proteinuria (++).
The electrocardiogram showed new onset atrial fibrillation (144/min). The diagnosis of left basal pneumonia with a contralateral pleural effusion was made on chest X-ray. Small and large bowel distension was evident from abdomen X-ray with some air fluid levels ().
The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU) for further management. He remained hypotensive (79/43 mmHg), with atrial fibrillation (166/min) and hypoxemia requiring nasal oxygen administration (5L/min). Initial therapy included fluid replacement, vasopressors, and potassium supplementation for persisting hypokalemia (2.9 mmol/L). Cefuroxime and clarithromycin were initially chosen for empirical antimicrobial therapy. No sputum was available for culture and blood cultures remained sterile. Urine Legionella testing returned positive for L. pneumophila serogroup 1 antigen and clarithromycin was continued alone.
Further clinical course was characterized by a progressive weaning of vasopressors and decrease of arterial lactate. Ileus persisted. Contrast enhanced abdominal computed tomography (CT) showed a diffuse distension of the large intestine and terminal ileum and a diffuse enhancement of the intestinal wall without evidence of bowel ischemia or stenosis (). Renal failure worsened and the patient became anuric on day 2 with serum creatinine 7.18 mg/dl and BUN 146 mg/dl. Intermittent hemodialysis was then started. The peak level of CK (202,000 IU/L) was reached on day 2.
Mechanical ventilation was ultimately required from hospital day 4 to 10 due to the progression of hypoxemia with hypercapnia; there was also a suspicion of inhalation pneumonia secondary to persisting ileus. During the period of mechanical ventilation, the patient did not receive opioid medications that could have resulted in a decreased intestinal motility.
Despite noncontributive abdominal CT findings, an explorative laparotomy was decided on day 12 that failed to demonstrate any mechanical occlusion.
Two weeks after ICU admission, patient was transferred to the nephrology ward with persisting ileus leading to a full month of parenteral nutrition. Antimicrobial therapy was stopped after 21 days. The patient was discharged home after two months. Hemodialysis was definitely stopped after 5 weeks, and renal function returned to normal values after 6 weeks.

output.reasoning: The CURB-65 score evaluates pneumonia severity based on five criteria (1 point each): C (Confusion), U (Urea > 19 mg/dL or > 7 mmol/L), R (Respiratory rate >= 30/min), B (Blood pressure: systolic < 90 mmHg or diastolic <= 60 mmHg), and 65 (Age >= 65). Analysis: 1) Confusion: Not explicitly mentioned in the text. Assumed absent (0 pts). 2) Urea (BUN): Admission BUN was 94 mg/dL (> 19 mg/dL). Present (1 pt). 3) Respiratory rate: Documented as 37/min (>= 30/min). Present (1 pt). 4) Blood pressure: Admission BP was 106/64 mmHg (neither systolic < 90 nor diastolic <= 60). Note: The patient became hypotensive later (79/43), but CURB-65 typically uses initial presentation. Based on initial ED presentation, BP criteria are not met. Absent (0 pts). 5) Age: 53 years old (< 65). Absent (0 pts).

A 60-year-old lady, a known case of type 2 diabetes mellitus for 15 years and hypertension for 3 years, presented to the clinic with fever, chills, and bilateral loin pain of 4 days duration. No other co-morbidities like chronic kidney disease or coronary artery disease were known in her in the past and she was apparently in normal state of health prior to the presenting illness.
She developed high-grade fever with chills and rigors. Fever was accompanied by dysuria and bilateral loin pain which was constant and aching in nature. Two days following the onset of fever and loin pain, she developed hematuria and subsequently her urine output started declining and she was anuric at the time of admission to the hospital. She also experienced severe generalized muscle ache. There was no arthralgia, skin rash or hemoptysis. Her medications included glipizide and multivitamin.
At the time of admission, she was conscious, irritable, febrile, and tachypneic. Her conjunctivae appeared suffused. There was no icterus, purpura, or lymphadenopathy. Muscle tenderness in limbs was noted. Her pulse was 104/min, BP–110/70 mm Hg, respiratory rate 23/min, temperature 101°F, oxygen saturation 95% at room air. Rales were appreciated in the basal regions of the chest. Examination of abdomen revealed no organomegaly, but both renal angles were tender on palpation.
Investigations revealed hemoglobin of 10.8 g/dL; leukocyte count of 28400 cells/μL; differential count revealed polymorphs 91%, lymphocytes 8%; platelet count 60000/μL. The blood film revealed normocytic normochromic RBCs, neutrophilic leucocytosis with toxic granulations. No hemoparasites were detectable. Urine analysis revealed 1+ protein, 40-50 WBCs/high power field, 20-25 RBCs/high power field, bile salts and pigments - negative, ketone-negative. Blood sugar on admission was 208 mg/dL; blood urea nitrogen 74 mg/ dL; serum creatinine 5.4 mg/dL; serum sodium 138 mmol/L; potassium 4.8 mmol/L; bicarbonate 7.0 mmol/L; chloride 104 mmol/L; plasma anion gap +27 mmol/L, pO290 mm Hg., pCO226 mm Hg. Serum total bilirubin measured 2.1 mg/dl; SGOT – 44 U/L; SGPT – 34 U/L; alkaline phosphatase – 242 U/L; serum total protein 6.5 g/L; and serum albumin measured 2.4 g/ dL. Urine culture revealed significant growth of E. Coli., (>105 colony forming units/ ml) sensitive to ciprofloxacin, amikacin, and ceftriaxone. However, blood culture grew no organism. Ultrasonogram of the abdomen revealed bilaterally enlarged kidneys with hydroureteronephrosis. No radioopaque lesion could be made out in the ultrasonogram or X-ray KUB region. An MR urogram performed to further evaluate the obstruction [] revealed bilateral renal papillary necrosis (RPN) with bilateral midureteric obstruction.
Thrombocytopenia and severe myalgia prompted a search for leptospirosis, a common cause of AKI in this part of the country. The organism could not be isolated from the urine or blood. However, the MAT (Microscopic Agglutination Test) done in the second week of the illness detected Leptospira icterohemorrhagica; serovar hardjo. Serologic investigations for dengue were negative.
She was subjected to a USG-guided renal biopsy which revealed acute tubular necrosis, interstitial inflammation, and mild mesangial proliferation [Figure –].
The patient was treated with hemodialysis and her antibiotics included benzyl penicillin and ceftriaxone. Gradually, the fever settled, her general condition stabilized, and renal function started to improve. By the 20th day of the illness, serum creatinine was 1.7 mg/ dL and platelet count was 190,000/μL and patient was discharged. Follow-up at 3 months revealed a serum creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

args:
A 38-year-old man presented with nausea, vomiting and loose stools of 2 days duration. Patient gave history of consumption of a tuber taken from the nearby forest with the intent of deliberate self-harm. Patient has consumed the tuber 6 days prior to the presentation. It was followed by nausea, vomiting, loose stools and haematochezia on 4th day. He also complained haematuria and bleeding from gums and rectum on 5th day. On 6th day he observed scalp hair fall, and easy pluckability of beard, moustache and body hair. He also complained oliguria on day 6. Patient's wife has brought the tuber. It was identified as G. superba. Patient revealed that he had consumed 250 g of the tuber along with water.
Patient had no history of any illness. He was apprehensive, with pulse rate of 120 bpm, blood pressure 70/40 mmHg, febrile, with congestion of conjunctivae. The pillow and bed were full of his hair. Patient himself demonstrated easy pluckability of hair. His investigations were, haemoglobin 6.0 g/dl, total leucocyte count 14,000/mm3, differential count: polymorphs: 60, lymphocytes 30, monocytes 5, eosinophils 5%, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 40 mm after first hour, platelet counts 0.24 lakhs/mm3, peripheral smear showed no abnormal cells and no features of haemolysis, there was thrombocytopenia, random blood glucose 120 mg/dl, serum creatinine 5.4 mg/dl, blood urea 188 mg/dl, serum sodium 127 mEq/L, serum potassium 3.9 mEq/L, serum chloride 88 mEq/L, serum bicarbonate 12.0 mmol/L, serum bilirubin 1.0 mg/dl, serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase 45 U/L, serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase: 65 U/L, serum alkaline phosphatase 125 U/L, serum proteins 6.9 g/dl, serum albumin 4.3 g/dl, serum creatinine kinase 65 IU/L (reference range: 40–200 IU/L), fractional excretion of sodium <1%, urine microscopy showed plenty of red blood cells and urine myoglobin was negative. Ultrasound abdomen revealed normal sized kidneys. Electrocardiogram showed T inversion in V4, V5, and V6. Chest radiograph was normal.
He was initiated on intravenous fluids, sodium bicarbonate supplementation and ionopressors. He was transfused with two units of whole blood. Blood pressure improved after 24 h. The bleeding manifestations subsided over next 72 h. Serum creatinine fell over next 6 days. Hair loss continued for next 10 days. He lost entire scalp hair. Patient regained scalp hair after 4 months.

A 52-year-old white male with O2-dependent COPD, hypertension, GERD, idiopathic gastroparesis, and chronic low back pain was noted to have persistent hypokalemia in the 2.7–3.3 meq/L range over more than 2 years. He complained also of chronic generalized weakness and fatigue. He denied nausea or vomiting, but did have occasional loose stools. The hypokalemia persisted despite discontinuation of diuretic treatment for hypertension and fludrocortisone that had been prescribed briefly for orthostatic hypotension. There was no improvement with aggressive oral potassium supplementation in amounts up to 120 meq per day. The patient's serum potassium level normalized on three occasions when he was hospitalized and given supplemental potassium (COPD exacerbations in 7/06 and 1/07, pseudoseizures in 7/07), but the hypokalemia promptly recurred after discharge from the hospital (Figure ). His medications were paroxetine, trazodone, pregabalin, sustained-release morphine, loratadine, isosorbide mononitrate, lisinopril, metoprolol, simvastatin, omeprazole, metoclopramide, potassium chloride, calcium/vitamin D tablets, alendronate, and mometasone, tiotropium, and albuterol inhalers. He smoked one-half pack of cigarettes per day and did not drink alcohol.
On physical examination, he was a chronically ill-appearing man wearing a nasal cannula. Height was 69 inches, weight 205 pounds. There were no cushingoid facies, buffalo hump, or abdominal striae. Vital signs were temperature 98.6 degrees, pulse 95, respiratory rate 14, blood pressure 128/73. There was no thyromegaly or lymphadenopathy. Lungs showed decreased breath sounds and mild expiratory wheezes bilaterally. Heart sounds were regular with no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The abdomen was soft and non-tender, with no masses or organomegaly. Extremities showed no edema, clubbing or cyanosis. The neurologic examination revealed mild generalized muscular weakness (4+/5) and normal deep tendon reflexes.
Laboratory results include serum sodium 137 mg/dL, potassium 3.0 mg/dL, chloride 95 mmol/L, CO2 30.0 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 5 mg/dL, creatinine 0.8 mg/dL, calcium 9.3 mg/dL, phosphorus 4.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.6 g/dL, ferritin 126 ng/mL, hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, white blood cell count 10.6 K/cmm, and platelet count 160 K/cmm. Serum aldosterone was 4.8 ng/dL (normal 4–31 ng/dL) and the plasma renin activity was 0.33 ng/mL/hr (normal 1.31–3.96 ng/mL/hr upright, 0.15–2.33 ng/mL/hr supine). Spot urine potassium was 8.6 mEq/L, urine sodium was < 10 mEq/L, and urine chloride was 16 mmol/L.
In the absence of a clear explanation for this patient's chronic hypokalemia, he was asked to give the details of his diet. He admitted to drinking 4 liters of Pepsi-Cola per day for the past several years. It was his habit to sip cola slowly but almost continuously, throughout the day. When hospitalized, he had stopped drinking cola and his potassium levels had temporarily normalized. In early May 2008, he decreased his cola intake to 2 liters per day, with a resultant increase in the serum potassium from 3.0 to 3.5 mg/dL (Figure ).

sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")

args: Along the medial aspect of the right calf and behind the knee a lacework of straight, bluish subcutaneous veins—appearing as recently recruited collateral channels rather than chronic varicosities—was readily visible when the leg was allowed to dangle for thirty seconds and emptied promptly on elevation; no similar network was seen on the left. … There is no cellulitic warmth, bursitis, muscle tear, tendon rupture, lymphedema, or Baker cyst fullness to account for the asymmetric calf girth, and given his prior venous event, the absence of any equally plausible alternative diagnosis, and the visibly engorged superficial collateral channels draining the affected area, expedited duplex ultrasonography of the entire right lower extremity venous system was ordered while the patient remains ambulatory in the assessment bay awaiting imaging.

output.reasoning: The patient presents with asymmetric calf swelling ('asymmetric calf girth') and visible collateral superficial veins. The note explicitly mentions a 'prior venous event' (previous DVT). The patient is ambulatory. Alternative diagnoses were considered (cellulitis, bursitis, etc.) and excluded ('no ... to account for the asymmetric calf girth'). No mention of active cancer, paralysis/paresis, bedridden status, or entire leg swelling is made, so these are treated as absent. As a male patient, gender is inferred. Age is not stated.

alternative_diagnosis: Alternative diagnosis at least as likely (-2)

args:
A 57-year-old man was admitted to the ED with complaints of syncope and weakness. We noticed that in his medical history, leeches were applied to treat the diabetic wounds on his feet two hours before he attended our clinic. After this, the leeches spontaneously detached but he observed bleeding from his bites and bleeding did not stop in spite of compression with tight bandages. A review of the patient’s past medical history revealed hypertension, diabetes mellitus, diabetic foot, ischemic heart disease and coronary artery bypass surgery.
Vital signs were as follows; fever: 36.6°C, blood pressure: 85/50 mmHg, heart rate: 112/min and respiratory rate: 28/min. Physical examination revealed tenderness in the whole abdomen. Ampulla was empty with rectal examination. There was an ulcerative lesion with size 5x3 cm on the site of the amputated first toe. Additionally, there were multiple bites due to leech therapy on both feet (Figure ).
Laboratory tests indicated the following: hemoglobin: 7.6 g/dL, white blood cells: 9370/mm3, platelets: 147000/mL, blood urea nitrogen: 29.1 mg/dL, creatinine: 1.98 mg/dL, glucose: 527 mg/dL, Na: 127 mmol/L, K: 6 mmol/L, CL: 94.5 mmol/L, C-reactive protein: 1.27 mg/dL and other routine blood tests and coagulation functions were all normal. Blood gas analysis revealed pH: 7.26, pO2: 86 mmHg, and bicarbonate: 20.2 mmol/L. Abdominal ultrasonography (USG) showed gas in the intrahepatic biliary ducts. No obstructive pathology was found to mesenteric blood flow on Doppler USG. CT without intravenous contrast was performed because of abnormal renal function tests. CT revealed gas near the stomach, among colon segments (pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis, PSI) and intrahepatic biliary ducts in central and left hepatic lobe (hepatic portal venous gas, HPVG) (Figure , Figure , respectively).
The patient was referred to another hospital that had an intensive care unit and started conservative treatment with antibiotics and intravenous fluid. Twelve hours after treatment, his general condition did not improve and signs of diffused peritonitis were observed on abdominal examination. The patient underwent exploratory laparotomy. Necrosis was found in the terminal ileum and the entire colon during laparotomy. Doppler USG detected low flow in the vessels supplying these necrotic segments. However, resection was decided due to necrosis of these segments. These necrotic intestinal segments were resected and an end-ileostomy was performed. He had an uneventful recovery and was discharged on the 17th postoperative day.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

args: A 64-year-old Caucasian female with a history of Noonan's syndrome presented with sudden onset of coffee ground emesis and without any history of black tarry stool. She was recently treated for severe erosive gastritis during her last hospital admission, and an endoscopy done during that admission suggested Los Angles Class C erosive gastritis with an otherwise normal stomach and duodenum. Her past medical history is significant for a history of hypercoagulability, bilateral lower extremity deep venous thromboses, extensive bilateral pulmonary embolism, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and dextrocardia. She complained of vague upper abdominal discomfort, and on further clinical examination, her epigastric region was soft, and distended and demonstrated no rebound. She was hemodynamically stable. Blood laboratory investigations revealed the following: white count 21.2 × 109/L (3.9–11.1), hemoglobin 14.9 gm/dL, hematocrit 43.7%, platelets 322,000, sodium 145 mEq/L, potassium 2.8 mEq/L, chloride 83 mEq/L, bicarbonate 49 mEq/L, BUN 32 mg/dL, creatinine 1.90 mg/dL, glucose 188 mg/dL, calcium 11.9 mg/dL, albumin 4.3 g/dL, total bilirubin 0.3 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase (ALT) 11 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase (AST) 27 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase of 80 U/L. The patient underwent a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis that showed marked gastric dilatation with extensive circumferential gas (pneumatosis) and portal venous gas suspicious for gastric ischemia. The likely etiology for these findings was the presence of gastric outlet obstruction (Figures , , , and ). On review of her history and comparing previous scans, there was no evidence of any prior hepatobiliary disease or prior endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography. An upper gastrointestinal endoscopy showed severe mucosal congestion, submucosal hemorrhage, and bluish and purple areas with ulcers in the gastric body. Unlike the prior endoscopy, now the pylorus was visible only on retroflexion, and the scope could not be advanced into the pylorus due to looping related to the abnormal anatomy. She underwent exploratory laparotomy and was found to have one thick anomalous congenital band with blood vessels in it that was found to be the cause of gastric outlet obstruction. A patent gastrojejunostomy was created to bypass the gastric outlet obstruction, along with a feeding jejunostomy. The patient tolerated the procedure well and her symptoms completely resolved without the need for gastric resection. She was discharged home on postoperative day six.

args:
A 23-yr-old woman with type 2 DM and Graves' disease has revisited to the Department of Emergency of Gyeongsang National University Hospital due to nausea, generalized weakness, polyuria and weight loss of 5 kg for 7 days in October 2003 since she was discharged from our hospital three weeks before. She was previously diagnosed as type 2 DM in 1996, and recently started insulin treatment to improve glycemic control due to persistently elevated HbA1c irrespective of oral administration of hypoglycemic agents. Three years before, she had taken propylthiouracil for hyperthyroid Graves' disease for 2 yr. In May 2003, she was diagnosed as relapse of hyperthyroidism at a local clinic and referred to our hospital for management of thyrotoxicosis refractory to usual dosage of propylthiouracil, 300 mg/day and proliferative diabetic retinopathy and nephropathy. She had 7.9 mM/L fasting blood glucose, 1.07 nM/L C-peptide (reference range [RR], 0.1-0.83), 222 mM/L fructosamine (RR, 205-285), 8.3% HbA1c (RR, 4.5-5.6) and 0.01 mU/L TSH and 2.02 pM/L free T3 on thryoid function tests. At that time, Her weight was 54 kg and height was 147 cm (body mass index, 25.0 kg/m2). Her thyroid had the volume of about 40 g and it looked diffusely enlarged and rubbery consistency on palpation. The technetium-99m (Tc-99m) thyroid scan revealed marginally increased and somewhat irregular uptake (3.8%). As from hospitalization, the patient had oral administration of relatively high dose of methimazole (60 mg daily), propranolol (160 mg daily) and 12 drops of Lugol's solution (three times a day) for 14 days, and consecutively lithium carbonate (900 mg daily) for 14 days because of persisting tachycardia (>110/ min), dyspnea on exertion and still high concentration of free T3 (4.44 pM/L). She was discharged with receiving methimazole (60 mg daily) and beta-blocker ().
She had no family history of thyroid disease, but both parents have been suffering from type 2 DM. She denied ingestion of any drugs including alcohol or excessive consumption of fizzy drinks these days. She also has been adhering to administration of antithyroid drug, beta-blocker and insulin (0.5 units/kg/day) since being discharged. On physical examination, the patient had 143/89 mmHg blood pressure, 36.8℃ body temperature, 22/min respiration rate, and 121/min pulse rate. She looked acutely ill, and had slightly tremulous, warmhands, and decreased skin turgor and dried tongue. She was 48 kg weight and body mass index 22.2 kg/m2. Exophthalmos was not present. The abdomen was soft and flat, and liver and spleen were not palpable.
On admission, her laboratory findings were as follows; hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, WBC 8.65×109/L (segmented neutrophil 58%, lymphocyte 29%), platelet 398×109/L, total protein 65 g/L, albumin 32 g/L, ALP 3.47 ukat/L, AST 0.42 ukat/L, ALT 0.6 ukat/L, BUN 9.2 mM/L and creatinine 88.7 µM/L. Levels of blood glucose (32.7 mM/L) and fructosamine (396 mM/L) were elevated. Her serum electrolytes and blood gas parameters were as follows: sodium 144.1 mM/L, potassium 5.4 mM/L, chloride 102 mM/L, calcium 2.5 mM/L, phosphorus 1.0 mM/L, pH 7.32, pCO2 35 mmHg, pO2 98 mmHg, and bicarbonate 16 mM/L. The fasting serum C-peptide was 0.44 nM/L. Serum anion gap was 20.9 and osmolarity was calculated as 321 mosm/kg. Amylase and lipase levels were normal and anti-GAD antibody was negative. Thyroid function tests revealed that the patient had more aggravated hyperthyroid state; 0.01 mU/L TSH, 2.78 pM/L free T3 and 45.38 pM/L free T4 (RR, 11.41-23.08) than when she was hospitalized two weeks before (0.12 mU/L TSH, 1.41 pM/L free T3 and 30.26 pM/L free T4). TSH-receptor antibody level was 26.3 IU/L (RR, <1) and antimicrosomal antibody was 29.3 U/mL (RR, <60). The Tc-99m scan showed still enlarged thyroid with more increased uptake 6.5% than previous uptake scan (3.8%) performed taking propylthiouracil two months before. Based on above results, the patient was diagnosed as HHS accompanying mild ketosis on type 2 DM and aggravated Graves' hyperthyroidism. We treated her with intravenous insulin injection and large volume of intravenous fluid administration. And also, she took high dose of methimazole (90 mg daily, six times a day) and beta-blockers. On the second day, nausea and generalized weakness have been improved.
At two months after acute presentation, we measured levels of C-peptide after 75 g glucose loading; 0 min (0.12 nM/L), 30 min (0.74 nM/L), 60 min (1.02 nM/L), 90 min (1.49 nM/L) and 120 min (1.32 nML). Throughout follow-ups for 2 months, she took insulin (0.9 units/kg/day), metformin (1,500 mg daily) and methimazole (45 mg daily). The mean fasting capillary blood glucose level on self monitoring was rather elevated than the target, but free T3 level became normalized ().

args: A 31-year-old man with acute on chronic liver failure secondary to alcohol was emergently admitted to our hospital for evaluation as a candidate for LDLT. On admission, his model for end-stage liver disease score was 22, and his mother, aged 52 years, was the living donor candidate. Her height and body weight were 156 cm and 51 kg, respectively, and the recipient's height and body weight were 157 cm and 52 kg, respectively. Multidetector row CT was used for preoperative dynamic CT. Figure shows a simulation of the donor hepatectomy, which was performed using the SYNAPSE VINCENT three-dimensional CT software (Fujifilm). The predicted right- and left-lobe volume was 635 and 379 mL, respectively. The right-lobe graft was sufficiently large for the recipient, with a graft–recipient weight ratio of 1.22%, while the left-lobe graft was small, with a ratio of 0.72%. Three-dimensional CT angiography revealed that the anterior and posterior arteries originated separately from the proper hepatic artery (Figure ). It was impossible to procure the right-lobe graft with single right hepatic artery because the anterior and posterior hepatic arteries encircled the common hepatic duct. Moreover, because the anterior artery travelled a deep roundabout route toward the infundibulum of the gall bladder and there was a severe adhesion around the anterior artery, we could not separate the anterior hepatic artery from the cystic artery (Figure ). Even if we could have separated the anterior artery from the cystic artery, we considered that the graft artery of the anterior artery was redundant and inappropriate for the reconstruction. Therefore, we planned to divide the anterior artery and to anastomose each anterior artery stump to avoid redundancy, using the surgical microscope, during back-table angioplasty (Figure , ). Figure shows the microsurgical angioplasty of the anterior artery and the anastomosis of the recipient's left hepatic artery. It took 9 minutes for ex vivo- and 35 minutes for ex vivo hepatic artery reconstruction. Total arterial reconstruction time was 44 minutes. Flow in the reconstructed artery was 56 mL/min, and the flow shapes in the anterior and posterior artery were confirmed as good using Doppler ultrasonography.

args: A 72-year-old woman with longstanding osteoporosis, diet-controlled type 2 diabetes, and stage 2 chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.4 mg/dL six months ago) was brought to the emergency department from an assisted-living facility after a day of fever, rigors, and progressively labored breathing that began shortly after she complained of left-flank pain and dysuria; staff noted that over the prior 24 hours she had voided only a single small cup of dark urine despite normal fluid intake. En-route, paramedics found her sitting bolt upright, clutching her costovertebral angle, speaking in full sentences but visibly air-hungry with a respiratory rate in the low thirties and an oxygen saturation of 82 % on ambient air. They applied a tight-fitting mask attached to a portable blower delivering continuous distending pressure of 10 cm H₂O blended with supplemental oxygen dialed to 0.51; within minutes the saturation climbed into the low nineties, and she continued to breathe spontaneously through that circuit for the remainder of the ten-minute transport. No sedatives, antimicrobials, intravenous fluids, vasopressors, or analgesics were given in the field. On arrival—prior to any hospital-initiated therapy—she remained seated forward on the same noninvasive interface, alert and oriented, answering questions briskly and following commands without hesitation; pupils were equal and brisk, speech was clear, and there were no focal neurologic deficits, yielding a Glasgow Coma Score of 15. Immediate vital signs obtained via automated cuff and confirmed by right radial arterial line placed within three minutes showed blood pressure 93/53 mm Hg, heart rate 118 beats per minute in sinus rhythm, respiratory rate matching 30 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation oscillating between 90 % and 92 % on the documented 51 % inspired oxygen, and oral temperature 38.6 °C. An arterial blood gas drawn concurrently with line placement revealed pH 7.28, PaCO₂ 32 mm Hg, bicarbonate 14 mEq/L, lactate 4.2 mmol/L, and a PaO₂ of 91 mm Hg while the mask remained undisturbed. Skin examination demonstrated flushed cheeks with a faint petechial dusting over the forearms but no peripheral cyanosis; capillary refill was approximately three seconds. Cardiopulmonary exam disclosed tachycardia without murmurs, diffuse fine crackles in the lower two-thirds of both lung fields, and equal chest rise around the mask seal. Abdomen was soft yet elicited marked tenderness to percussion over the left costovertebral angle; no guarding or rebound was present, but a ballotable left kidney was suspected. There was no scleral icterus, though the conjunctivae appeared subicteric on close inspection. Extremities were warm with trace dependent edema. A 16-F Foley catheter inserted during secondary survey issued 40 mL of cloudy, foul-smelling urine immediately; the facility’s intake-output chart faxed with the patient documented a cumulative 530 mL over the preceding day. Initial laboratory studies drawn simultaneously with the arterial sample showed white-blood-cell count 22.4 × 10³/µL with 14 % bands, hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, hematocrit 39 %, and platelet count 136 000 /µL. Basic metabolic panel returned sodium 131 mmol/L, potassium 5.1 mmol/L, chloride 98 mmol/L, bicarbonate aligning with the arterial value, blood urea nitrogen 68 mg/dL, and creatinine markedly elevated at 3.3 mg/dL relative to her baseline. Hepatic panel demonstrated total bilirubin 3.0 mg/dL (direct 2.2 mg/dL), aspartate aminotransferase 91 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 77 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 182 U/L, and an international normalized ratio of 1.4. High-sensitivity troponin was undetectable. Urinalysis obtained from the catheter showed 3+ leukocyte esterase, numerous white cells, many bacteria, and moderate bilirubin; point-of-care ultrasound performed before any antimicrobial or fluid therapy revealed a dilated left renal pelvis with echogenic debris and absent ureteric jet on that side, a small collapsible inferior vena cava, and hyperdynamic left-ventricular contractility without segmental wall-motion abnormalities. Portable chest radiograph taken while she remained on the same positive-pressure mask showed mild bilateral perihilar infiltrates without frank consolidation or effusion and no evidence of endotracheal hardware. Non-contrast computed tomography of the abdomen and pelvis, obtained expeditiously but prior to any treatment initiation, displayed a 1.1-cm obstructing calculus in the proximal left ureter with moderate upstream hydronephrosis and perinephric stranding, findings consistent with obstructive pyelonephritis. Electrocardiography demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 120 beats per minute with a normal axis and no ischemic changes. Up to the moment all aforementioned physiologic and biochemical data were captured, she had received neither crystalloid bolus nor vasopressor infusion, no empiric antibiotics or antipyretics, and remained breathing spontaneously through the non-invasive circuit delivering 51 % inspired oxygen and continuous distending pressure, maintaining her own airway without endotracheal instrumentation but still exhibiting relative hypotension, modest thrombocytopenia, marked hyperbilirubinemia, severe azotemia with significant oliguria, and a measured arterial oxygen tension of 91 mm Hg under those baseline conditions.

output.reasoning: The SOFA score evaluates organ dysfunction in six categories. 1. Respiration: PaO2 is 91 mm Hg on an FiO2 of 0.51 (51%). The PaO2/FiO2 ratio is 91 / 0.51 ≈ 178 (Score 3). 2. Coagulation: Platelet count is 136,000 /µL (Score 0). 3. Liver: Bilirubin is 3.0 mg/dL (Score 2). 4. Cardiovascular: The patient is hypotensive (BP 93/53 mm Hg, MAP ≈ 66 mm Hg). No vasopressors are required as per the prompt (Score 1). 5. CNS: GCS is 15 (Score 0). 6. Renal: Creatinine is 3.3 mg/dL and urine output is low (40 mL + 530 mL/day < 500 mL/day, making it oliguric). Creatinine 3.3 implies a score of at least 3, but the oliguria confirms significant renal failure (Score 3 or 4 depending on strict definitions, here creatinine > 3.5 is 4, so 3.3 is likely 3).

pao2_fio2_ratio: PaO2/FiO2 ratio (e.g., 350)
platelets: Platelet count in 10^9/L (e.g., 150)
bilirubin: Total bilirubin in mg/dL (e.g., 1.0)
map_or_vasopressors: 0=MAP>=70, 1=MAP<70, 2=low dose dopamine, 3=high dose dopamine, 4=norepinephrine
gcs: Glasgow Coma Scale (3-15)
creatinine: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: hypoxemia_pao2_fio2_ratio_100_200, liver_dysfunction_bilirubin_2.0_5.9, hypotension_map_lt_70, renal_dysfunction_creatinine_3.5_or_oliguria
- Conditions ABSENT: coagulopathy_platelets_lt_150k, gcs_less_than_15, need_for_vasopressors
- Demographics: Age=72, Sex=female

args:
A 31-year-old woman was diagnosed with hypertension by a private clinic in August 2013, based upon a systolic blood pressure of 160 mm Hg and she was prescribed antihypertensive drugs to treat the condition. Initial therapy consisted of hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg and losartan 50 mg daily. After 1 week, her blood pressure was still high, so the doctor changed her medication to amlodipine 5 mg, losartan 100 mg, and indapamide 2.5 mg per day. Five days after initiating this second regimen of drugs, she developed pretibial pitting edema, and thorough workup was done for the symptom. There were bilateral adrenal masses on abdominal-pelvic computed tomography (CT). Routine blood tests revealed the following: potassium 2.2 mmol/L, plasma renin activity (PRA) 0.12 ng/mL/hr, plasma aldosterone concentration (PAC) 16.76 ng/dL, and an aldosterone/renin ratio (PAC/PRA) >30, indicative of primary hyperaldosteronism.
Following her possible diagnosis of primary hyperaldosteronism, the patient was referred to our hospital for additional testing. At the time of admission, her blood pressure was 155/111 mm Hg, height was 164.5 cm, and weight was 63.5 kg (body mass index, 23.47 kg/m2). Pretibial pitting edemas were detected, but no physical signs of Cushing syndrome were observed.
A routine blood test at the time of admission showed the following: white blood cell 6,900/µL, hemoglobin 13.5 g/dL, platelet 351,000/µL, total protein 6.9 g/dL, albumin 4.1 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 76 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 126 U/L, blood urea nitrogen 7.3 mg/dL, creatinine 0.71 mg/dL, sodium 141 mmol/L, potassium 2.9 mmol/L, chloride 101 mmol/L, total cholesterol 210 mg/dL, triglyceride 104 mg/dL, high density lipoprotein 50 mg/dL, low density lipoprotein 141 mg/dL, and hemoglobin A1c 5.9%. Biochemical evaluation of pheochromocytoma showed no excessive catecholamine production. The transtubular potassium concentration gradient was 12.64%, suggesting the possibility of hyperaldosteronism or hypercortisolism. PAC and PRA were 24.4 ng/dL and 0.76 ng/mL/hr, respectively, and the PAC/PRA ratio was 32.11 (). After correcting for hypokalemia, a saline loading test was performed, which revealed no suppression of the aldosterone level after saline administration, consistent with a diagnosis of hyperaldosteronism (the basal PAC 24.4 ng/dL increased to 27.3 ng/dL after a 2 L saline infusion). Dexamethasone suppression tests (DSTs) resulted in no suppression of cortisol levels (). Adrenal CT demonstrated two bilateral adrenal masses in each of the adrenal glands, including a 1.6-cm mass on the right and a 2-cm mass on the left adrenal gland (). An adrenal cortical scan using iodine-131-6β-iodomethyl-19-norcholesterol (NP-59) showed focally increased radioactive uptakes in the bilateral adrenal gland areas, with a substantially higher uptake on the left side ().
Bilateral adrenal venous sampling (AVS) with adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) stimulation () revealed inadequate right adrenal venous blood sampling (right adrenal vein cortisol level/peripheral blood cortisol level [CRt/CP] <10), whereas the cortisol concentration in the left adrenal venous blood showed successful sampling on the left side. One explanation for this difference may be that the low level of CRt was the result of suppressed cortisol secretion due to excessive cortisol secretion from the left adrenal mass. The left adrenal vein aldosterone/cortisol (ALt/CLt) ratio was lower than that of peripheral blood, implying the possibility of suppressed aldosterone secretion from this side caused by a contralateral adrenal mass which might secrete excessive aldosterone []. Four days after the first AVS, a second AVS was performed using low-dose DST but without ACTH infusion (). This time, we also measured the epinephrine levels in the both adrenal veins and peripheral blood; a large peripheral-to-adrenal gradient of epinephrine assured successful catheterization on both sides []. CLt/CP testing confirmed that the left adrenal mass was a cortisol-secreting adenoma [].
Even though those two AVS results appeared to provide clear evidence of the lateralization of functioning adrenal masses, such an independent functioning of bilateral masses was highly suspicious. We therefore concluded that sequential adrenalectomies, rather than concurrent operations, would be beneficial for making a definitive diagnosis. Moreover, the patient agreed to do sequential operations. The first procedure was a laparoscopic partial adrenalectomy of the right adrenal gland to remove the putative aldosterone-producing mass, with the goal of controlling hypertension and hypokalemia. A firm, 1.5-cm ovoid, well-circumscribed, bright yellow tumor mass lacking necrosis or hemorrhage was arising from the cortex of the adrenal gland in the pathologic specimen. Within 24 hours after surgery, the patient's blood pressure had fallen to 110/80 mm Hg without antihypertensive medication, serum potassium normalized at 4.5 mmol/L, and PAC decreased to 3 ng/dL. These large postoperative changes strongly suggested that the right adrenal adenoma was producing excessive aldosterone. By day 3, an overnight DST continued to show no cortisol suppression, indicating persistent secretion of corticosteroid from the left adrenal mass. To limit the likelihood of subclinical Cushing syndrome progressing to overt Cushing syndrome, we opted to completely remove the left adrenal gland as a means of controlling hypercortisolism. The excised portion of the adrenal mass contained an encapsulated brown solid mass.
The patient began taking prednisolone 10 mg once a day for steroid replacement 1 day after the second surgery. By 2 months she had lost 5 kg of body weight. At that time, a rapid ACTH test revealed adrenal insufficiency (). At 16 months after surgery, the patient has gradually reduced her prednisolone intake to 5 mg every other day. Her blood pressure and serum potassium levels have remained within normal ranges, without the need for additional antihypertensive medications or potassium supplements.

sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
height_cm: Patient height in centimeters (e.g., 170.0)
