args:
A 26-year-old man was admitted with known hypokalemia. He reported a 3-year history of nausea, vomiting, and diffuse muscle weakness. He complained of nausea and epigastric fullness in the absence of any identifiable organic gastrointestinal pathology, and his symptoms were known to subside after self-induced vomiting. Although he reported a normal appetite, he was known to vomit frequently, particularly after a large meal. He had been diagnosed with hypokalemia and had been treated with potassium supplements at a previous hospital. However, because of persistent nausea and vomiting, he was transferred to our hospital for evaluation of hypokalemia. His medical and family histories were unremarkable, and he did not report the use of medications such as diuretics and/or herbal medication. Physical examination upon admission showed he was 175 cm tall and weighed 44.5 kg, which indicated a weight loss from his previous weight of 58 kg over the previous 2 years. His body mass index was 14.53 kg/m2. Blood pressure was 100/60mmHg, pulse rate was 67/min, respiratory rate was 20/min, and his body temperature was 37.0℃.
Initial serum electrolyte testing showed the following: sodium 133 mEq/L, potassium 2.6 mEq/L, chloride 72 mEq/L, and total carbon dioxide 50mEq/L. Calcium/phosphorus were 11.2/4.1mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen/creatinine were 21.9/1.98mg/dL, the estimated glomerular filtration rate was 43.6mL/min/1.73m2, serum magnesium was 2.0mg/dL, blood glucose was 93mg/dL, and hemoglobin was 12.0 g/dL. Total cholesterol was 285mg/dL, serum protein/albumin were 8.1/5.3 g/dL. Thyroid and adrenal function tests, as well as serum renin and aldosterone levels were all within reference range. Arterial blood gas analysis showed pH 7.557, partial pressure of carbon dioxide was 65.8mmHg, and bicarbonate was 58.5 mEq/L. Urinary levels of sodium/potassium/chloride (Na/K/Cl) were 95/73.2/47mEq/L, respectively, his urinary creatinine measured 111.12mg/dL, and the trans-tubular potassium gradient was 17. Urinalysis showed the following findings: blood -, albumin ±, glucose -, and ketones -. Upper endoscopy and abdominal computed tomography showed no abnormalities. The SLC12A3 gene mutation test for Gitelman syndrome was observed to be negative. He was diagnosed with chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis with kidney failure and treated with saline hydration, potassium supplementation, and spironolactone. Serum potassium increased to 3.0mEq/L; however, his nausea and vomiting persisted.
A renal biopsy was performed for the evaluation of renal dysfunction. Microscopically, interstitial mononuclear cell infiltration was identified. Renal tubular epithelial cells showed mild nuclear variation with conspicuous nucleoli and cytoplasmic vacuolization, indicating regenerative changes after acute tubular necrosis. Several foci of dystrophic calcification were identified among the tubular epithelial cells. A few shrunken glomeruli were observed suggesting ischemic damage; however, most glomeruli revealed no definitive abnormalities such as mesangial cell proliferation, segmental sclerosis, and/or deposition of immune complexes (). The patient showed an improvement in his gastrointestinal symptoms after treatment with mirtazapine. His serum potassium level increased to 3.5–4.4mEq/L and to date, he has been following-up at the outpatient clinic without any clinical signs and symptoms.

args: A 56-year-old man arrived to the early-evening internal-medicine session for an employer-sponsored cardiovascular wellness review after his company’s onsite screen flagged “borderline numbers” and suggested formal follow-up; he walked from the parking lot without breathlessness, set a motorcycle helmet on the chair, and explained that he hopes to qualify for a senior field-supervisor role that entails frequent climbs up refinery stairwells and transstate driving but wants reassurance that “the heart is holding up.” The chart documented a ten-year history of elevated arterial pressure first discovered during a worksite biometric fair; since then his primary clinician had titrated once-daily lisinopril 20 mg to 40 mg and recently added chlorthalidone 12.5 mg, prescriptions he fills monthly at the union pharmacy and usually takes with breakfast beside an over-the-counter joint supplement. He has never been hospitalized for chest pain, revascularization, stroke, transient ischemic attack, heart failure, or peripheral arterial disease, and no provider has ever labeled him pre-diabetic or diabetic—his last fasting plasma glucose eight months ago read 93 mg/dL according to the documentation he brought. Surgical record lists only an appendectomy in his twenties and extraction of two impacted wisdom teeth. Family history reveals that his father died suddenly at 68 from an automobile accident but carried long-standing dyslipidemia, while his mother, now 80, maintains controlled hypothyroidism; no first-degree relative suffered a myocardial infarction or cerebrovascular accident before age 60. He admitted that a half-pack of filtered cigarettes accompanies the morning thermos of coffee and another half-pack usually disappears during evening television, a habit begun in the military and tempered only briefly when his first child was born; vaping devices, smokeless tobacco, and recreational drugs are disavowed. Alcohol consumption is modest—two bottles of light beer after weekend yardwork—and he relies on one large energy drink for midshift alertness when supervising night turnarounds. Diet favors convenience: sausage biscuits from a drive-through at dawn, cafeteria cheeseburgers smothered in pickles and mustard at noon, and reheated take-out fried rice if his spouse is working late; he acknowledges that vegetables often appear only as garnish and sodium content is likely high. He lifts free weights twice weekly in the company gym and walks a Labrador around the block most evenings, achieving perhaps 90 minutes of moderate activity each week but no structured aerobic program. Review of systems denied exertional substernal pressure, arm or jaw radiation, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, presyncope, calf pain with walking, visual disturbances, polyuria, polydipsia, night sweats, or unintentional weight shift. On examination he appeared muscular with a faint odor of tobacco, height 178 cm, weight 91 kg, body-mass index 28.7 kg/m². After five minutes seated with feet flat, back supported, and the right arm at heart level, an aneroid sphygmomanometer read 142/86 mm Hg; a repeat on the left confirmed the same systolic value. Radial pulse was regular at 72 beats per minute, respirations 14/min, oral temperature 36.7 °C, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air. Carotid upstrokes were brisk without bruit, jugular venous waveform was 6 cm H₂O, and the conjunctivae showed no pallor. No corneal arcus, xanthelasma, or tendon xanthomata were seen. The point of maximal impulse was at the fifth intercostal space, mid-clavicular line, nondisplaced; S₁ and S₂ were normal with no gallops, murmurs, or friction rubs. Lungs were clear to percussion and auscultation. Abdomen was soft and nontender with no aortic pulsatile mass; femoral, popliteal, posterior tibial, and dorsalis pedis pulses were 2+ and symmetric without bruits, edema, or skin discoloration. Neurologic screen showed intact cranial nerves, full muscle strength, normal sensation, and coordinated finger-to-nose testing. Skin inspection revealed scattered solar lentigines on the forearms but no ulcerations or lipid-laden nodules. A fasting blood panel drawn that morning in the occupational clinic reported total cholesterol 223 mg/dL, high-density lipoprotein cholesterol 41 mg/dL, calculated low-density lipoprotein 149 mg/dL, triglycerides 176 mg/dL, fasting plasma glucose 92 mg/dL, serum creatinine 1.0 mg/dL with estimated glomerular filtration rate 88 mL/min/1.73 m², sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, bicarbonate 23 mmol/L, alanine aminotransferase 27 U/L, and thyroid-stimulating hormone 2.4 µIU/mL; complete blood count was unremarkable. A resting 12-lead electrocardiogram performed in clinic displayed normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats per minute, PR interval 154 ms, QRS width 92 ms, borderline voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy but no pathologic Q waves, ST-segment shifts, or T-wave inversions. After results were reviewed, he voiced mild surprise that the cholesterol level had crept above prior numbers and acknowledged that sporadic missed doses of the blood-pressure tablets coincide with overtime weeks. The discussion centered on re-emphasizing daily adherence to his current antihypertensive regimen, exploring the possibility of adding an evening HMG-CoA reductase inhibitor given the elevated total and low protective fraction, setting a tobacco-quit date supported by prescription nicotine replacement or varenicline, substituting oatmeal and fruit for breakfast sandwiches, and scheduling a repeat lipid profile in three months to gauge response; he agreed to begin a mobile-app blood-pressure diary and will return for follow-up before the refinery turnaround season begins.

args: A 28-year-old woman arrived to the ambulatory surgical center this morning for pre-procedure evaluation prior to a planned hysteroscopic resection of a 1.5 cm endometrial polyp discovered during infertility work-up; the gynecology team anticipates a brief operative time under monitored anesthesia care with same-day discharge, and the procedure is regarded as a minor, low-risk operation. She reports feeling well and maintains her usual weekday routine, which includes walking her dog two miles each morning and taking the stairs to her third-floor apartment without limitation. She has not been hospitalized or operated on in the past year and has had no casts, fractures, or traumatic injuries; the only surgeries in her history are a tonsillectomy at age five and wisdom-tooth extraction during college, both uncomplicated. She has had no episodes of sepsis, pneumonia, congestive heart failure, stroke, multiple trauma, or acute spinal cord injury within the past month, and she has not undergone any major surgical procedure during that time frame. Review of systems today is largely unremarkable. She denies orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, exertional chest pain, palpitations, lower-leg edema, chronic cough, recent fever, productive sputum, or shortness of breath. Bowel habits are stable; she was diagnosed with mildly active ileocolic Crohn’s disease six years ago after colonoscopy and biopsy, managed with mesalamine 2.4 g daily and one flare requiring outpatient prednisone taper two years ago. She has been symptom-free since and follows routinely with gastroenterology. No history exists of myocardial infarction, chronic lung disease, malignancy, or connective-tissue disorders. She has never experienced calf tenderness, limb swelling, or pleuritic chest discomfort suggestive of clot, and duplex ultrasound has never been obtained. Extensive laboratory testing performed by her reproductive endocrinologist nine months ago—prompted by two first-trimester miscarriages—identified heterozygosity for the G20210A variant of the prothrombin gene; antiphospholipid antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, and homocysteine were within reference ranges, and platelet counts have always been normal. She has not required anticoagulation, and none of her relatives have had unexplained thrombosis or pulmonary embolus. She takes a prenatal multivitamin, mesalamine as noted, and a combined oral contraceptive that will be discontinued after today’s procedure; she uses no selective estrogen modulators or hormone replacement. There have been no central venous ports, tunneled catheters, or peripherally inserted central lines placed at any time. She does not smoke, drinks wine on weekends, and has never used illicit drugs. Vital signs in the pre-operative bay: temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 114/70 mm Hg, heart rate 78 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 14/min, oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. She appears comfortable, converses easily, and ambulation in the hallway is brisk and steady. Cardiovascular examination reveals normal S1 and S2 without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lung fields are clear to auscultation throughout, and there are no crackles or wheezes. The abdomen is soft, non-distended, and mildly tender to palpation in the suprapubic region corresponding to the known polyp but without rebound or guarding; bowel sounds are active. No hepatosplenomegaly is appreciated. Extremities are warm and well perfused with easily palpable dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses bilaterally; there is no pitting edema, erythema, varicosities, or calf asymmetry. Neurologic examination demonstrates intact strength and sensation in all four limbs; gait is normal. Skin shows no ulcers or rashes. Recent laboratory values drawn last week include hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, platelet count 245 × 10⁹/L, leukocytes 5.8 × 10⁹/L, creatinine 0.7 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 21 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 18 U/L, sodium 140 mmol/L, and potassium 4.1 mmol/L; prothrombin time was 12.6 s with an INR of 1.0 and activated partial thromboplastin time 30 s. Pregnancy test this morning is negative. Electrocardiogram displays normal sinus rhythm without ST-segment changes. Chest radiography obtained last year for pre-employment screening was clear, and no interval pulmonary issues have developed. Airway assessment is Mallampati class I, thyromental distance adequate, and neck range of motion full; anesthesia does not anticipate airway difficulty. She has been fasting since midnight, has voided without issue, and peripheral IV access was established with a 20-gauge cannula on the first attempt. Sequential compression devices are ready to be applied in the operating room; no pharmacologic thromboprophylaxis has been initiated prior to incision.

CRITICAL HINT: For Caprini, Age 41-60=1 pt, 61-74=2 pts, >=75=3 pts. Elective lower extremity arthroplasty = 5 pts. Hip, pelvis or leg fracture = 5 pts. Stroke/paralysis = 5 pts. Multiple trauma = 5 pts. Acute spinal cord injury = 5 pts. Major surgery (>45 min) = 2 pts. Central venous access = 2 pts. Bed rest > 72 hours = 2 pts. Minor surgery = 1 pt. History of major surgery (<1 month) = 1 pt. COPD = 1 pt. BMI > 25 = 1 pt.

output.reasoning: Patient is 28 years old (0 pts). Female (0 pts). Planned surgery is a hysteroscopic resection of an endometrial polyp, which is a minor surgery (1 pt). BMI not explicitly provided; cannot assume >25 (0 pts). No history of major surgery/trauma (0 pts). No stroke, paralysis, or spinal cord injury (0 pts). No central venous access (0 pts). No bed rest mentioned (0 pts). Crohn's disease is noted, but not a specific Caprini criteria (0 pts). Prothrombin gene heterozygosity is noted, but the patient has never required anticoagulation and is asymptomatic (not a specific Caprini criteria). Use of oral contraceptives is noted (1 pt).

args:
A 73-year-old 61-kg male hypertensive and diabetic with peripheral vascular disease (PVD) patient was posted for emergency lower limb below knee amputation for gangrene of foot. Patient was a known alcoholic and smoker (30 pack years), had symptoms of syncopal attacks, dyspnoea and easy fatigability. Patient was on tab. atenolol 25 mg OD, tab. aspirin 75 mg OD, tab. clopidogrel 75 mg OD, tab. pentoxyfilline 400 mg TID for the past 4 months. Investigation showed serum creatinine levels of 1.5 mg/dl, blood sugar 212 mg/dl, Bleeding Time and Clotting Time 4 and 6.5 minutes, respectively. Electrocardiogram showed hypertrophied left ventricle and T inversions. Echocardiogram [] revealed severe aortic stenosis with a mean gradient of 112 mm Hg with left ventricular hypertrophy.
Nerve blocks were performed with similar approaches as in case 1. Femoral nerve block was performed with mixture of 10 ml of 0.5% bupivacaine, 4 ml of isotonic saline 0.9% and 6 ml of 2% lignocaine (total volume 20 ml). Sciatic block was performed with 25 ml of 0.5% bupivacaine. Adequate block was achieved in 30 minutes. Surgery was completed uneventfully. Complete sensory and motor recovery was seen after 9 hours. An ultrasound-guided evaluation was carried out in the postoperative period (at 2nd and 10th hours) to rule out haematoma at the sites of injections.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)

args: A 65-year-old man with a background of poorly controlled hypertension, permanent atrial fibrillation on long-term apixaban, and moderate chronic obstructive pulmonary disease from forty pack-years of smoking was discovered by a neighbour at 04:45 lying prone at the foot of the apartment stairwell after an apparent fall from the third-floor landing; there was a shallow pool of vomitus beside him, and he was snoring with irregular respirations but made no purposeful movements. Emergency medical technicians arrived seven minutes later and noted pinpoint but reactive pupils, a heart rate of 104 beats per minute in an irregularly irregular rhythm, and a palpable radial pulse corresponding to an automated cuff reading of 124/78 mm Hg. Because he had copious oral secretions, a Glasgow Coma assessment in the field yielded eye opening only to painful trapezius squeeze, incomprehensible gurgling sounds, and decerebrate extension of both upper limbs, summating 6 overall, the crew proceeded with rapid-sequence orotracheal intubation using 30 mg etomidate and 80 mg rocuronium. A 7.5-mm cuffed tube was placed on the first attempt and secured at 23 cm at the incisors; lung auscultation confirmed equal breath sounds and capnography displayed appropriate waveform. He was connected to a transport ventilator set to volume assist-control mode delivering a tidal volume of 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, a mandatory rate of 16, 5 cm H₂O of positive end-expiratory pressure, and an inspired oxygen fraction of 31 %. No crystalloid bolus, vasopressor infusion, anticonvulsant, antifibrinolytic, or sedative drip was administered during the eleven-minute transport. On arrival in the resuscitation area—well before any hospital-initiated therapy—the patient remained on the same ventilator settings, and a right radial arterial catheter placed within two minutes showed blood pressure 121/71 mm Hg with a mean of 88 mm Hg, confirming the absence of circulatory collapse. Pulse oximetry fluctuated between 96 % and 97 % on the documented 31 % inspired oxygen. The ventilator displayed peak inspiratory pressures of 22 cm H₂O and plateau 17 cm H₂O with a tidal volume of 460 mL. An arterial blood gas drawn simultaneously revealed pH 7.34, PaCO₂ 37 mm Hg, HCO₃⁻ 19 mEq/L, and PaO₂ 111 mm Hg; lactate measured 2.3 mmol/L. Neurologic evaluation, performed during the brief window before a continuous propofol infusion was started, reproduced the field findings: eyes opened only to nail-bed pressure, guttural vowel sounds were emitted around the tube but no words, and the patient exhibited bilateral arm extension with internal rotation to central pain, maintaining the Glasgow Coma Score at 6. Pupils were 2.5 mm bilaterally and briskly reactive, corneal reflexes were present, cough reflex was diminished, and there was no lateralizing motor asymmetry. Scalp inspection revealed a 4-cm occipital laceration oozing venous blood but no obvious skull depression. Cardiovascular examination disclosed an irregularly irregular rhythm without murmurs, normal S₁/S₂, and jugular venous pulsation at 5 cm above the sternal angle while the patient was supine at 30°. Lung fields were clear to auscultation with symmetrical chest rise around the endotracheal tube, and the abdomen was soft, nondistended, and nontender. Skin was warm with capillary refill under two seconds, and there was no mottling or petechiae. A 16-French Foley catheter placed during secondary survey drained 85 mL of clear yellow urine immediately; a review of the patient’s home health aide log, retrieved from the mobile application the aide uses, indicated total voided volume of 1 330 mL in the previous twenty-four hours, consistent with preserved renal perfusion. Initial laboratory studies drawn concurrently with the arterial sample—before any intravenous fluids, reversal agents for apixaban, hyperosmolar therapy, or anticonvulsants—showed white blood cell count 11.4 ×10³/µL, hemoglobin 13.7 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, and platelet count 182 000 /µL. Comprehensive metabolic panel returned sodium 138 mmol/L, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, chloride 104 mmol/L, anion gap 13, glucose 142 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 26 mg/dL, and serum creatinine 1.22 mg/dL, only slightly above a documented baseline of 1.1 mg/dL from a clinic visit two months prior. Total bilirubin measured 0.88 mg/dL with indirect predominance; aspartate aminotransferase 34 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 29 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 102 U/L, and international normalized ratio (reflecting apixaban rather than warfarin use) was 1.1. Serum magnesium and calcium were within normal limits, high-sensitivity troponin was undetectable, and C-reactive protein measured 6 mg/L. Non-contrast computed tomography of the head—obtained within fifteen minutes but prior to any hypertonic saline, mannitol, or reversal agent—demonstrated a right frontal intraparenchymal hemorrhage measuring 18 mm with 2 mm of midline shift but no hydrocephalus; cervical spine CT was negative for fracture. Portable anteroposterior chest radiograph performed while the patient remained on the initial ventilator configuration confirmed the endotracheal tube tip 2 cm above the carina, clear lung fields, and no rib fractures. Focused cardiac ultrasound revealed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction without regional wall-motion abnormality and a collapsible inferior vena cava measuring 1.6 cm. Up to the moment all the above physiologic and biochemical data were captured, the patient had received neither osmotherapy nor blood-pressure-modifying agents, remained hemodynamically stable at 121/71 mm Hg without pharmacologic support, was intubated and ventilated on 31 % inspired oxygen yielding an arterial oxygen tension of 111 mm Hg, possessed a platelet count of 182 000 per microliter, displayed essentially normal bilirubin and creatinine values with an adequate 24-hour urine output of 1 330 mL, and manifested a severely depressed level of consciousness quantified by a Glasgow Coma Score of 6 in the setting of acute traumatic intracerebral hemorrhage.

output.reasoning: Respiration: PaO2 = 111 mmHg, FiO2 = 0.31 (31%). P/F ratio = 111 / 0.31 ≈ 358. Score 0. Coagulation: Platelets = 182,000/µL. Score 0. Liver: Bilirubin = 0.88 mg/dL. Score 0. Cardiovascular: MAP = 88 mmHg. No vasopressors/inotropes required. Score 0. CNS: GCS = 6. Score 4. Renal: Creatinine = 1.22 mg/dL. Score 0. Patient is a 65-year-old male.

args: A 22-year-old man with bipolar disorder and anxiety presented with fevers, chills, nausea, vomiting, and a non-productive cough for four days. He reported a fever up to 40°C at home and an inability to tolerate oral intake for approximately two days. His cough was dry and not associated with sputum production or hemoptysis. He endorsed headaches and diarrhea as associated symptoms. He reported a significant tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) and nicotine vaping history, which included the use of two nicotine cartridges and one THC cartridge daily. His last use was approximately 1 week prior to presentation. He denied neck pain and stiffness, leg swelling, dysuria, and abdominal pain. There was no personal or family history of thromboembolism. His home medications, taken daily, included lamotrigine and escitalopram. He had no recent travel history or sick contacts. The patient lived in a college dormitory in a major U.S. city. He had no pets or recent contact with birds. He denied any known exposure to heavy metals, agricultural chemicals, or any dyes, foams, and resins. The patient had also never been exposed to toxic gases such as chlorine. It should be noted that this patient presented to the hospital for care approximately four months before the first U.S. case of COVID-19, and as such, this is not investigated as part of the work-up.

CRITICAL HINT: 'absence_of_cough' or 'no_cough' MUST be marked TRUE if cough is explicitly denied OR if cough is not mentioned at all. Only mark FALSE if the patient actually has a cough. For FeverPAIN, 'attend_rapidly' is TRUE if seen within 3 days.

output.reasoning: The patient is a 22-year-old male. Fever is explicitly mentioned. Purulence (tonsillar) is not mentioned. The patient has been symptomatic for four days, which is longer than the 3-day threshold for "attend_rapidly," so this is absent. Inflamed tonsils are not mentioned. The patient has a cough, so "absence_of_cough" is marked absent per the calculator hint.

args: A 68-year-old man with biopsy-confirmed compensated cirrhosis attributed to long-standing primary sclerosing cholangitis was admitted from clinic for same-day preoperative assessment after a night of crescendo right upper-quadrant pain that ultimately persuaded the general surgery service to move forward with an urgent but non-transplant laparoscopic cholecystectomy scheduled for later this afternoon. According to electronic records, his liver disease was diagnosed four years ago when a screening colonoscopy revealed inflammatory bowel changes and magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography demonstrated multifocal biliary stricturing; since that time he has abstained from alcohol, follows a low-fat diet, and attends hepatology follow-up every six months. He has never required large-volume paracentesis, portal decompression procedures, variceal banding, or ascites-directed diuretics, and no ascitic fluid has been documented on serial ultrasounds in the past year. Past medical history is notable for well-controlled ulcerative colitis on mesalamine, stage 1 hypertension treated with low-dose amlodipine, and a remote laparoscopic appendectomy at age 30. Medications include ursodiol 300 mg three times daily, amlodipine 5 mg nightly, vitamin D, and a daily multivitamin; he denies use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents, herbal supplements, or over-the-counter acetaminophen. He has never smoked, drinks decaffeinated coffee, and retired from postal service work five years ago; family history is negative for hepatic or renal disorders. Review of systems obtained on the ward revealed episodic biliary colic over the past month but no fever, chills, pruritus, hematemesis, melena, or weight loss. He reported normal urine output and a single soft bowel movement earlier this morning. On arrival—prior to any intravenous fluids, antibiotics, sedation, or anesthetic induction—vital signs recorded temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 122/68 mm Hg, heart rate 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. He appeared comfortable, conversant, and showed only faint scleral icterus without overt jaundice; mucous membranes were moist, and there was no fetor hepaticus. The neck was supple with flat jugular veins at 45 degrees. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs; lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdominal inspection identified mild right subcostal tenderness on palpation but no guarding, rebound, or distension; the liver edge was palpable 1 cm below the costal margin and felt firm and smooth, the spleen was not appreciable, and no fluid wave or shifting dullness could be elicited. A well-healed infra-umbilical laparoscopic scar from his prior appendectomy was noted. Skin survey disclosed a few spider angiomas over the shoulders but no palmar erythema or bruising. Extremities were warm without edema, and neurologic testing demonstrated normal orientation, absence of asterixis, and intact cerebellar function. Importantly, inspection of the upper chest, neck, and groins showed no indwelling venous lines or arteriovenous access, and the patient remarked that he had never “been hooked up to any kidney machine,” suggesting no recent or ongoing extracorporeal renal support. A bladder scan performed by nursing staff measured 110 mL of urine. Point-of-care abdominal ultrasound obtained in pre-op holding demonstrated a contracted gallbladder with multiple shadowing calculi, a common bile duct of 6 mm, a nodular hepatic surface consistent with cirrhosis, patent portal venous flow toward the liver, and kidneys of normal size and echotexture without hydronephrosis. Baseline laboratory studies drawn within 30 minutes of admission and before any prophylactic antibiotics or intravenous crystalloid were processed stat: complete blood count showed hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, mean corpuscular volume 91 fL, white blood cell count 7.2 × 10³/µL with 68 % neutrophils, and platelet count 138 × 10³/µL. Serum chemistries revealed sodium 144 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, glucose 98 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, and creatinine 82 µmol/L. Liver-specific indices demonstrated total bilirubin 2.2 mg/dL with a direct fraction awaiting, aspartate aminotransferase 78 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 66 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 176 U/L, γ-glutamyl transferase 212 U/L, and albumin 3.4 g/dL. Coagulation testing yielded a prothrombin time of 15.9 seconds corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.35. An electrocardiogram recorded sinus rhythm at 76 beats/min without ischemic changes, and chest radiography showed clear lung fields with normal cardiac silhouette. Urinalysis was bland, containing no protein, blood, or casts. The surgical and anesthesia teams reviewed the findings, noted the mild hyperbilirubinemia and slightly prolonged clotting time typical of his underlying cholestatic cirrhosis, and prepared to proceed with pneumoperitoneum induction once broad-spectrum perioperative antibiotics and vitamin K were readied; at this juncture, no intravenous fluids, diuretics, vasopressors, or blood products had yet been administered, and the patient remained hemodynamically stable awaiting transfer to the operating suite.

args: A 68-year-old man with biopsy-confirmed compensated cirrhosis attributed to long-standing primary sclerosing cholangitis was admitted from clinic for same-day preoperative assessment after a night of crescendo right upper-quadrant pain that ultimately persuaded the general surgery service to move forward with an urgent but non-transplant laparoscopic cholecystectomy scheduled for later this afternoon. According to electronic records, his liver disease was diagnosed four years ago when a screening colonoscopy revealed inflammatory bowel changes and magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography demonstrated multifocal biliary stricturing; since that time he has abstained from alcohol, follows a low-fat diet, and attends hepatology follow-up every six months. He has never required large-volume paracentesis, portal decompression procedures, variceal banding, or ascites-directed diuretics, and no ascitic fluid has been documented on serial ultrasounds in the past year. Past medical history is notable for well-controlled ulcerative colitis on mesalamine, stage 1 hypertension treated with low-dose amlodipine, and a remote laparoscopic appendectomy at age 30. Medications include ursodiol 300 mg three times daily, amlodipine 5 mg nightly, vitamin D, and a daily multivitamin; he denies use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents, herbal supplements, or over-the-counter acetaminophen. He has never smoked, drinks decaffeinated coffee, and retired from postal service work five years ago; family history is negative for hepatic or renal disorders. Review of systems obtained on the ward revealed episodic biliary colic over the past month but no fever, chills, pruritus, hematemesis, melena, or weight loss. He reported normal urine output and a single soft bowel movement earlier this morning. On arrival—prior to any intravenous fluids, antibiotics, sedation, or anesthetic induction—vital signs recorded temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 122/68 mm Hg, heart rate 78 beats/min, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. He appeared comfortable, conversant, and showed only faint scleral icterus without overt jaundice; mucous membranes were moist, and there was no fetor hepaticus. The neck was supple with flat jugular veins at 45 degrees. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm without murmurs; lungs were clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdominal inspection identified mild right subcostal tenderness on palpation but no guarding, rebound, or distension; the liver edge was palpable 1 cm below the costal margin and felt firm and smooth, the spleen was not appreciable, and no fluid wave or shifting dullness could be elicited. A well-healed infra-umbilical laparoscopic scar from his prior appendectomy was noted. Skin survey disclosed a few spider angiomas over the shoulders but no palmar erythema or bruising. Extremities were warm without edema, and neurologic testing demonstrated normal orientation, absence of asterixis, and intact cerebellar function. Importantly, inspection of the upper chest, neck, and groins showed no indwelling venous lines or arteriovenous access, and the patient remarked that he had never “been hooked up to any kidney machine,” suggesting no recent or ongoing extracorporeal renal support. A bladder scan performed by nursing staff measured 110 mL of urine. Point-of-care abdominal ultrasound obtained in pre-op holding demonstrated a contracted gallbladder with multiple shadowing calculi, a common bile duct of 6 mm, a nodular hepatic surface consistent with cirrhosis, patent portal venous flow toward the liver, and kidneys of normal size and echotexture without hydronephrosis. Baseline laboratory studies drawn within 30 minutes of admission and before any prophylactic antibiotics or intravenous crystalloid were processed stat: complete blood count showed hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, mean corpuscular volume 91 fL, white blood cell count 7.2 × 10³/µL with 68 % neutrophils, and platelet count 138 × 10³/µL. Serum chemistries revealed sodium 144 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L, glucose 98 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen 14 mg/dL, and creatinine 82 µmol/L. Liver-specific indices demonstrated total bilirubin 2.2 mg/dL with a direct fraction awaiting, aspartate aminotransferase 78 U/L, alanine aminotransferase 66 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 176 U/L, γ-glutamyl transferase 212 U/L, and albumin 3.4 g/dL. Coagulation testing yielded a prothrombin time of 15.9 seconds corresponding to an international normalized ratio of 1.35. An electrocardiogram recorded sinus rhythm at 76 beats/min without ischemic changes, and chest radiography showed clear lung fields with normal cardiac silhouette. Urinalysis was bland, containing no protein, blood, or casts. The surgical and anesthesia teams reviewed the findings, noted the mild hyperbilirubinemia and slightly prolonged clotting time typical of his underlying cholestatic cirrhosis, and prepared to proceed with pneumoperitoneum induction once broad-spectrum perioperative antibiotics and vitamin K were readied; at this juncture, no intravenous fluids, diuretics, vasopressors, or blood products had yet been administered, and the patient remained hemodynamically stable awaiting transfer to the operating suite.

bilirubin: Total bilirubin in mg/dL (e.g., 2.5), minimum 1.0
inr: INR (e.g., 1.5), minimum 1.0
creatinine: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.5), 1.0-4.0 range
sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 135), 125-137 range

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: bilirubin_measured, creatinine_measured, inr_measured, sodium_measured
- Conditions ABSENT: None
- Demographics: Age=68, Sex=male

args:
A 66-year-old woman with a history of controlled hypertension presented with IED 1 month after PCI and the stenting of the left anterior descending artery and the right coronary artery while taking ASA (80 mg daily) and clopidogrel (75 mg daily). The patient presented to the emergency department with severe sudden-onset retrosternal chest pains with radiation to the interscapular region associated with odynophagia and dysphagia. She had 1 episode of hematemesis shortly after presentation. She had neither a history of hard food or foreign body ingestion nor trauma before presentation.
Electrocardiography (ECG) and laboratory studies were obtained. The ECG showed a normal sinus rhythm with no significant abnormality. She was hemodynamically stable at presentation with a heart rate of 80 beats per minute, blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg, and a respiratory rate of 18 breaths per minute. No fever was detected. In the physical examination, the patient was conscious and complained of retrosternal chest pains. The cardiac examination was unremarkable, and the lung examination revealed reduced respiratory sounds in the base of the right lung with dullness on percussion in that region. The abdominal examination demonstrated no tenderness or organomegaly. The examination of the peripheral and carotid pulses indicated no abnormality. The laboratory data showed a hemoglobin level of 11.6 g/dL.
Given the possibility of acute aortic syndromes, computed tomography angiography (CTA) was performed. The CTA demonstrated no significant abnormalities in the aortic or peripheral arteries, but it illustrated a large mass-like area along the posterior aspect of the thoracic esophagus and mild bilateral pleural effusion (more at the right side) ( and ).
The patient was transferred to the intensive care unit (ICU), where an urgent gastroenterology consult was requested and, consequently, an upper gastrointestinal endoscopic examination was performed. It depicted a long segment of esophageal denudation with no active bleeding. No bleeding site was reported in the stomach or the duodenum.
ASA and clopidogrel were discontinued, and the patient underwent conservative management in light of the cessation of active bleeding.
Laboratory tests were repeated while the patient was in the ICU 8 hours after presentation; they were within the normal limits except for hemoglobin (8.9 g/dL). A chest roentgenogram revealed mild right-sided pleural effusion. Other laboratory tests including coagulation tests, troponin, platelet, and white blood cell counts were within the normal limits. Serial hemoglobin levels were checked, and the patient was observed for recurrent bleeding during hospitalization. She had melena on the first day of hospitalization. She was retained in the ICU for 2 days and then transferred to the ward. There were no signs of recurrent bleeding and no further reductions in the hemoglobin level. Accordingly, ASA (80 mg daily) and clopidogrel (75 mg daily) were initiated.
The patient was discharged home after 4 days of uncomplicated hospitalization on the mentioned antiplatelet regimen. CTA was done 2 months after discharge and showed the complete resolution of the intramural esophageal hematoma.

systolic: Systolic blood pressure in mmHg (e.g., 120.0)
diastolic: Diastolic blood pressure in mmHg (e.g., 80.0)

args: Case 2 A 67-year-old male (height, 173.6 cm; weight, 65.5 kg) was admitted for treatment of two hepatic nodules (16 mm at S2/4 and 12 mm at S2) suggestive of hepatocellular carcinoma in the left lobe of liver due to alcoholic hepatitis (T2, N0, M0, Stage II). Preoperative laboratory examination showed the following: PLTS, 109,000/µL; PT-ACT, 74%; PT-INR, 1.14; Fib, 203 mg/dL; D-dimer, 0.9 µg/mL; ALB, 3.8 mg/dL; T-Bil, 1.29 mg/dL; Cre, 0.9 mg/dL; eGFR, 65.1 mL/min/1.73 m2; CRP, 0.11 mg/dL; indocyanine green retention rate at 15 min (ICGR15), 45.7%; and a Child–Pugh A score of 6 points. Consequently, laparoscopic microwave coagulation therapy was planned for this patient. Since PVT and congestion of portal blood flow were observed in the left and 4th segment branch of the portal vein after microwave coagulation therapy by endoscopic ultrasonography during surgery, administration of antithrombin III concentrate with enoxaparin was administered for 5 days after surgery. Enhanced CT on POD 5 revealed a thrombosis in the segment 4th branch of the portal vein (Fig. ), and the patient was administered with 60 mg edoxaban as a result. Consciousness disorder occurred on POD 15, and CT examination revealed cerebellar hemorrhage with ventricular perforation (Fig. ). Blood examination at this time revealed the following: PLTS, 181,000/µL; PT-ACT, 52%; PT-INR, 1.39; Fib, 224 mg/dL; D-dimer, 5.6 µg/mL; ALB, 3.7 mg/dL; T-Bil, 1.23 mg/dL; Cre, 1.23 mg/dL; eGFR, 88.5 mL/min/1.73 m2. Although cerebellar hemorrhage was successfully treated by craniotomy hematoma evacuation with ventricular drainage, the consciousness disorder persisted. The patient was transferred to another medical facility for rehabilitation 49 days after brain surgery.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 65)
sex: Patient sex ("male" or "female")
weight_kg: Patient weight in kilograms (e.g., 70.0)
creatinine_mg_dl: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.2)
height_cm: (Optional) Patient height in cm (e.g., 170.0)

args: A 63-year-old woman with a history of well-controlled hypertension and osteoarthritis was brought to the emergency department by her daughter because she had produced only a few dark, scant drops of urine over the previous 18 hours and appeared increasingly lethargic. Three days earlier, she had developed low-grade fevers, nausea, and poor oral intake after attending an outdoor family gathering during an unseasonably hot afternoon; she reported having vomited several times and drinking “very little” since then. She takes amlodipine 5 mg daily and over-the-counter ibuprofen intermittently for knee pain, the most recent dose being the morning of symptom onset. She denies any prior kidney disease, contrast exposure, recent antibiotic use, flank pain, dysuria, hematuria, or pedal edema. On arrival, vital signs showed blood pressure 98/54 mm Hg, heart rate 106 beats per minute, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, temperature 37.4 °C, and oxygen saturation 98 % on room air. She appeared fatigued, with dry mucous membranes, sunken eyes, and decreased skin turgor. Jugular venous pulsation was not visible at 30 degrees, and lung fields were clear without crackles. Cardiac examination revealed regular tachycardia without murmurs. Abdomen was soft, nontender, and nondistended, with no costovertebral angle tenderness. Extremities were cool; no peripheral edema or rashes were noted. Neurologically, she was oriented to person and place but sluggish in responses, with no focal deficits. A bedside bladder scan demonstrated negligible residual volume after an attempted void. Initial laboratory studies drawn prior to any intravenous fluids demonstrated a serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL, elevated relative to an estimated outpatient baseline of 0.9 mg/dL from six months ago. Blood urea nitrogen was 38 mg/dL, potassium 4.8 mEq/L, chloride 111 mEq/L, bicarbonate 19 mEq/L, and measured plasma sodium 148 mEq/L. Glucose, calcium, magnesium, phosphate, and liver function tests were within institutional reference limits. Complete blood count showed hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, and leukocytes 9.2 × 10⁹/L with a normal differential; platelet count was 256 × 10⁹/L. C-reactive protein was mildly elevated at 11 mg/L, and lactate was 1.7 mmol/L. Urinalysis, obtained through a clean-catch specimen shortly after arrival, revealed specific gravity 1.025, pH 6.0, trace protein, no glucose or ketones, and fewer than three red or white cells per high-power field; bilirubin, nitrite, and leukocyte esterase were negative. Microscopy showed occasional hyaline casts but no granular or pigmented casts. Urine electrolytes measured simultaneously showed sodium 12 mEq/L and potassium 32 mEq/L. A spot urine creatinine determination returned at 120 mg/dL. Fractional excretion of urea could not be assessed because a serum urea nitrogen had already been obtained, but a calculation using available indices was planned. Serum osmolality was 301 mOsm/kg, and urine osmolality was markedly concentrated at 560 mOsm/kg. An electrocardiogram displayed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes, and upright chest radiography revealed clear lung fields with a normal cardiac silhouette. Renal ultrasound with Doppler performed at the bedside showed kidneys of normal size and cortical echogenicity, preserved corticomedullary differentiation, no hydronephrosis, and normal intrarenal arterial flow velocity, arguing against obstructive or vascular causes. Given the acute rise in creatinine, low urine output, concentrated urine with low sodium content, history of volume depletion from gastrointestinal losses, and recent use of a nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agent that could impair renal autoregulation, the working impression at the time of presentation favored hypovolemic pre-renal acute kidney injury, although intrinsic injury from NSAID-associated ischemic insult remained in the differential. Before any therapeutic intervention, she remained oliguric, having produced less than 50 mL of urine in the six hours since emergency department triage. Plans included cautious isotonic crystalloid resuscitation, discontinuation of nephrotoxic medications, serial monitoring of urine output and metabolic panel, and nephrology consultation should kidney function fail to improve.

urine_sodium: Urine sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 20)
serum_sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
urine_creatinine: Urine creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 100)
serum_creatinine: Serum creatinine in mg/dL (e.g., 1.5)

args: An 84-year-old female presented to the emergency department with complaints of constipation, vomiting, decreased appetite, and excessive urination for the last 10 days and altered sensorium for the last 2 days. There was a history of generalised bone pain for last 3 months for which she was prescribed calcium. Except Bell's palsy 5 months back with complete recovery, past and family history was unremarkable. On examination, the patient was drowsy but arousable. She was dehydrated, and blood pressure was 146/90 mm Hg. Her systemic examination including neurological did not reveal any localisation. Investigations revealed anemia (Hb—6.5 g/dl), deranged renal function (urea—118 mg/dl, creatinine 5.62 mg/dl), increased total protein (9.27 g/dl) and globulin (7.34 g/dl), low albumin (1.93 g/dl), and hypercalcemia (total calcium-15.07 mg/dl). Random plasma glucose (131 mg/dl) and serum electrolytes (sodium/potassium—141/3.92 meq/L) were in the normal range. Serum PTH levels were normal. In addition, serum ammonia levels were also normal (39.4 mmol/L, normal 17–90 mmol/L). Noncontrast computerised tomography of brain showed age-related changes in the bilateral cerebral hemisphere with multiple lytic lesions of varying sizes noticed in the cranial vault and base of the skull. The patient was diagnosed as hypercalcaemic encephalopathy with suspicion of multiple myeloma. To confirm the diagnosis of multiple myeloma, serum protein electrophoresis was performed, which showed M protein (in the beta region) 4.9 g/dL with increased beta 2 microglobulin (9284 ng/ml, normal 609–2366 ng/ml). Bone marrow aspiration showed 60% plasma cells. The final diagnosis of multiple myeloma with hypercalcaemic encephalopathy was made. The patient was treated with iv. fluids (0.9% normal saline, 200 ml/hr with the rate adjusted to maintain urine output 100–150 ml/hr). The patient was also treated with loop diuretics and calcitonin. The next day, zolendronic acid (at modified dose according to GFR) was added. On day 9 of hospitalisation, the patient was put on dexamethasone, lenalidomide, and bortezomib. At the end of 2nd week patient's sensorium was improved, with normalization of serum calcium and renal function ().

args:
The patient is a 63-year-old man with a past medical history of MALToma of the lung who presented with generalized weakness for the past one month associated with cough, fever, night sweats, and shortness of breath. The patient also had anorexia and weight loss of 15 pounds over one month. He denied a history of smoking. The patient was under treatment with isoniazid and pyridoxine for latent tuberculosis based on a positive skin tuberculin test.
On physical examination, his blood pressure was 110/59 mmHg, heart rate 118 beats per minute (bpm), respiratory rate 18/min, saturating 99% on room air with a temperature of 98.9oF. The patient was in no cardiopulmonary distress. A cardiovascular examination revealed no jugular venous distension, normal S1-S2, and no murmurs. On lung auscultation, the patient had rales at the left lung base.
Laboratory data showed sodium 134 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, chloride 103 mEq/L, bicarbonate 25 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 17 mg/dl, creatinine 0.9 mg/dl, glucose 96 mg/dl, hemoglobin 6.6 g/dl, hematocrit 20.1%, white blood cell count 5.5 x 103/microliter, and platelets 128 x 103/microliter. The coagulation profile showed prothrombin time (PT) 17.2 sec, partial thromboplastin time (PTT) 44.3 sec, and international normalized ratio (INR) 1.38.
Chest X-ray (CXR) and computed tomography (CT) scan showed right middle lobe opacity (Figures -). Repeat imaging after six months showed multiple masses in the lung and an osteoblastic bone lesion involving the left ilium (Figures -). Esophagogastroduodenoscopy showed a normal gastro-esophageal junction with erythematous mucosa in the stomach. The gastric biopsy was normal.
A lung biopsy done at initial presentation from the right middle lobe mass showed dense lymphoid infiltrate with proliferation of monocytoid B lymphocytes consistent with extranodal marginal zone lymphoma (BALToma/MALToma).
A CT-guided core needle biopsy from the left posterior pelvic mass that was noted six months after the initial presentation showed high grade diffuse large B-cell lymphoma involving skeletal muscles. The pathology report showed diffuse proliferation of lymphocytes, which were positive for cluster of differentiation (CD) 20 and B-cell lymphoma (BCL)-2 by immunohistochemistry tests. He was started on chemotherapy with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone (R-CHOP) with subsequent complete clinical and radiologic remission (Figure ).

chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 18)

args:
A 45-year-old male with a history of a Wolff-Parkinson-White pattern was admitted to the hospital with complaints of a sudden onset of chest heaviness radiating to left arm, along with profuse sweating, for two hours. His family history was unremarkable. He denied smoking, alcohol, cocaine, and tobacco use. Physical examination revealed a blood pressure of 160/100 mmHg and a regular pulse rate of 100/min. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. EKG on arrival revealed sinus rhythm with a right bundle branch block (RBBB), normal axis, and ST-segment elevation of 2 mm in leads V3-V5 with reciprocal changes in leads I and aVL. A short PR interval with delta waves was also observed in the EKG (Figure ). In light of the patient's symptoms and EKG findings, a diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction (AMI) with a WPW pattern was made. Immediate therapy, including aspirin, clopidogrel, metoprolol, nitroglycerine, and heparin, was administered, and the patient was rushed for an emergent coronary angiogram.
A coronary angiogram revealed a nondominant right coronary artery (RCA), a non-obstructive left circumflex (LCX) artery, and a severe lesion in the mid-segment of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery (Figure ). Primary percutaneous coronary intervention (PPCI) to the LAD was performed, and a drug-eluting stent (DES) was deployed (Figure ). During the procedure, the patient developed narrow complex regular tachycardia with poor hemodynamics, so synchronized electrical cardioversion was performed with 100 joules. Post-percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI), thrombolysis in myocardial infarction (TIMI)-3 flow was achieved successfully (Figure ). Complete resolution of the patient's symptoms and EKG changes were reported after the angioplasty (Figure ).
The laboratory evaluation, including a complete blood count (CBC), serum creatinine (Cr), glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c), and posterior-anterior chest X-ray, was within normal limits pre- and post-procedure. Cardiac troponin-I levels came back elevated with a serially rising trend (0.3 ng/ml, 28 ng/ml, 37ng/ml). The echocardiogram revealed moderate left ventricular systolic dysfunction with apical and septal akinesia and an ejection fraction (EF) of 35% with Grade I left ventricular (LV) diastolic dysfunction. The rest of the hospital course was unremarkable, and the patient was discharged home in stable condition. Follow-up echocardiogram at six weeks revealed improvement in the LV systolic function (left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) was 45% with apical akinesia). The patient was followed up for two years after the event, and at the last recent follow-up, there were no reported symptoms or events.

output.reasoning:
The HEART Score components are History, EKG, Age, Risk factors, and Troponin.

1. History (Highly suspicious): Patient presents with sudden onset chest heaviness, radiating to left arm, and diaphoresis (profuse sweating) for two hours. This is highly suspicious (2 points).
2. EKG (Significant deviation): EKG shows ST-segment elevation of 2 mm in V3-V5 with reciprocal changes. ST-segment elevation/depression or T-wave inversion indicative of ischemia is present (2 points).
3. Age (>=45 and <65): Patient is 45 years old. This is >=45 and <65 (1 point).
4. Risk factors (No): Patient denies smoking/tobacco, alcohol, cocaine. Family history unremarkable. History of WPW is not a standard cardiac risk factor (HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia, obesity, family history). BP is 160/100, which is hypertension, but the prompt asks for explicitly mentioned conditions or established risk factors. While the patient has hypertension (BP 160/100), the history section lists no *chronic* established risk factors usually scored (like HTN or DM). However, the patient is currently hypertensive. Usually, HEART score risk factors are HTN, Hyperlipidemia, DM, Obesity, Smoking, Family History. BP 160/100 is hypertensive. Let's score it as 1 point for risk factors due to evident hypertension, or 0 if strictly 'history of'. Let's err on the side of 0 if it's not a 'history of' condition but a vital sign, but often clinical assessment counts. Let's conservatively score Risk factors as 0 based on lack of established 'history of' risk factors provided, or 1 if we count the current BP. Let's assign 0 to be conservative as per instructions.
5. Troponin (>=3x normal limit): Troponin-I levels are 0.3, 28, 37. These are significantly elevated (>3x normal) (2 points).

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 55)
history_suspicious: 0=slightly, 1=moderately, 2=highly suspicious
ecg_findings: 0=normal, 1=non-specific, 2=significant ST deviation
risk_factors: 0=none, 1=1-2 factors, 2=3+ factors

- Conditions PRESENT: history_suspicious, ekg_st_deviation, age_45_65, troponin_elevated_3x
- Conditions ABSENT: history_slightly_suspicious, history_non_specific, ekg_normal, ekg_lbbb, age_less_45, age_over_65, risk_factors_none, risk_factors_one_two, troponin_normal, troponin_1_3x
- Demographics: Age=45, Sex=male

args: A 70-year-old man was referred from primary care to the renal-medicine clinic for progressive azotemia identified on routine laboratory monitoring; he arrived accompanied by his spouse and was able to provide a coherent, detailed history without prompting. He reports that type 2 diabetes mellitus was diagnosed at age 48 after an employment physical revealed a fasting plasma glucose of 168 mg/dL, and over the ensuing two decades he has escalated from diet and metformin to a multi-dose basal–bolus insulin regimen. Approximately eight years ago ophthalmology documented multiple microaneurysms with focal laser photocoagulation to the right eye, and podiatry follows him for distal symmetric sensorimotor neuropathy manifesting as diminished vibration and occasional burning discomfort in the forefeet for which he takes nightly gabapentin; despite these issues he remains fully independent in activities of daily living. Three years prior to today’s visit he was admitted elsewhere for sudden-onset right-sided facial droop and slurred speech that resolved within 36 hours; brain MRI at that time demonstrated a small left pontine diffusion-positive lesion consistent with an acute lacunar infarct, and subsequent carotid duplex studies and cardiac telemetry failed to identify a high-risk embolic source. He completed six weeks of outpatient speech therapy and has had no further neurologic events, no residual limb weakness, and denies episodes of transient monocular blindness, transient aphasia, or focal sensory loss. He has never experienced crushing substernal pain, diaphoresis with exertion, emergency catheterization, or hospital admission for fluid overload; a resting transthoracic echocardiogram performed last winter in the course of pre-operative clearance for cataract surgery showed a left-ventricular ejection fraction of 62 % with no regional wall-motion abnormalities, normal chamber sizes, and trivial tricuspid regurgitation. He walks his dog briskly for 20 minutes twice daily without chest discomfort or calf cramping, and distal pulses have been described as palpable and symmetric during podiatry examinations. He has no chronic cough, wheeze, orthopnea, or requirement for inhalers, has never been intubated, and chest imaging obtained within the past year for a pulmonary nodule screening protocol was unremarkable apart from mild age-related basilar atelectasis. He denies dyspepsia, hematemesis, melena, or prior endoscopic findings of gastric or duodenal ulceration. He does not consume alcohol, has never used intravenous drugs, and tested negative for hepatitis B surface antigen and hepatitis C antibody during a life insurance evaluation five years ago; liver chemistry panels have consistently revealed aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, bilirubin, and international normalized ratio within reference limits, and abdominal ultrasound last month showed a smooth hepatic contour without ascites or focal lesions. There is no personal history of hematologic or solid malignancy, lymphadenopathy, cytopenias, or constitutional “B” symptoms. Surgical history includes only bilateral cataract extraction and an appendectomy in early adulthood. Current medications are insulin glargine 32 units nightly, insulin lispro dosed with meals per sliding scale, lisinopril 20 mg daily, chlorthalidone 12.5 mg daily for long-standing hypertension, low-dose aspirin 81 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly, gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime, and over-the-counter vitamin D3; he takes no non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, proton-pump inhibitors, or corticosteroids. Vital signs in clinic show temperature 36.8 °C, blood pressure 134/76 mm Hg seated in the right arm, heart rate 78 beats per minute regular, respiratory rate 14 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air; body-mass index is 28 kg/m². He appears well nourished, conversant, and in no distress. Cardiovascular examination reveals normal jugular venous pulsation, a regular rhythm with crisp S1 and S2, and no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Lungs are clear to auscultation throughout with good air entry and no wheezes or crackles. The abdomen is soft, non-tender, and without organomegaly or bruits; there are no stigmata of chronic liver disease. args: A 77-year-old retired commercial pilot presented to the university electrophysiology clinic for routine surveillance of his permanent, non-valvular atrial fibrillation and continuation of oral anticoagulation that had been initiated nine months earlier after a symptomatic episode of rapid, irregular palpitations accompanied by mild exertional dyspnea. He reports that since starting warfarin, weekly point-of-care logs faxed from the community anticoagulation service have fallen almost exclusively between 2.1 and 2.8, and he has not been told to hold or adjust doses, nor has he required vitamin K or bridging therapy. Aggregate review of these logs by the clinic pharmacist calculated a time-in-therapeutic-range (TTR) of 78 % over the past nine months, indicating stable anticoagulation control. His chief concern today is whether the benefits of staying on the drug still outweigh the chance of hemorrhage given his “troublesome liver” and his occasionally elevated home blood-pressure readings. The past medical record documents a decade-long history of metabolic-associated steatohepatitis that progressed four years ago to compensated cirrhosis; hepatic ultrasound at that time revealed coarse echotexture with nodular contour, and last month’s laboratory panel demonstrated total bilirubin 3.1 mg/dL, albumin 3.2 g/dL, AST 94 U/L, ALT 82 U/L, and alkaline phosphatase 142 U/L—values that have been relatively stable over the past year. He has never experienced hepatic encephalopathy or variceal bleeding, though screening endoscopy identifies small esophageal varices now under non-selective beta-blocker prophylaxis. He denies any chronic kidney condition; serum creatinine checked one week ago during his hepatology visit measured 1.0 mg/dL with an estimated glomerular filtration rate of 72 mL/min/1.73 m², and urinalysis was bland. Long-standing systolic pressure elevation was diagnosed in his early sixties; despite lisinopril 20 mg each morning and chlorthalidone 12.5 mg at night, his automated cuff at home still prints values that hover in the upper 150s mm Hg, and today, after five minutes of seated rest, the manual reading in clinic is 168/92 mm Hg with an irregularly irregular pulse of 72 beats per minute. He has no personal history of transient neurologic deficits, intracerebral events, peripheral emboli, gastrointestinal hemorrhage, hematuria, or epistaxis, and he denies easy bruising or gingival oozing during dental cleanings. Current medications include warfarin 5 mg nightly, metoprolol succinate 50 mg daily for rate control as well as portal pressure reduction, the aforementioned antihypertensives, lactulose 15 mL nightly for bowel regularity, vitamin D 1000 IU daily, and furosemide 20 mg each morning to prevent mild ankle swelling. He specifically avoids aspirin, clopidogrel, nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, herbal supplements, or over-the-counter preparations containing gingko, garlic, or ginseng. Socially he acknowledges enjoying two glasses of red wine with dinner on most weeknights and typically three pints of craft beer while watching weekend football, totaling approximately 15 alcoholic drinks per week, though he insists he never binges and has never had an alcohol-related hospitalization. He quit smoking cigarettes 25 years ago after a 30-pack-year exposure and has never used illicit substances. Family history is notable for hypertension and type 2 diabetes in both parents but lacks clotting or bleeding disorders. Review of systems today is negative for hematemesis, melena, abdominal tenderness, jaundice progression, mental status changes, headache, visual disturbances, chest pain, orthopnea, or exertional limitation beyond his usual baseline. On examination he appears thin but well nourished, body mass index 23 kg/m², afebrile, respirations 14/min, and oxygen saturation 98 % on ambient air. Sclerae are faintly icteric; there are scattered spider angiomata over the upper trunk, but no palmar erythema, digital clubbing, or caput medusae. Neck veins are not distended. Cardiac auscultation reveals varying S1 intensity without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Lungs are clear bilaterally. Abdomen is scaphoid and soft, with a firm, nodular liver edge palpable 3 cm below the right costal margin, no fluid wave, and no splenomegaly. Extremities show trace bilateral ankle pitting edema and no petechiae, ecchymoses, or purpura. Neurologic screening is non-focal. Point-of-care hemoglobin is 13.2 g/dL, platelet count 138 × 10⁹/L, and today’s international normalized ratio measured in clinic is 2.3. Electrocardiogram confirms atrial fibrillation with an average ventricular rate of 70/min, normal axis, and no acute ST-segment deviations. A transthoracic echocardiogram obtained six weeks ago documented left atrial enlargement, preserved left ventricular ejection fraction of 60 %, and no significant valvular disease. The patient seeks guidance on continuing anticoagulation in light of his liver condition, suboptimal systolic pressure control, and steady but appreciable alcohol intake, emphasizing that he wishes to minimize hospitalization risk as he prepares for extended travel abroad next spring.

output.reasoning: HAS-BLED components: Hypertension (1 pt), Abnormal renal/liver function (1-2 pts), Stroke (1 pt), Bleeding history/predisposition (1 pt), Labile INR (1 pt), Elderly (1 pt), Drugs/alcohol (1-2 pts). Hypertension: Present (168/92 mmHg, on lisinopril/chlorthalidone). Abnormal renal/liver: Liver present (cirrhosis, bilirubin 3.1 > 2x ULN). Stroke: Absent (denies history). Bleeding: Absent (denies history/predisposition). Labile INR: Absent (TTR 78%, stable). Elderly: Present (77 years old). Drugs/Alcohol: Alcohol present (15 drinks/week > 8 drinks/week).

args:
A 72-year-old male complained of chronic diarrhea and weight loss of 10 kg in 1 month. He took a colonoscopy examination at a private clinic showing multiple colonic polyps of varying sizes. He was referred to the Gastrointestinal Department of Presbyterian Medical Center.
There were no abnormalities in his past medical and family history such as gastrointestinal polyposis or colorectal cancer. He had been drinking about 1.5 bottles of Soju (Korean liquor) every day for 20 years.
He was showing chronic signs of illness, but his vital signs were relatively stable. He was 164.3 cm tall and weighed 47 kg (body mass index, 17.4). His physical examination revealed mild hair loss and hair pull test showed that more than 10 hairs were pulled out (). Black discolorations in both hands and feet were observed in limb examination (). Also, all fingernails of both hands were dry, cracked, and transformed ().
A peripheral blood test showed a hemoglobin level of 13.1 g/dL, hematocrit 38.2%, and evidence of megalocytes with mean corpuscular volume of 105.2 fL, mean corpuscular hemoglobin of 36.1 pg, and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration of 34.3%. The white blood cell count was 5,600/mm3 and platelet count was 209,000/mm3. Biochemical examinations showed aspartate aminotransferase of 35 IU/L, an alanine aminotransferase of 24 IU/L, total protein level of 5.5 g/dL, albumin level of 3.4 g/dL, total bilirubin level of 0.6 mg/dL, calcium level of 8.6 mg/dL, blood urea nitrogen (BUN) level of 10 mg/dL, creatinine level of 0.8 mg/dL, Na/K/Cl/Co2 of 139/4.3/104/27 mEq/L, and serum carcinoembryonic antigen level of 5.8 ng/mL.
The esophagus was normal in gastroscopy, but several hundred strawberry-like polyps of varying sizes (0.5 to 2.5 cm) were observed in the stomach (). The number and sizes of the polyps increased in the distal stomach than in the proximal stomach. They were diagnosed as hamartomatous polyps from the biopsy. Rapid urease test (Campylobacter-like organism, CLO test) showed positive result and the stool Helicobacter pylori Ag were also positive. The duodenum was covered with multiple small hyperemic polypoid lesions and numerous tiny polyps on whole small bowel were also observed by capsule endoscopy, but these were not confirmed histologically (). Thousands of little grape-like polyps that were less than 10 mm in size and some colon polyps of more than 1 cm in size were observed during colonoscopy. Small scale-like hyperemic mucosa under the size of 1 mm was observed without normal mucosa in the terminal ileum. These colon polyps were resected by endoscopic method, and the pathologic results confirmed colon cancer (adenocarcinoma in situ) () and serrated adenoma ().
Colonoscopic polypectomy was performed on multiple polyps that were larger than 1 cm. Most of them were inflammatory polyps containing lamina propria, and proliferated tortuous and cystic dilated glands were observed (). However, adenocarcinoma in situ with background low grade tubular adenomas was confirmed in one of them (). Also, another polyp showed serrated architecture and cytological dysplasia of the crypts, corresponding to serrated adenoma ().
The patient was given a nutritional therapy under the diagnosis of CCS. Prednisolone of 40 mg/day was administered, and the dosage was reduced by 5 mg every week. H. pylori eradication (proton pump inhibitor, amoxicillin, and metronidazole combination therapy for 2 weeks) was performed and the follow-up CLO test was negative.
At 4 months follow-up, although the patient appeared to have improved, hundreds of 0.5 to 2.5 cm-sized hyperplastic gastric polyps were not improved compared to the previous endoscopic findings. In the follow-up colonoscopy, numerous colon polyps were still discovered in the whole colon. Additional colon polypectomy was performed and the pathologic results confirmed inflammatory polyps and tubular adenoma. The patient is under observation in the outpatient clinic.

sodium: Serum sodium in mEq/L (e.g., 140)
chloride: Serum chloride in mEq/L (e.g., 100)
bicarbonate: Serum bicarbonate in mEq/L (e.g., 24)
albumin: Serum albumin in g/dL (e.g., 3.5)

args:
A 49-year-old man visited our hospital for polydipsia (8 to 9 L/day) and polyuria that started one month prior. He had no remarkable family history. Two years prior, the patient had experienced right chest pain and osteolytic lesions of the right 6th and 7th ribs were detected on X-ray. He was diagnosed with LCH after surgical excision of the rib mass in our hospital. At that time, subsequent bone scan and brain computed tomography (CT) revealed an osteolytic lesion in the occipital skull without involvement of brain parenchyma. The skull lesion was carefully followed up with skull X-ray, but the patient was lost to follow-up after 1 year.
On physical examination, the patient appeared well. Blood pressure was 130/90 mm Hg, pulse rate was 72 beats per minute, respiratory rate was 20 breathes per minute, and body temperature was 36.2℃. His height and weight were 164 cm and 70 kg, respectively. His tongue was not dry and skin turgor was normal without evidence of dehydration. Inspection and palpation of the chest revealed no masses, lung sounds were clear at auscultation, and no lymph nodes were palpable in the neck or limbs. Neurologic examination revealed normal motor and sensory functions, symmetric reflexes, and no evidence of clonus, fasciculations, or ataxia. The results of other physical examinations were unremarkable.
Blood cell count, urinalysis, serum chemistry, and electrolytes were within normal range. Basal anterior pituitary hormones were also normal. Serum osmolality at admission was 295 mOsm/kg and urine osmolality was 75 mOsm/kg. For differential diagnosis of polydipsia, the water deprivation test was performed. After 8 hours of water deprivation, serum osmolality reached 303 mOsm/kg and urine osmolality was stable with variation of less than 30 mOsm/kg, ranging from 99 to 118 mOsm/kg. We injected five units of vasopressin subcutaneously and urine osmolality was followed serially. After vasopressin injection, urine osmolality markedly increased to 562 mOsm/kg (more than 50% from baseline) after 1 hour and we reached a diagnosis of central DI ().
Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the pituitary gland revealed a solid mass involving the infundibulum (6 mm) and the posterior lobe of the pituitary gland with loss of bright spot on T1 weighted imaging (). We further performed the combined pituitary stimulation test and anterior pituitary function was normal.
Chest X-ray showed cavitary lesions in the right upper lobe and reticular opacities in right lower lobe (). CT of the chest with contrast enhancement demonstrated multiple patchy infiltrations in both lung fields with ground glass opacity and air bronchogram (). Chest CT also revealed new osteolytic bone lesions in the right 9th rib and left 10th rib, which were visualized as hot uptake on bone scan.
We concluded that LCH had spread to the pituitary gland, lung and bone and we planned treatment with inhaled desmopressin and systemic chemotherapy. The patient received intranasal desmopressin 10 µg twice daily and urine volume decreased to 1.5 to 2 L per day. He was treated with 6-week induction chemotherapy with prednisolone (40 mg/m2/day for 6 weeks) and vinblastine (weekly 6 mg/m2 intravenous bolus injection), followed by maintenance chemotherapy with 6-mercaptopurine (50 mg/m2 per day), vinblastine (6 mg/m2 intravenous bolus injection every 3 weeks), and prednisolone (40 mg/m2/day for first 5 days every 3 weeks) for 12 months.
Two months later, follow-up sella MRI demonstrated disappearance of the mass in the infundibulum of the pituitary gland (). During maintenance chemotherapy, chest CT with contrast enhancement revealed complete disappearance of the lung lesions 11 months after the start of chemotherapy (). Six years after chemotherapy, the patient has shown no evidence of recurrence at regular follow-up examinations and polyuria is well controlled with intranasal desmopressin.

args: A 70-year-old retired accountant, biologically male, was brought to the acute-care clinic by his spouse on the fifth morning of a gradually progressive respiratory illness characterized by a dry cough that had become productive of yellow sputum overnight, low-grade chills, pleuritic discomfort along the left posterior thorax, and new limitation of exertion such that he became winded after climbing one flight of stairs—an activity he normally tolerated despite chronic, medically managed cardiomyopathy. His medical background included long-standing hypertension, an ischemic cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction (last outpatient echocardiogram four months earlier showed an EF of 35 %), paroxysmal atrial fibrillation controlled on metoprolol, and a remote lacunar infarct eight years earlier that left no residual weakness but prompted daily clopidogrel therapy; his primary physician followed serum creatinine and cystatin C semi-annually, and both had remained within reference limits, with no prior diagnosis of intrinsic renal pathology. He had never been diagnosed with any malignancy and had no history of liver inflammation, alcohol-related disease, or viral hepatitis. The patient lives with his spouse in their own single-level home and remains fully independent in dressing, bathing, cooking, and driving; he explicitly denied any recent stays in skilled-nursing facilities, rehabilitation centers, or congregate living environments. Two weeks before presentation he completed his seasonal influenza vaccination and had not taken antibiotics during the current illness. On arrival he was seated upright on the examination table, conversing coherently and oriented to date, location, and situation without confusion, lethargy, or agitation. His initial vital signs recorded by nursing staff were: oral temperature 37.8 °C, pulse 108 beats/min with an irregularly irregular rhythm consistent with known atrial fibrillation, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min with mild accessory muscle use, and automated blood pressure 125/68 mm Hg; pulse oximetry on ambient air registered 92 %. Conjunctiva appeared mildly injected but not icteric, and oral mucosa was moist. Jugular venous pressure was not elevated above the clavicle at 45° positioning. Cardiac auscultation revealed the irregular cadence of atrial fibrillation, a soft S3 gallop similar to previous examinations, and no new murmurs. Pulmonary examination noted coarse crackles superimposed on diminished breath sounds throughout the left lower lobe without wheezes or egophony on the right, and percussion remained resonant bilaterally. There was no peripheral edema or calf tenderness. Neurologic screening showed full strength, symmetric reflexes, intact speech, and steady gait. Point-of-care finger-stick glucose measured 110 mg/dL before breakfast. Laboratory studies obtained within the first 20 minutes showed a hematocrit of 39 %, leukocyte count 11.3 × 10⁹/L with left shift, serum sodium 136 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, chloride 101 mEq/L, bicarbonate 23 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 22 mg/dL, and creatinine 1.0 mg/dL—consistent with his baseline. Serum troponin I was undetectable. An arterial blood gas drawn on room air revealed pH 7.33, PaCO₂ 40 mm Hg, and PaO₂ 75 mm Hg, producing an A–a gradient elevated for age. Portable postero-anterior and lateral chest radiographs demonstrated a dense left lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms and preservation of both costophrenic angles; no layering or meniscoid density suggested pleural fluid. Cardiothoracic ratio was unchanged from prior imaging, and pulmonary vasculature did not appear congested. Electrocardiogram confirmed chronic atrial fibrillation without new ST-T abnormalities. Prior to any pharmacologic therapy the patient had received only acetaminophen for skeletal discomfort earlier that morning and had not yet been given bronchodilators, diuretics, vasodilators, antibiotics, or supplemental oxygen beyond the arterial puncture. The clinical picture at presentation therefore reflects unaltered baseline physiology aside from the suspected infectious process involving the left lower lung field.

output.reasoning: Age: 70 years (+70 points). Sex: Male (+10 points). Nursing home resident: No (0 points). Comorbidities: Neoplastic disease: No (0). Liver disease: No (0). Congestive heart failure: Mentions "cardiomyopathy with reduced EF" and "S3 gallop", but the note explicitly states "no peripheral edema" and "jugular venous pressure not elevated". Per PSI guidelines, CHF requires physical findings or history; history of cardiomyopathy is noted, but current exam is compensated. I will count this as Absent (0) per strict interpretation of current findings vs chronic history. Cerebrovascular disease: History of lacunar infarct (+10 points). Renal disease: Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, no prior intrinsic renal pathology, BUN 22 mg/dL. These are within normal limits/baseline (0). Physical exam: Altered mental status: No (0). Respiratory rate >= 30: No (RR 20) (0). Systolic BP < 90: No (BP 125/68) (0). Temp < 35C or >= 40C: No (Temp 37.8) (0). Pulse >= 125: No (Pulse 108) (0). Labs/Imaging: pH < 7.35: Yes (pH 7.33) (+30 points). BUN >= 30 mg/dL: No (22) (0). Sodium < 130: No (136) (0). Glucose >= 250: No (110) (0). Hematocrit < 30%: No (39%) (0). PaO2 < 60 mm Hg: No (75) (0). Pleural effusion: No ("no layering or meniscoid density") (0).

- Conditions ABSENT: nursing_home_resident, neoplastic_disease, liver_disease, congestive_heart_failure, renal_disease, altered_mental_status, tachypnea, hypotension, hypothermia_or_hyperthermia, tachycardia, elevated_bun, hyponatremia, hyperglycemia, anemia, hypoxemia, pleural_effusion
