args: A 74-year-old man was evaluated this morning in the peri-operative medicine suite five days before a planned open transperitoneal repair of a 5.8-cm infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm discovered during surveillance ultrasonography for peripheral artery disease; the vascular team anticipates a long midline laparotomy with proximal and distal aortic clamping, approximately four hours of surgical time, blood loss in the 500–800 mL range, the need for large-bore venous access and arterial line placement, and immediate postoperative admission to the intensive-care unit for ventilatory weaning and close hemodynamic observation. He arrived driving himself, parked on the second deck of the visitor ramp, and walked the two city blocks to the clinic without stopping, noting only mild calf fatigue attributable to his known superficial femoral artery stenosis but no chest discomfort, light-headedness, or undue breathlessness. Around the home he mows a small yard with a self-propelled mower in two short sessions, carries a fifty-pound bag of water-softener salt from garage to basement once a month, and climbs a full flight of stairs to his bedroom at night without needing to pause; he sleeps flat, has never awakened gasping, and has not required pillows for dyspnea. There is no personal history of myocardial infarction, angina, coronary stenting, coronary artery bypass, or rhythm disturbance, and serial annual treadmill tests requested by his primary physician for fitness documentation have consistently reached eight to nine metabolic equivalents without electrocardiographic evidence of ischemia, the last such examination performed ten months ago. He denies stroke, transient focal neurologic episodes, carotid surgery, seizure, or memory lapses. Diabetes mellitus type 2 was diagnosed twenty-five years earlier; progressive fasting hyperglycemia despite maximal metformin and a sulfonylurea led to initiation of insulin glargine nine years ago and addition of rapid-acting insulin five years ago. His current regimen is glargine 34 units subcutaneously at 2100 h and insulin lispro before meals with a 1-unit-per-12-gram carbohydrate ratio and a correction factor of 1 unit for every 30 mg/dL above 140 mg/dL. Finger-stick logs reviewed today show pre-breakfast glucose values ranging 95–150 mg/dL over the past month with no readings below 70 mg/dL; the most recent hemoglobin A1c, obtained by his endocrinologist six weeks ago, was 7.7 %. Essential hypertension is treated with amlodipine 5 mg and losartan 50 mg each morning, producing home pressures averaging 126/68 mm Hg; hyperlipidemia is controlled with rosuvastatin 20 mg nightly, and low-dose aspirin 81 mg is taken for peripheral arterial disease prevention. Pulmonary history is limited to mild chronic bronchitis related to a 25-pack-year smoking exposure; he quit 18 years ago and currently uses an albuterol inhaler perhaps once a month during seasonal pollen surges. Surgical history includes appendectomy at age 17 and right rotator-cuff repair at 63, both without anesthesia-related difficulties. He drinks one can of beer with dinner on weekends and denies illicit drug use. Family history is notable for a father who died of a ruptured aneurysm at 79 and a mother who developed type 2 diabetes in her sixties; there are no premature coronary deaths. Review of systems today is negative for fever, night sweats, weight loss, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, orthopnea, ankle swelling, palpitations, claudication beyond the mild calf fatigue described, abdominal pain, melena, hematuria, or change in mental status. After five minutes seated, vital signs measured blood pressure 128/72 mm Hg in the right arm, regular pulse 70 beats/min, respiratory rate 14 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97 % breathing ambient air, oral temperature 36.6 °C, height 178 cm, weight 92 kg, and body-mass index 29.0 kg/m². He appeared comfortable, well nourished, and euvolemic. Jugular venous pulsations were not elevated, carotid upstrokes were brisk with no bruit, and the point of maximal impulse was palpated in the fifth intercostal space at the mid-clavicular line without displacement. Cardiac auscultation revealed a crisp S1 and S2 with no gallop, murmur, or rub. Lungs were clear to percussion and auscultation with good air entry bilaterally. The abdomen was soft with a palpable pulsatile mass just left of midline above the umbilicus, non-tender on gentle pressure, and without bruit; bowel sounds were normoactive. Peripheral pulses were 2+ at the radial arteries, diminished to 1+ at both dorsalis pedis arteries, and skin of the lower legs was warm without edema or trophic changes. Neurologic screening showed full orientation, intact cranial nerves, 5/5 strength throughout, fine sensation to light touch and vibration, and a steady narrow-based gait. Airway examination documented Mallampati class II, inter-incisor distance 4 cm, thyromental distance 6 cm, and full neck extension. Pre-operative laboratory studies drawn one hour before examination revealed hemoglobin 14.0 g/dL, hematocrit 42 %, white blood cell count 7.2 × 10⁹/L, platelet count 238 × 10⁹/L, sodium 137 mmol/L, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L, bicarbonate 24 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 28 mg/dL, and a serum creatinine of 1.76 mg/dL, corresponding to an estimated glomerular filtration rate of approximately 45 mL/min/1.73 m²; fasting plasma glucose obtained simultaneously measured 138 mg/dL, alanine aminotransferase 22 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 20 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 85 U/L, total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, and international normalized ratio 1.0. A twelve-lead electrocardiogram printed in clinic showed normal sinus rhythm at 68 beats/min, PR interval 160 ms, QRS duration 92 ms, axis 15°, and subtle flat inferolateral T waves judged nonspecific, unchanged from a tracing one year earlier. Upright postero-anterior chest radiograph displayed clear lung fields and a normal cardiomediastinal silhouette without calcification of the aneurysm wall. The anesthetic plan, reviewed with the patient, includes balanced general anesthesia with epidural catheter placement for postoperative analgesia, invasive arterial monitoring, large-bore central venous access, urine output measurement, permissive moderate hypotension during aortic clamping, judicious crystalloid and cell-saver autotransfusion guided by stroke-volume variation, continuation of antihypertensive and statin therapy on the morning of surgery, reduction of the bedtime basal insulin dose by 25 % the night before, withholding rapid-acting insulin after midnight pending point-of-care glucose checks, and sequential compression devices intra-operatively with subcutaneous unfractionated heparin beginning the evening of surgery for thrombo-prophylaxis. After a detailed discussion of bleeding, renal impairment, spinal cord ischemia, pulmonary complications, myocardial events, limb ischemia, wound infection, and the possibility of temporary dialysis should kidney function deteriorate further, the patient accurately repeated medication timing, fasting instructions, and signs of postoperative concern and expressed readiness to proceed with elective definitive aneurysm repair.

output.reasoning: The patient is 74 years old. The RCRI criteria include: 1. High-risk surgery (intraperitoneal, intrathoracic, or suprainguinal vascular procedures). The patient is undergoing open transperitoneal repair of an abdominal aortic aneurysm, which qualifies as high-risk surgery. 2. History of ischemic heart disease (history of MI, positive exercise test, current angina, etc.). The note explicitly states no history of MI, angina, coronary stenting, or CABG, and recent exercise tests were negative for ischemia. 3. History of heart failure. Note mentions he sleeps flat and denies PND/orthopnea; no history of heart failure. 4. History of cerebrovascular disease (stroke or TIA). Patient denies stroke or TIA. 5. Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus. Patient is on insulin glargine and lispro. 6. Preoperative serum creatinine > 2.0 mg/dL. Patient creatinine is 1.76 mg/dL, so this is absent. Present RCRI factors: High-risk surgery, Insulin-dependent diabetes.

high_risk_surgery: Intraperitoneal, intrathoracic, or suprainguinal vascular surgery
ischemic_heart_disease: History of MI, positive stress test, angina, nitrate use, Q waves
congestive_heart_failure: History of CHF, pulmonary edema, PND, bilateral rales, S3, CXR with pulmonary edema
cerebrovascular_disease: History of stroke or TIA
insulin_diabetes: Insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus
creatinine_elevated: Preoperative creatinine > 2.0 mg/dL

MEDICAL ANALYSIS (from reasoning stage):
- Conditions PRESENT: high_risk_surgery, insulin_dependent_diabetes
- Conditions ABSENT: history_ischemic_heart_disease, history_heart_failure, history_cerebrovascular_disease, preoperative_serum_creatinine_gt_2mg_dL
- Demographics: Age=74, Sex=male
What is the patient's Charlson Comorbidity Index (CCI)? You should use the patient's medical values and health status when they were first admitted to the hospital prior to any treatment.

```python
def identify_calculator(question: str, available_calculators: list[str]) -> dict:
    """Identify which medical calculator is needed based on the question.

    Analyze the question and match it to one of the available calculators.

    Return a dict with:
    - "calculator_name": exact name from the available list (CRITICAL: Copy the name exactly character-for-character)

    - "reasoning": brief explanation

    Examples:
    >>> identify_calculator("What is the patient's BMI?", ["Body Mass Index (BMI)", "Ideal Body Weight (Devine)"])
```

args: A 97-year-old retired civil engineer who spent five decades inspecting suspension bridges was brought to the outpatient perioperative medicine office this morning by his granddaughter after a urology team requested a comprehensive evaluation before scheduling image-guided brachytherapy for a recently discovered, organ-confined neoplasm of the prostate (transrectal biopsy three weeks earlier showed a single focus of acinar adenocarcinoma, Gleason score 3 + 4 = 7, magnetic-resonance imaging demonstrates capsular integrity and no nodal enlargement). The patient walked in with a polished mahogany cane he fashioned himself, greeting staff by name and accurately recalling the height of the center span of the last bridge he certified, testimony to an intact memory and sharp executive function; during history taking he recited his medication list without hesitation and named the current head of state, the clinic address, and today’s date. Musculoskeletal history is notable for long-standing seropositive rheumatoid arthritis diagnosed in his late sixties after an episode of painful morning stiffness in the metacarpophalangeal joints lasting more than an hour; he has taken methotrexate 10 mg weekly with folic acid for the past two decades, supplemented by low-dose prednisone bursts during winter flares, and follows regularly with rheumatology where the most recent Disease Activity Score was in the low range. Despite occasional ulnar deviation at the hands, he buttoned his shirt unassisted this morning and continues to spend afternoons carving chess pieces, describing only mild aching controlled by acetaminophen 500 mg as needed. Gastrointestinal review reveals a cratered duodenal lesion noted on esophagogastroduodenoscopy twelve years ago after dark, tarry stools; Helicobacter testing was positive and triple therapy administered, followed by eight weeks of pantoprazole until complete mucosal healing was documented. Since that episode he avoids non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, takes omeprazole 20 mg each dawn, and denies subsequent hematemesis, melena, coffee-ground emesis, or iron-deficiency anemia. He has never experienced jaundice, encephalopathy, fluid retention, or evidence of portal hypertension, and a right-upper-quadrant sonogram performed last month to evaluate biliary colic showed a smooth hepatic contour, patent hepatic and portal veins, and no ascites. Endocrine screening one week ago revealed fasting plasma glucose 96 mg/dL and hemoglobin A1c 5.6 %, values that have remained stable for years without pharmacologic intervention; no clinician has labeled him diabetic, and he follows a breakfast routine of oatmeal and fruit and records his weekly weights on graph paper taped near the pantry. The patient firmly denies ever feeling crushing or squeezing substernal discomfort, radiation to jaw or arm, diaphoresis disproportionate to exertion, or syncope; he has never been hospitalized for myocardial infarction, and no clinician has informed him of coronary artery occlusion. He climbs the eight steps to his front porch carrying the morning newspaper without dyspnea and sleeps flat on a single pillow; he has also never received a diagnosis of congestive heart failure. Cardiologist notes from his annual wellness visit six months ago document a normal stress echocardiogram reaching 8 METS with no ischemic changes, preserved global systolic performance, and trivial aortic sclerosis appropriate for age. He similarly reports no episodes of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, ankle swelling, orthopnea, or unexplained weight gain; his daughter confirms that socks do not leave indents around the calves at bedtime. Vascular review is silent for calf cramping, hip or buttock pain on walking, cold extremities, or prior vascular interventions, and he has never been told he has peripheral arterial disease; pulses were graded 2 +/4 at all peripheral sites during today’s examination. Neurologic inquiry reveals no sudden unilateral weakness, slurred speech, facial droop, blurred vision, gait imbalance, seizure activity, or sensory loss, and he continues to beat his grandson at rapid-fire chess without oversight. He reports no prior cerebrovascular accident or transient ischemic attack and has never experienced paralysis or hemiplegia. Respiratory history is remarkable only for childhood pertussis; he never smoked, has no chronic cough or wheeze, has never carried a diagnosis of asthma, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, or other chronic lung disorder, and lung fields were clear at the last chest radiograph obtained during prostate work-up. His medication list includes methotrexate and folic acid as noted, a daily 81-mg aspirin recommended by his primary practitioner for vascular prophylaxis, omeprazole nightly, acetaminophen as needed, and a multivitamin; he reports hives after a sulfonamide injection in the 1940s but no other drug allergies. He drinks a small glass of red wine with Sunday dinner, abstains from liquor, has never used illicit substances, and lives independently in a single-story cottage equipped with grab bars he installed himself. Family pedigree shows longevity—both parents surpassed ninety—with no early coronary events, cerebrovascular accidents, or cirrhotic complications. Vital signs recorded after five minutes of seated rest read temperature 36.7 °C, blood pressure 128/72 mm Hg in the right arm, heart rate 68 beats/min regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 97 % on room air; body mass index is 23 kg/m². Physical examination reveals an alert, pleasant gentleman sitting upright without distress; sclerae anicteric, oral mucosa moist. Carotid upstrokes are brisk without bruit, jugular venous pulsation rises 3 cm above the sternal angle at 45°, the apical impulse is nondisplaced in the fifth intercostal space mid-clavicular line, first and second heart sounds are crisp with a soft midsystolic murmur heard best at the right upper sternal border, no gallops, rubs, or peripheral edema. Lungs are resonant to percussion, breath sounds vesicular throughout, no wheezes or crackles. Abdomen is flat, bowel sounds normoactive, liver edge smooth and palpable 1 cm below the costal margin, no splenic tip, no tenderness, masses, or fluid wave; digital rectal examination deferred to urology. Extremities are warm with capillary refill under two seconds, radial, femoral, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial pulses symmetrical; nail beds show no clubbing. Hands reveal mild ulnar deviation and Swan-neck deformities but no active synovitis. Neurologic testing finds orientation to person, place, time, and situation intact, clear and fluent speech, cranial nerves II–XII grossly normal, symmetric strength 5/5 in all extremities, deep tendon reflexes 2 +/4, intact light-touch and vibration sense to the great toes, and a steady tandem gait executed without aid. Recent laboratory data reviewed today: sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (estimated glomerular filtration rate 74 mL/min/1.73 m²), alanine aminotransferase 28 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 30 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, albumin 4.2 g/dL, international normalized ratio 1.0, hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, mean corpuscular volume 88 fL, white blood cell count 5.8 × 10⁹/L with a normal differential, and platelets 210 × 10⁹/L. Complete blood counts over the years have shown no leukocytosis, blasts, or cytopenias, and he has never been diagnosed with leukemia or other hematologic malignancies. He tested negative for human immunodeficiency virus during routine blood donor screening in his eighth decade and has had no exposures or illnesses suggestive of immunodeficiency. Electrocardiogram obtained in clinic shows normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats/min, PR interval 160 ms, QRS duration 90 ms, axis 20°, no pathologic Q waves, and nonspecific ST-T changes unchanged from prior tracings. He seeks counsel on peri-procedural management of methotrexate, gastric acid suppression around planned radiation, and confirmation that his cardiovascular and pulmonary status present no contraindication to the proposed curative therapy.

output.reasoning: Age: 97 years old (Age > 80 = 4 points). Neoplasm: History of prostate adenocarcinoma (localized) = 2 points. Rheumatic disease: Documented history of long-standing seropositive rheumatoid arthritis = 1 point. Peptic ulcer disease: History of duodenal lesion (healed) = 1 point. Diabetes: HbA1c 5.6% and fasting glucose 96 mg/dL; no diagnosis of diabetes = Absent. CHF: No history, normal echo, no symptoms = Absent. Myocardial infarction: No history, no symptoms = Absent. Peripheral vascular disease: No history, normal pulses = Absent. Cerebrovascular disease: No history, no symptoms = Absent. Liver disease: No history, normal labs (ALT/AST/Bilirubin), no evidence of cirrhosis or portal hypertension = Absent. Renal disease: Creatinine 0.9 mg/dL, GFR 74 mL/min = Absent (Normal renal function). Hemiplegia/Paralysis: Absent. Dementia: Cognitive function described as sharp, intact memory, executes complex tasks (carving, chess) = Absent. Leukemia/Lymphoma/Multiple Myeloma: No evidence, explicitly denied = Absent. AIDS/HIV: Negative test/denied = Absent.

Hands reveal mild ulnar deviation and Swan-neck deformities but no active synovitis. Neurologic testing finds orientation to person, place, time, and situation intact, clear and fluent speech, cranial nerves II–XII grossly normal, symmetric strength 5/5 in all extremities, deep tendon reflexes 2 +/4, intact light-touch and vibration sense to the great toes, and a steady tandem gait executed without aid. Recent laboratory data reviewed today: sodium 139 mmol/L, potassium 4.1 mmol/L, chloride 103 mmol/L, bicarbonate 25 mmol/L, blood urea nitrogen 18 mg/dL, creatinine 0.9 mg/dL (estimated glomerular filtration rate 74 mL/min/1.73 m²), alanine aminotransferase 28 U/L, aspartate aminotransferase 30 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L, total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, albumin 4.2 g/dL, international normalized ratio 1.0, hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, hematocrit 40 %, mean corpuscular volume 88 fL, white blood cell count 5.8 × 10⁹/L with a normal differential, and platelets 210 × 10⁹/L. Complete blood counts over the years have shown no leukocytosis, blasts, or cytopenias, and he has never been diagnosed with leukemia or other hematologic malignancies. He tested negative for human immunodeficiency virus during routine blood donor screening in his eighth decade and has had no exposures or illnesses suggestive of immunodeficiency. Electrocardiogram obtained in clinic shows normal sinus rhythm at 70 beats/min, PR interval 160 ms, QRS duration 90 ms, axis 20°, no pathologic Q waves, and nonspecific ST-T changes unchanged from prior tracings. He seeks counsel on peri-procedural management of methotrexate, gastric acid suppression around planned radiation, and confirmation that his cardiovascular and pulmonary status present no contraindication to the proposed curative therapy.

A 3-year-old boy weighing 12 kg was diagnosed as congenital complete heart block (CHB) with a moderate PDA. The clinical evaluation showed heart rate of 70 bpm and continuous murmur on auscultation. The electrocardiogram (ECG) showed narrow QRS CHB. The TTE showed a 3 mm PDA with continuous left to right shunt. The aortogram in lateral view showed a 2.8 mm type ‘A’ PDA with good ampulla. The PA pressures were normal. A 6F delivery sheath was placed in the descending aorta and a 6/4 Amplatzer duct occluder (ADO) was used for the closure. The device was positioned in the ampulla and planned to release in right anterior oblique view []. The ADO was embolized into descending aorta instantaneously. Careful subsequent evaluation revealed improperly positioned device as there was gap between the aortic retention disc and ampulla.
The device retention screw in the descending aorta was facing cranially and felt unfavorable for the retrieval. And hence, a pigtail catheter was used from the pulmonary artery through PDA into descending aorta to manipulate the device keeping an 8 mm inflated Tyshak balloon (NuMED Canada Inc. Cornwall, ON, Canada) below the device to avoid further dislodgment. The device was manipulated to direct the retention screw to face caudally. A 7F long primary sheath was exchanged in the femoral artery and gradually advanced close to the retention screw using anteroposterior and lateral fluoroscopy views. Now a 6F secondary sheath was taken into the previously placed sheath and advanced gradually into the retention screw. A 3F delivery cable was advanced in the sheath and re-screwed the device gradually by clock-wise rotation like in the case 1. Now the same retrieved device was taken and repositioned properly with a constant pull into the PA. The angiogram in lateral view showed well-positioned device []. The follow-up assessment showed no residual shunt or malposition of the device.

args:
A 65-year-old female with history of chronic alcohol use was hospitalized for sudden onset of hematemesis. She also reported few days of nausea and vomiting associated with worsening ascites. She denied any significant past medical history except for a ruptured ovarian cyst at age of 30 years that required emergent surgery. Patient had a long history of alcohol use (two glasses of vodka daily) for over 10 years. Physical exam showed scleral icterus, there was mild abdominal distention with epigastric tenderness, no hepatosplenomegaly was noted. No signs of encephalopathy or asterixis were seen. Blood test showed hemoglobin 10.4 × 10 g/dL, white blood cell count of 7.7 × 103 cells/µL and platelet count of 190 × 103 cells/µL. Her prothrombin time and international normalized ratio (INR) were elevated at 1.4 and 14.3 s, respectively. Liver function test showed aspartate transaminase 140 U/L, alanine transaminase 29 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 332 U/L, total bilirubin 9.6 mg/dL (direct 7.4 mg/dL, indirect 2.2 mg/dL). Few hours after admission, she developed an episode of hematemesis with drop in hemoglobin to 7.7 g/dL. Emergent endoscopy showed a non-bleeding 2 cm prepyloric gastric antral ulcer (). Biopsy of the ulcer base was negative for malignancy or H. pylori.
Follow-up endoscopy at an interval of 6 weeks showed a healing prepyloric antral ulcer with mild portal hypertensive gastropathy (). Patient had a repeat endoscopy at 4 months for persistent nausea and vomiting which again showed a healing antral ulcer. She continued to drink alcohol and was intermittently followed by her the primary care physician. She also reported daily NSAID use for abdominal pain and nausea. She had multiple emergency department (ED) visits for the same complaints and underwent repeat endoscopy which showed a duodenal ulcer (). She was managed with pantoprazole twice daily and was advised to avoid NSAIDs and alcohol. She also underwent extensive workup for recurrent nausea and vomiting which were all unremarkable. Systemic disorders including but not limited to neurological cause, adrenal, parathyroid and diabetes were also ruled out.
She again presented to the ED for worsening nausea and vomiting associated with upper abdominal pain. Endoscopy, now 7 years from the discovery of the initial lesion, showed a GDF. The prepyloric antral ulcer had fistulized into the duodenal bulb creating the appearance of a double pylorus (). Biopsy of the ulcer base was again negative for malignancy or H. pylori. H. pylori stool antigen and serum H. pylori antibody test were also negative. She was discharged on oral proton pump inhibitor and has been followed up with gastroenterology for management of Laennec’s cirrhosis. With more frequent outpatient follow up, alcohol abstinence and use of acid-suppression medication, she had gradual improvement in her symptoms in about 1 - 2 months.

age: Patient age in years (e.g., 55)
ast: AST in U/L (e.g., 45)
alt: ALT in U/L (e.g., 50)
platelets: Platelet count in 10^9/L (e.g., 150)

args:
This is a 47-year-old Caucasian male with a history significant for a living unrelated renal transplant two years prior to presentation and recent acute cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection. He presented with a two-day history of worsening, ascending lower extremity weakness, numbness, and tingling. The weakness had worsened rapidly to the point where he had difficulty ambulating and imbalance. Seven days prior to presentation, he had first presented with fever, chills, myalgias, and headache of one-week duration. Work-up at that time revealed acute CMV infection and he was started on oral valganciclovir at treatment doses. On review of systems, the patient complained of mild shortness of breath that worsened on exertion. He denied fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, lightheadedness, cough, lower extremity edema, or any changes in his urine output. He denied any recent travel, sick contacts, recent history of insect bites, or vaccinations.
His past medical history was significant for hypertension, dyslipidemia, asthma, and a distant history of melanoma. The etiology of his end stage kidney disease was biopsy proven focal segmental glomerulosclerosis presumed to be secondary to anabolic steroid use. He had a living unrelated kidney transplant two years prior to presentation. Induction was with steroids and basiliximab. His posttransplant course was complicated by biopsy proven BK Virus Nephropathy one year after transplant for which he was successfully treated with reduction in immunosuppression and intravenous immunoglobulin. At the time of transplantation, the donor was CMV positive, and the recipient was CMV negative. He received prophylaxis with valganciclovir for CMV more than one year. His current immunosuppression regimen consisted of prednisone 5 mg daily, tacrolimus 1.5 mg every twelve hours, and mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg every twelve hours.
On physical examination, the patient appeared anxious but was awake, alert, and oriented to person, place, and time. Vital signs included a blood pressure of 137/98 mmHg, pulse rate of 81/min, temperature of 97 degrees Fahrenheit, respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min, and a pulse oximetry of one hundred percent on room air. Physical examination was significant for a neurological examination, which demonstrated decreased motor strength in proximal and distal muscles in both upper and lower limbs (grades 3/5 and 2/5, resp.). He had absent deep tendon reflexes in his lower extremities. His gait could not be assessed due to lower extremity weakness and inability to ambulate. Two days before, his neurological exam was noncontributory except for decreased temperature sensation in his hands and feet.
Laboratory data from one week prior to presentation included mild thrombocytopenia, mild increase in liver transaminases, and a stable creatinine. Diagnosis of acute CMV infection was made based on a positive serum CMV IgM titer of 149 au/ml (normal < 30 au/ml), negative CMV IgG, and CMV PCR with 4800 copies/ml. Lumbar puncture and other serologic work-ups were negative. MRI of the brain done revealed no acute changes. Due to a diagnosis of acute CMV infection, he was started on oral valganciclovir. Laboratory data at this presentation included white blood cell count 5.9 k/microliter; hemoglobin 16.8 grams/deciliter; platelets 312 k/microliter; sodium 139 mmol/liter; potassium 3.6 mmol/liter; chloride 105 mmol/liter; bicarbonate 23 mmol/liter; blood urea nitrogen 17 mg/dl; and creatinine 1.28 mg/dl (at baseline). Urine heavy metal screen was negative for arsenic, cadmium, mercury, or lead. HIV, HTLV I/II, HBV, HCV, HSV, EBV, and HHV-6 PCR analyses were all negative. Lumbar puncture showed cerebrospinal fluid protein at 63 mg/dL, glucose of 68 mg/dL, cell count of 1, and albumin of 52.7 mg/dL. CMV PCR was positive in the CSF. Serum CMV PCR was 4000 copies/mL.
Based on positive serum and cerebrospinal fluid CMV PCR, the typical albuminocytologic dissociation of CSF analysis, and progressive neurological manifestations, a diagnosis of CMV associated Guillain-Barré Syndrome was made.
The patient was on an immunosuppression regimen that consisted of prednisone 5 mg daily, tacrolimus 1.5 mg every twelve hours, and mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg every twelve hours. The mycophenolate mofetil was discontinued, and the Prograf dosage was reduced to target FK506 troughs of 5–7. In addition to reduction in immunosuppression, the patient received eleven treatments of plasma exchange over a course of two weeks (each exchange consisted of two plasma volumes each with albumin as the replacement) and a total of 1 g/kg body weight of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) in two divided doses. He was continued on valganciclovir 900 mg orally two times a day. After 72 hours, the patient began to improve with increased motor strength and improved negative inspiratory flow and vital capacity measurements. Every successive plasma exchange treatment seemed to subjectively show a favorable response to his motor recovery and muscle strength. After a two-week hospitalization, the patient regained his ability to ambulate and he was subsequently discharged on oral valganciclovir. He was continued on treatment doses of valganciclovir at 900 mg orally two times a day as an outpatient. His serum CMV PCR was negative after two weeks of treatment. One month later, the patient returned with lower extremity weakness and was readmitted for a presumed relapse. At this time, his CMV PCR was still negative. The patient was then treated with a total of 7 plasma exchanges. He regained his motor strength once again after initiation of plasma exchange and subjectively reported progressive improvement with each treatment. The patient had no adverse reactions to the IVIG or to the plasmapheresis. He has been in remission after treatment. His renal function has remained stable through all of this.

args:
This is a 47-year-old Caucasian male with a history significant for a living unrelated renal transplant two years prior to presentation and recent acute cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection. He presented with a two-day history of worsening, ascending lower extremity weakness, numbness, and tingling. The weakness had worsened rapidly to the point where he had difficulty ambulating and imbalance. Seven days prior to presentation, he had first presented with fever, chills, myalgias, and headache of one-week duration. Work-up at that time revealed acute CMV infection and he was started on oral valganciclovir at treatment doses. On review of systems, the patient complained of mild shortness of breath that worsened on exertion. He denied fever, chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, lightheadedness, cough, lower extremity edema, or any changes in his urine output. He denied any recent travel, sick contacts, recent history of insect bites, or vaccinations.
His past medical history was significant for hypertension, dyslipidemia, asthma, and a distant history of melanoma. The etiology of his end stage kidney disease was biopsy proven focal segmental glomerulosclerosis presumed to be secondary to anabolic steroid use. He had a living unrelated kidney transplant two years prior to presentation. Induction was with steroids and basiliximab. His posttransplant course was complicated by biopsy proven BK Virus Nephropathy one year after transplant for which he was successfully treated with reduction in immunosuppression and intravenous immunoglobulin. At the time of transplantation, the donor was CMV positive, and the recipient was CMV negative. He received prophylaxis with valganciclovir for CMV more than one year. His current immunosuppression regimen consisted of prednisone 5 mg daily, tacrolimus 1.5 mg every twelve hours, and mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg every twelve hours.
On physical examination, the patient appeared anxious but was awake, alert, and oriented to person, place, and time. Vital signs included a blood pressure of 137/98 mmHg, pulse rate of 81/min, temperature of 97 degrees Fahrenheit, respiratory rate of 16 breaths/min, and a pulse oximetry of one hundred percent on room air. Physical examination was significant for a neurological examination, which demonstrated decreased motor strength in proximal and distal muscles in both upper and lower limbs (grades 3/5 and 2/5, resp.). He had absent deep tendon reflexes in his lower extremities. His gait could not be assessed due to lower extremity weakness and inability to ambulate. Two days before, his neurological exam was noncontributory except for decreased temperature sensation in his hands and feet.
Laboratory data from one week prior to presentation included mild thrombocytopenia, mild increase in liver transaminases, and a stable creatinine. Diagnosis of acute CMV infection was made based on a positive serum CMV IgM titer of 149 au/ml (normal < 30 au/ml), negative CMV IgG, and CMV PCR with 4800 copies/ml. Lumbar puncture and other serologic work-ups were negative. MRI of the brain done revealed no acute changes. Due to a diagnosis of acute CMV infection, he was started on oral valganciclovir. Laboratory data at this presentation included white blood cell count 5.9 k/microliter; hemoglobin 16.8 grams/deciliter; platelets 312 k/microliter; sodium 139 mmol/liter; potassium 3.6 mmol/liter; chloride 105 mmol/liter; bicarbonate 23 mmol/liter; blood urea nitrogen 17 mg/dl; and creatinine 1.28 mg/dl (at baseline). Urine heavy metal screen was negative for arsenic, cadmium, mercury, or lead. HIV, HTLV I/II, HBV, HCV, HSV, EBV, and HHV-6 PCR analyses were all negative. Lumbar puncture showed cerebrospinal fluid protein at 63 mg/dL, glucose of 68 mg/dL, cell count of 1, and albumin of 52.7 mg/dL. CMV PCR was positive in the CSF. Serum CMV PCR was 4000 copies/mL.
Based on positive serum and cerebrospinal fluid CMV PCR, the typical albuminocytologic dissociation of CSF analysis, and progressive neurological manifestations, a diagnosis of CMV associated Guillain-Barré Syndrome was made.
The patient was on an immunosuppression regimen that consisted of prednisone 5 mg daily, tacrolimus 1.5 mg every twelve hours, and mycophenolate mofetil 500 mg every twelve hours. The mycophenolate mofetil was discontinued, and the Prograf dosage was reduced to target FK506 troughs of 5–7. In addition to reduction in immunosuppression, the patient received eleven treatments of plasma exchange over a course of two weeks (each exchange consisted of two plasma volumes each with albumin as the replacement) and a total of 1 g/kg body weight of intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) in two divided doses. He was continued on valganciclovir 900 mg orally two times a day. After 72 hours, the patient began to improve with increased motor strength and improved negative inspiratory flow and vital capacity measurements. Every successive plasma exchange treatment seemed to subjectively show a favorable response to his motor recovery and muscle strength. After a two-week hospitalization, the patient regained his ability to ambulate and he was subsequently discharged on oral valganciclovir. He was continued on treatment doses of valganciclovir at 900 mg orally two times a day as an outpatient. His serum CMV PCR was negative after two weeks of treatment. One month later, the patient returned with lower extremity weakness and was readmitted for a presumed relapse. At this time, his CMV PCR was still negative. The patient was then treated with a total of 7 plasma exchanges. He regained his motor strength once again after initiation of plasma exchange and subjectively reported progressive improvement with each treatment. The patient had no adverse reactions to the IVIG or to the plasmapheresis. He has been in remission after treatment. His renal function has remained stable through all of this.
